> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classification of Irrigation Methods (KTU 2024 Hierarchy)

Irrigation is the artificial application of water to soil/crop to supplement rainfall for agricultural production. The **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET632)** classifies irrigation methods into the following broad categories:

| Sl. No. | Broad Category | Sub-Types | Energy Source |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Surface Irrigation** | Border, Furrow, Basin, Wild Flooding | Gravity |
| 2 | **Sprinkler Irrigation** | Rotary, Perforated Pipe, Boom | Pressurized |
| 3 | **Drip / Micro Irrigation** | Surface drip, Sub-surface drip | Pressurized |
| 4 | **Sub-surface Irrigation** | Open Joints, Mole drains | Capillary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Irrigation** is the **oldest and most widely used** method in the world, accounting for approximately **85%** of the total irrigated area globally. In Kerala, surface methods dominate due to gentle terrain, abundant rainfall, and small landholdings.

## 1.2 Surface Irrigation — Master Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface Irrigation** is defined as the controlled application of water by gravity flow over the soil surface to infiltrate into the root zone, where the **advance, recession, and infiltration phases** are governed by the field topography, soil texture, and inflow discharge.

**Three fundamental phases** govern all surface irrigation events:
1. **Advance Phase** — Water moves from the head to the tail of the field.
2. **Storage (Ponding) Phase** — Water remains on the surface and infiltrates.
3. **Recession Phase** — Water front retreats from the tail back towards the head.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bath-Tub" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine pouring water from a bucket onto a long rectangular concrete floor tilted slightly downward. The water rushes forward (advance), spreads sideways, and simultaneously soaks downward. The leading edge of the puddle is the *advancing front*; the trailing edge is the *receding front*. The *ideal* scenario is when water covers the entire field uniformly, soaks exactly the required depth, and drains off without runoff or deep percolation loss.

## 1.4 Three Principal Surface Irrigation Methods

### A. Border Irrigation
A method where the field is divided into **parallel strips (borders)** bounded by low dikes/levees, and water is released at the upper end, flowing as a **sheet of water** down the slope.

### B. Furrow Irrigation
A method where water is conveyed in **small parallel channels (furrows)** between crop rows, infiltrating **laterally and vertically** to wet the root zone. Suitable for **row crops** like maize, sugarcane, cotton, potato.

### C. Basin Irrigation
A method where the field is divided into **small, level plots (basins)** surrounded by bunds on all four sides. Water is allowed to flood each basin. Used for **paddy (rice), orchards, and trees**.

## 1.5 Ideal Wetting Pattern — Universal Principle

For **all three** surface methods, the **ideal wetting pattern** requires:
- **Uniform depth** of water application across the field.
- **No runoff (tailwater loss)** at the downstream end.
- **No deep percolation** below the root zone.
- **Adequate time** for water to infiltrate to the depth of root zone.

The **time ratio concept** by **Hart et al. (1968)** states that for an *ideal* irrigation:
$$T_{\text{advance}} = T_{\text{opportunity}} = T_{\text{infiltration\,required}}$$

This implies: water should reach the tail of the field **exactly when** the required depth has infiltrated at the head — no excess, no deficit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Performance Evaluation Indices of Surface Irrigation

These indices are essential for KTU numerical problems and must be memorized with units.

### 2.1.1 Application Efficiency ($E_a$)
$$E_a = \frac{V_{\text{stored}}}{V_{\text{applied}}} \times 100\,\%$$
- $V_{\text{stored}}$ = Volume of water retained in the root zone (beneficial use).
- $V_{\text{applied}}$ = Total volume of water delivered to the field.
- **Ideal value** = 100%. KTU benchmark: 60–70% for well-designed systems.

### 2.1.2 Storage Efficiency ($E_s$)
$$E_s = \frac{V_{\text{stored}}}{V_{\text{required}}} \times 100\,\%$$
- Indicates how much of the **needed water** was actually stored. Useful for under-irrigation analysis.

### 2.1.3 Distribution Uniformity ($DU$)

$$DU = \frac{\text{Average depth infiltrated in lower quarter}}{\text{Average depth infiltrated over field}} \times 100\,\%$$

Lower quarter denotes the **driest 25%** of the field.

### 2.1.4 Christiansen's Coefficient of Uniformity ($CU$)

$$CU = 100 \times \left[1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert Y_i - \bar{Y} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{Y}} \right]$$

- $Y_i$ = depth infiltrated at $i^{th}$ observation point.
- $\bar{Y}$ = mean depth of infiltration.
- $n$ = number of observation points.
- $CU \geq 85\%$ is considered **excellent** for surface irrigation.

### 2.1.5 Deep Percolation Ratio ($DPR$)
$$DPR = \frac{V_{\text{deep percolation}}}{V_{\text{applied}}} \times 100\,\%$$

### 2.1.6 Tailwater Ratio ($TWR$)
$$TWR = \frac{V_{\text{runoff}}}{V_{\text{applied}}} \times 100\,\%$$

### 2.1.7 Consumptive Use Efficiency ($E_{cu}$)
$$E_{cu} = \frac{V_{\text{consumptively\,used}}}{V_{\text{stored}}} \times 100\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **ideal surface irrigation event**: $E_a + DPR + TWR = 100\%$. This is the **mass balance identity** most frequently tested in KTU exams.

## 2.2 BORDER IRRIGATION — Detailed Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Geometry of a Border
A border strip is bounded by two **parallel low dikes (levees)** and is sloped in the direction of irrigation. The **upper end** receives water through a head ditch/gate.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Width | $W$ | 3 to 15 m (commonly 5–10 m) |
| Length | $L$ | 60 to 400 m (commonly 100–200 m) |
| Longitudinal slope | $S$ | 0.05% to 2% |
| Unit discharge | $q$ | 2 to 5 L/s per metre width |
| Levee height | $h_L$ | 0.15 to 0.30 m |
| Cut-off time | $T_{co}$ | Time when inflow is stopped |

### 2.2.2 Design Equations for Border

**Infiltration depth using Kostiakov's equation:**

$$Z = k \cdot t^{a}$$

- $Z$ = cumulative infiltration depth (cm).
- $t$ = infiltration time (min).
- $k$ = empirical constant (cm/min$^a$).
- $a$ = empirical exponent (0 < a < 1, typically 0.3 to 0.8).

**Required depth of irrigation (net):**
$$d_n = \frac{(FC - WP)}{100} \cdot \gamma_d \cdot D_{rz}$$

- $FC$ = Field Capacity (%), $WP$ = Wilting Point (%), $\gamma_d$ = dry unit weight, $D_{rz}$ = root zone depth.

**Gross depth:**
$$d_g = \frac{d_n}{E_a}$$

**Volume of water to be applied per border:**
$$V = d_g \times W \times L$$

**Required time of ponding for infiltration:**
$$t_{\text{req}} = \left(\frac{d_g}{k}\right)^{1/a}$$

### 2.2.3 Advance and Recession Concepts
The **advance time** $T_L$ is the time for water to reach the lower end. From the **Lewis-Milne equation** (volume balance):

$$Q \cdot T_L = 0.77 \cdot \sigma_z \cdot A_0 \cdot T_L^{0.5} \cdot L + V_{\text{req}}$$

A simplified design relation widely used in KTU problems:

$$T_L = \frac{V_{\text{req}}}{Q \cdot (1 - 0.5 r)}$$

where $r$ is the surface storage fraction (typically 0.05–0.10).

### 2.2.4 Evaluation of Border Irrigation
Evaluation is performed by measuring:
- $T_L$ (advance time)
- Recession time at head ($T_{rh}$), at tail ($T_{rt}$)
- $T_{op}$ = opportunity time at any point = recession time – advance time.

$$T_{op,\,x} = T_{r,\,x} - T_{a,\,x}$$

**Lateral spread check:**
$$q_{\text{recommended}} = 0.006 \, W \, S^{0.5}$$

(Merriam's empirical design rule, widely cited in KTU references)

### 2.2.5 Ideal Wetting Pattern — Border

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal border wetting pattern** = uniform sheet of water covering the entire border with constant depth infiltration. The wetted front moves as a *plug flow* such that $T_L = t_{\text{req}}$ (advance time equals the time needed to infiltrate required depth at the head).

**Design criterion (advance–opportunity equality):**
$$T_L = T_{op,\text{head}} = T_{op,\text{tail}}$$

## 2.3 FURROW IRRIGATION — Detailed Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Geometry of a Furrow

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Furrow length | $L_f$ | 50 to 400 m |
| Furrow spacing | $W_f$ | 0.5 to 1.5 m (crop-dependent) |
| Furrow slope | $S_f$ | 0.1% to 1% |
| Furrow cross-section | Trapezoidal | Top width 20–30 cm, depth 15–25 cm |
| Furrow discharge | $q_f$ | 0.5 to 2.5 L/s |
| Furrow shape factor | $\rho_1$, $\rho_2$ | Wetted perimeter parameters |

### 2.3.2 Types of Furrows

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 mandates** knowledge of the following furrow types:

1. **Straight Furrows** — aligned along the general slope direction; most common.
2. **Contour Furrows** — laid out approximately on the contour; used on rolling land.
3. **Serrated Furrows** — alternating deep and shallow sections to reduce slope and erosion.
4. **Corrugation Furrows** — small, shallow furrows (~5 cm) for close-growing crops.
5. **Dead-end (Blocked) Furrows** — closed at the lower end; suitable for permeable soils.
6. **Open-end Furrows** — drain at the lower end into a tailwater ditch.
7. **Siphon/Tube Furrows** — water introduced via siphons from a head ditch.
8. **Gated Pipe Furrows** — water supplied through a pressurized pipe with gates.

### 2.3.3 Infiltration in Furrows (per unit length)

For wetted perimeter infiltration:
$$I_f = k \cdot t^{a} \cdot W_f \cdot L_f$$

The **volume balance** for a single furrow:
$$q_f \cdot t = \sigma_z \cdot A_0 \cdot x + V_{\text{infiltrated,\,x}}$$

where $x$ is the advance distance at time $t$.

### 2.3.4 Lateral Wetting and Spacing
The horizontal lateral spread (Kruse's equation):
$$W_{\text{lateral}} = C \cdot \left(\frac{q_f \cdot t}{k}\right)^{b}$$

Empirically, furrow spacing should not exceed:
$$W_f \leq 0.8 \times \text{root spread diameter}$$

### 2.3.5 Furrow Design Equations

**Required volume per furrow:**
$$V_f = d_n \times W_f \times L_f \times \frac{1}{E_a}$$

**Maximum non-erosive discharge (from US SCS):**
$$q_{\text{max}} = \frac{0.6}{S_f} \quad \text{(L/s per furrow)}$$

**Minimum non-erosive length relation:**
$$L_f = \frac{10 \cdot q_f^{0.5}}{S_f^{0.25}}$$

### 2.3.6 Evaluation of Furrow Irrigation
Same indices as border ($E_a, E_s, DU, CU, DPR, TWR$).
**Additional metric specific to furrow:**
$$\text{Tailwater Loss Fraction (TLF)} = \frac{V_{\text{runoff from furrow}}}{q_f \cdot T_{co}}$$

### 2.3.7 Ideal Wetting Pattern — Furrow
The **ideal** furrow wetting has:
- **Moisture front** moves vertically AND laterally to merge with adjacent furrows.
- The **time** for the wetting front from two adjacent furrows to meet equals the time to infiltrate required depth.
- **No tailwater runoff** (closed end) OR all tailwater captured and reused.

> [!TIP]
> The "ideal" cross-section of wetting looks like an **overlapping circle pair** (when seen from above), with the **wetting front from adjacent furrows meeting exactly at the mid-point** between them at the end of the irrigation event.

## 2.4 BASIN IRRIGATION — Detailed Theoretical Framework

### 2.4.1 Types of Basins

| Basin Type | Description | Typical Use |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Check Basin** | Small rectangular plots (1–10 m²) with bunds; high precision | Vegetables, pulses |
| **Rice Basin (Paddy Basin)** | Large, level flooded plots (0.05 to 5 ha) | Paddy cultivation |
| **Orchard Basin** | Tree-base circular/square depressions, ~2–5 m diameter | Fruit trees, coconut |
| **Ring Basin** | Circular bund around each tree | Coffee, banana, mulberry |
| **Contour Basin** | Basins on contour with reduced slope | Hilly terrain |
| **Direct Sown Paddy Basin** | Level field, no transplanting | Mechanized rice farming |

### 2.4.2 Basin Geometry and Size

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|:---|:---|
| Basin area (Check basin) | 1 to 10 m² |
| Basin area (Rice basin) | 0.05 to 5 ha |
| Basin shape | Square, rectangular, circular, ring |
| Bund height (Check) | 0.15 to 0.25 m |
| Bund height (Paddy) | 0.30 to 0.50 m |
| Slope of basin floor | **Zero** (level) |

### 2.4.3 Design Equations for Basin

**Volume of water to fill the basin:**
$$V_b = (d_n + d_{\text{puddling}}) \times A_b$$

For paddy, **puddling depth** ~ 0.05 to 0.10 m is added.

**Time to fill the basin:**
$$T_{\text{fill}} = \frac{V_b}{Q_{\text{inlet}}}$$

**Time of opportunity (basin) = fill time + storage time:**
$$T_{op} = T_{\text{fill}} + T_{\text{storage}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **time of opportunity** in basin irrigation is *uniform* across the entire field (since basin is small and level). This is the **chief advantage** of basin irrigation — high uniformity and application efficiency (often 90–100%).

### 2.4.4 Ideal Wetting Pattern — Basin

> [!NOTE]
> The **ideal** basin wetting pattern is a **uniform horizontal layer** of water of constant depth over the entire basin, with infiltration occurring uniformly downward. The shape is essentially a **2D rectangular block** in cross-section (1D vertical infiltration).

**Uniformity is maximum in basin** because:
1. No surface runoff (closed bunds).
2. No lateral variation in infiltration time.
3. Static water acts equally on every point.

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Quick-Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner's hint:** The following table consolidates every formula required for Module 1 numerical/derivation problems.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Application Efficiency | $E_a = V_s / V_a$ | % | Beneficial volume ratio |
| 2 | Storage Efficiency | $E_s = V_s / V_r$ | % | vs Required volume |
| 3 | Distribution Uniformity | $DU = Y_{25\%} / \bar{Y}$ | % | Lower quarter |
| 4 | Christiansen's $CU$ | $100[1 - \sum\vert Y_i - \bar{Y}\vert/(n\bar{Y})]$ | % | Statistical |
| 5 | Deep Percolation Ratio | $DPR = V_{dp} / V_a$ | % | Below root zone |
| 6 | Tailwater Ratio | $TWR = V_{tw} / V_a$ | % | Runoff loss |
| 7 | Kostiakov Infiltration | $Z = k t^{a}$ | cm | Empirical |
| 8 | Net Irrigation Depth | $d_n = (FC-WP)\gamma_d D_{rz}/100$ | cm | Soil-water |
| 9 | Gross Irrigation Depth | $d_g = d_n / E_a$ | cm | Design |
| 10 | Time to infiltrate $d_g$ | $t_{req} = (d_g / k)^{1/a}$ | min | Required |
| 11 | Max non-erosive $q$ (furrow) | $q_{max} = 0.6 / S_f$ | L/s | US SCS |
| 12 | Furrow length limit | $L_f = 10\,q_f^{0.5} / S_f^{0.25}$ | m | US SCS |
| 13 | Basin volume | $V_b = d_g A_b$ | m³ | Per basin |
| 14 | Border advance time | $T_L = V_{req}/[Q(1 - 0.5 r)]$ | min | Simplified |
| 15 | Merriam's discharge rule | $q = 0.006\,W\,S^{0.5}$ | L/s/m | Empirical |
| 16 | Mass balance identity | $E_a + DPR + TWR = 100$ | % | Always true |

**Commonly used Infiltration Parameters (for KTU numericals):**

| Soil Type | $k$ (cm/min$^a$) | $a$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Sand | 0.55 | 0.45 |
| Loamy sand | 0.40 | 0.50 |
| Sandy loam | 0.30 | 0.55 |
| Loam | 0.25 | 0.45 |
| Clay loam | 0.18 | 0.40 |
| Clay | 0.10 | 0.35 |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Surface irrigation design directly impacts **water-use efficiency** in agriculture. In India, where ~80% of freshwater is consumed by agriculture, improving $E_a$ from 40% to 70% can save **millions of cubic metres of water annually** per district — a key driver of sustainable agriculture and SDG-6 (Clean Water) compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Christiansen's Coefficient of Uniformity ($CU$)

This derivation is a **favourite KTU Module 1 question** (frequently asked for 7 marks).

**Starting point:** We want a statistical measure of how close the individual infiltration depths $Y_i$ are to the mean $\bar{Y}$.

**Step 1:** Define the mean depth of infiltration.
$$\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$

**Step 2:** Define the sum of absolute deviations from the mean.
$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert Y_i - \bar{Y} \vert$$

**Step 3:** Define the average absolute deviation.
$$D_{\text{avg}} = \frac{D}{n} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert Y_i - \bar{Y} \vert$$

**Step 4:** Express uniformity as the *complement* of relative deviation.
$$CU = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{D_{\text{avg}}}{\bar{Y}}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $D_{\text{avg}}$ to obtain the final form:
$$CU = 100 \times \left[1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert Y_i - \bar{Y} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{Y}}\right]$$

**Step 6 (Verification):** If all $Y_i = \bar{Y}$ (perfect uniformity):
$$D = 0 \Rightarrow CU = 100\%$$ 

**Step 7 (Verification):** If deviations equal $\bar{Y}$ (extreme non-uniformity):
$$D = n\bar{Y} \Rightarrow CU = 0\%$$ 

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary range:** $0\% \leq CU \leq 100\%$. In KTU problems, a $CU \geq 85\%$ indicates *excellent* uniformity; $CU < 70\%$ is *poor*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Irrigation Depth from Soil-Water Relationship

This derivation is required for **all three surface methods** (border, furrow, basin).

**Step 1:** Define *Available Water Capacity* (AWC) of the soil:
$$AWC = FC - WP \quad \text{(\% by weight)}$$

**Step 2:** Convert AWC to **volumetric** form using dry unit weight $\gamma_d$:
$$AWC_v = \frac{(FC - WP)}{100} \times \gamma_d \quad \text{(cm of water per cm of soil)}$$

**Step 3:** Net depth of water to refill root zone to field capacity:
$$d_n = AWC_v \times D_{rz} = \frac{(FC - WP)}{100} \times \gamma_d \times D_{rz}$$

**Step 4 (Example calculation):**
Given: $FC = 28\%$, $WP = 14\%$, $\gamma_d = 1.4$ g/cm³, $D_{rz} = 60$ cm.

$$d_n = \frac{(28 - 14)}{100} \times 1.4 \times 60 = 0.14 \times 1.4 \times 60 = 11.76\,\text{cm}$$

**Step 5 (Gross depth with $E_a$):**
If $E_a = 70\%$, then:
$$d_g = \frac{d_n}{E_a} = \frac{11.76}{0.70} = 16.8\,\text{cm}$$

**Step 6 (Mass balance check):**
Volume stored = $d_n \times A = 11.76 \times A$ (beneficial)
Volume applied = $d_g \times A = 16.8 \times A$
Losses = $(d_g - d_n) \times A = 5.04 \times A$ (percolation + runoff)

$$E_a = \frac{11.76}{16.8} \times 100 = 70\% \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kostiakov's Infiltration Equation & Required Time

**Step 1 (Empirical starting point):** For a given soil, cumulative infiltration $Z$ vs time $t$ follows a power law:
$$Z = k \cdot t^{a}$$

**Step 2 (Initial infiltration rate):**
$$i(t) = \frac{dZ}{dt} = k \cdot a \cdot t^{a-1}$$

**Step 3 (Basic infiltration rate $i_b$):** As $t \to \infty$, $i \to 0$ for Kostiakov. To introduce $i_b$, the **modified Kostiakov** form (Kostiakov–Lewis) is used:
$$Z = k \cdot t^{a} + i_b \cdot t$$

**Step 4 (Solve for $t_{req}$):** Set $Z = d_g$:
$$d_g = k \cdot t_{req}^{a}$$
$$t_{req}^{a} = \frac{d_g}{k}$$
$$\boxed{t_{req} = \left(\frac{d_g}{k}\right)^{1/a}}$$

**Step 5 (Numerical example):**
Given: $d_g = 8$ cm, $k = 0.30$ cm/min$^a$, $a = 0.55$ for sandy loam.

$$t_{req} = \left(\frac{8}{0.30}\right)^{1/0.55} = (26.67)^{1.818}$$
$$\log(26.67) = 1.426 \Rightarrow 1.818 \times 1.426 = 2.593$$
$$t_{req} = 10^{2.593} \approx 392\,\text{min} \approx 6.53\,\text{hours}$$

**Step 6 (Design implication):** The border cut-off time $T_{co}$ must be at least $t_{req}$ for the **head end** to receive full depth.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Lewis–Milne Volume Balance for Advance Phase

The **fundamental volume balance** for surface irrigation:

**Step 1 (Statement):** Volume of water supplied = Volume stored on surface + Volume infiltrated.

$$Q \cdot t = V_{\text{surface}}(t) + V_{\text{infiltrated}}(t)$$

**Step 2 (Surface storage approximation, ellipsoidal profile):**
For a border of width $W$ with wetted area approximated as half-ellipse:
$$V_{\text{surface}} = 0.77 \cdot \sigma_z \cdot A_0 \cdot x$$

where $\sigma_z$ = surface shape factor (typically 0.7–0.8), $A_0$ = cross-sectional flow area at inlet, $x$ = advance distance.

**Step 3 (Infiltrated volume, with $Z = kt^a$):**
$$V_{\text{infiltrated}} = \int_{0}^{x} k \cdot t_a^{a} \, dx$$
where $t_a(x)$ is the advance time at position $x$.

**Step 4 (Assumption — power advance):** $x = p \cdot t^r$, giving $t_a = (x/p)^{1/r}$.

**Step 5 (Combining):**
$$Q \cdot t = 0.77 \sigma_z A_0 \cdot p \cdot t^r + k \int_{0}^{x} \left(\frac{x'}{p}\right)^{a/r} dx'$$

This yields the **Philip–Farre** equation, but for KTU the **simplified form** is used:
$$\boxed{T_L = \frac{V_{\text{req}}}{Q \cdot (1 - 0.5 r)}}$$

where $r$ is the surface storage fraction.

**Step 6 (Numerical example):**
$V_{req} = 200$ m³, $Q = 50$ L/s = 0.05 m³/s, $r = 0.1$.
$$T_L = \frac{200}{0.05 \times (1 - 0.05)} = \frac{200}{0.0475} \approx 4210\,\text{s} \approx 70.2\,\text{min}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Maximum Non-Erosive Furrow Discharge

**Step 1 (Empirical basis — US SCS):** In furrows, scour occurs when tractive force exceeds soil resistance.

**Step 2 (Manning's equation for furrow):**
$$V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S_f^{1/2}$$

**Step 3 (Tractive stress balance):** Maximum permissible tractive force on cohesive soil:
$$\tau_c = 0.06 \cdot \gamma_w \cdot R \cdot S_f$$

**Step 4 (Substituting $V = q_f / A$, with empirical constants):** After simplification, US SCS gives:
$$q_{\text{max}} = \frac{0.6}{S_f} \quad \text{L/s per furrow}$$

**Step 5 (Example):** For $S_f = 0.005$ (0.5%):
$$q_{\text{max}} = \frac{0.6}{0.005} = 120\,\text{L/s}$$ 

*Note: This is a per-furrow limit; check the actual SCS chart for design.*

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **comparison matrix** is the most frequently asked 14-mark question in KTU Module 1.

| Parameter | Border | Furrow | Basin |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Land slope** | 0.05–2% | 0.1–1% | 0% (level) |
| **Best-suited crop** | Close-growing (wheat, grass, alfalfa) | Row crops (maize, cotton, sugarcane) | Paddy, trees |
| **Water conveyance** | Sheet flow | Channelized in furrows | Flooded in basin |
| **Infiltration mechanism** | Vertical only | Vertical + lateral | Vertical only |
| **Application efficiency** | 60–75% | 55–70% | 85–95% |
| **Uniformity** | High (along length, poor across) | Moderate | Very High |
| **Runoff risk** | Moderate | High (open furrows) | None |
| **Deep percolation risk** | Moderate | High at upper end | Low (controlled) |
| **Suitability for paddy** | No | No | Excellent |
| **Labour requirement** | Low | Moderate | Moderate-High |
| **Land grading needed** | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Capital cost** | Low | Low-Moderate | High (levelling) |
| **Typical size** | $L$=100–200 m, $W$=5–10 m | $L_f$=50–400 m, $W_f$=0.5–1.5 m | $A_b$=0.05–5 ha |
| **Wetting pattern shape** | 2D rectangular sheet | Wedge/elliptical lateral spread | Uniform block |
| **Dominant loss** | Tailwater runoff | Deep percolation | Seepage through bunds |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU 7-Mark Problem

**Problem:** A border strip is 200 m long, 8 m wide, on a sandy loam soil. $FC = 30\%$, $WP = 15\%$, $\gamma_d = 1.5$ g/cm³, root zone depth = 50 cm, $k = 0.30$ cm/min$^a$, $a = 0.55$, $E_a = 70\%$. Calculate: (a) net and gross depth of irrigation, (b) time required to infiltrate gross depth, (c) volume of water to be applied, (d) inflow rate needed if the irrigation must complete in 4 hours.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**
$$d_n = \frac{(30 - 15)}{100} \times 1.5 \times 50 = 0.15 \times 1.5 \times 50 = 11.25\,\text{cm}$$
$$d_g = \frac{11.25}{0.70} = 16.07\,\text{cm}$$

**Part (b):**
$$t_{req} = \left(\frac{16.07}{0.30}\right)^{1/0.55} = (53.57)^{1.818}$$
$$\log(53.57) = 1.729 \Rightarrow 1.818 \times 1.729 = 3.144$$
$$t_{req} = 10^{3.144} \approx 1393\,\text{min} \approx 23.2\,\text{hours}$$

**Part (c):**
$$V = d_g \times W \times L = 0.1607\,\text{m} \times 8\,\text{m} \times 200\,\text{m} = 257.1\,\text{m}^3$$

**Part (d):**
If $T_L = 4$ hours = 240 min (and assuming $T_L \approx t_{req}$ design):
$$Q = \frac{V}{T} = \frac{257.1}{4 \times 3600} = 0.01786\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 17.86\,\text{L/s}$$

**Unit discharge check:**
$$q = \frac{Q}{W} = \frac{17.86}{8} = 2.23\,\text{L/s/m}$$ 

This is within the typical 2–5 L/s/m range for sandy loam. **Design acceptable.**

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1.** Define **Application Efficiency** and **Storage Efficiency** of a surface irrigation system. State the ideal values and the typical range for well-designed systems.

**Model Answer:**
Application efficiency $E_a$ is the ratio of volume of water stored in the root zone to the volume of water applied at the field inlet, expressed as a percentage. **Ideal value = 100%**, typical range 60–75%.

$$E_a = \frac{V_{\text{stored}}}{V_{\text{applied}}} \times 100\,\%$$

Storage efficiency $E_s$ is the ratio of water actually stored to the water *required* to refill the root zone. **Ideal value = 100%**.

$$E_s = \frac{V_{\text{stored}}}{V_{\text{required}}} \times 100\,\%$$

The two indices differ in the denominator: $E_a$ measures *application* efficiency, while $E_s$ measures *soil-moisture adequacy*.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q2.** List **any three types of furrows** used in surface irrigation. State one specific use of each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Straight Furrow** — Aligned along general slope; used for row crops like maize, cotton on uniform terrain.
2. **Serrated Furrow** — Alternating deep/shallow segments; used on steep slopes to control erosion and reduce advance velocity.
3. **Contour Furrow** — Aligned approximately on the contour; used on rolling/undulating land for row crops where land levelling is uneconomical.
4. (Optional) **Dead-end Furrow** — Closed at the lower end; used in highly permeable soils to minimize runoff.
5. (Optional) **Corrugation Furrow** — Small shallow (~5 cm) furrows; used for close-growing crops like small grains, grasses.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Question A]**

**Q A. (a)** Explain the **classification of surface irrigation methods** in detail. Compare **border**, **furrow**, and **basin** irrigation based on slope suitability, water-use efficiency, and dominant losses.

**(b)** A border strip 150 m long and 6 m wide is to be irrigated on a **sandy loam** soil with $FC = 28\%$, $WP = 14\%$, $\gamma_d = 1.45$ g/cm³, root zone depth = 60 cm, Kostiakov parameters $k = 0.30$ cm/min$^a$ and $a = 0.55$. Assuming application efficiency $E_a = 70\%$, calculate:
(i) Net and gross depth of irrigation
(ii) Time required to infiltrate the gross depth at the head end
(iii) Total volume of water to be applied
(iv) Required unit discharge using Merriam's empirical rule for $S = 0.2\%$

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Surface irrigation methods are classified as **border, furrow, and basin irrigation** based on how water is conveyed and applied.

| Method | Slope | Efficiency | Dominant Loss |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Border | 0.05–2% | 60–75% | Tailwater runoff |
| Furrow | 0.1–1% | 55–70% | Deep percolation |
| Basin | 0% (level) | 85–95% | Seepage through bunds |

**Part (b) (i):** 
$$d_n = \frac{(28 - 14)}{100} \times 1.45 \times 60 = 0.14 \times 1.45 \times 60 = 12.18\,\text{cm}$$
$$d_g = \frac{12.18}{0.70} = 17.4\,\text{cm}$$

**Part (b) (ii):**
$$t_{req} = \left(\frac{17.4}{0.30}\right)^{1/0.55} = (58)^{1.818}$$
$$\log(58) = 1.763, \quad 1.818 \times 1.763 = 3.207$$
$$t_{req} = 10^{3.207} \approx 1611\,\text{min} \approx 26.85\,\text{hours}$$

**Part (b) (iii):**
$$V = 0.174 \times 6 \times 150 = 156.6\,\text{m}^3$$

**Part (b) (iv):** Using Merriam's rule $q = 0.006 \cdot W \cdot S^{0.5}$:
$$q = 0.006 \times 6 \times (0.002)^{0.5} = 0.036 \times 0.0447 = 0.00161\,\text{m}^3/\text{s/m}$$
$$q \approx 1.61\,\text{L/s/m}$$

For the full border: $Q_{total} = 1.61 \times 6 = 9.66$ L/s.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Question B]**

**Q B. (a)** Discuss the **ideal wetting pattern** for a furrow irrigation system. With the help of neat sketches, explain how the **lateral spread** of water from adjacent furrows creates a continuous wetted zone. Define the **time ratio concept**.

**(b)** A field has furrows of length 200 m, spacing 0.75 m, slope 0.4%, with $q_f = 1.5$ L/s per furrow. The soil is clay loam with $k = 0.18$ cm/min$^a$, $a = 0.40$, and the required net depth is 6 cm. Determine:
(i) Maximum permissible non-erosive discharge per furrow (US SCS rule)
(ii) Whether the given $q_f$ is safe
(iii) The maximum safe furrow length
(iv) The application efficiency if deep percolation accounts for 20% and runoff for 5% of applied water.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** In an *ideal* furrow irrigation, the **wetting front from adjacent furrows meets exactly at the mid-point** between furrows at the end of the irrigation event, ensuring uniform moisture across the field. The **time ratio concept** (Hart et al. 1968) requires:
$$T_{\text{advance}} = T_{\text{opportunity}} = T_{\text{infiltration required}}$$
implying that the **time water is on the surface** at any point should equal the **time required to infiltrate the design depth** at that point.

**Part (b) (i):** US SCS rule: $q_{\text{max}} = 0.6/S_f$
$$q_{\text{max}} = \frac{0.6}{0.004} = 150\,\text{L/s}$$ 

(Note: 150 L/s is the upper limit; the design uses 1.5 L/s which is far below the limit.)

**Part (b) (ii):** Given $q_f = 1.5$ L/s, which is **well below** 150 L/s. **Hence the design is safe** from erosion.

**Part (b) (iii):** $L_f = 10 q_f^{0.5} / S_f^{0.25}$
$$L_f = \frac{10 \times (1.5)^{0.5}}{(0.004)^{0.25}} = \frac{10 \times 1.2247}{0.2512} = \frac{12.247}{0.2512} \approx 48.76\,\text{m}$$

*Note:* This is much smaller than 200 m, suggesting the given $L_f$ may need re-checking. The actual formula may be applied with different units — always verify constants with your course textbook.

**Part (b) (iv):** Mass balance: $E_a + DPR + TWR = 100\%$
$$E_a = 100 - 20 - 5 = 75\%$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in Module 1 surface irrigation problems:**
> 
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** $d_g$ in cm vs m³ calculation. Always convert cm → m before multiplying with $W \times L$ in metres.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $E_a$ with $E_s$:** $E_a$ uses $V_{\text{applied}}$ in denominator; $E_s$ uses $V_{\text{required}}$ in denominator. Swapping these costs 2–3 marks.
> 
> 3. **Kostiakov exponent confusion:** $a$ is dimensionless; $k$ has units cm/min$^a$. When taking $(d_g/k)^{1/a}$, ensure proper dimensional consistency.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting mass balance identity:** Always state $E_a + DPR + TWR = 100$ as a starting step in any efficiency numerical.
> 
> 5. **Misidentifying ideal wetting pattern:** Many students describe *all three* methods identically. Examiner expects **method-specific** description (sheet for border, overlapping ellipses for furrow, uniform block for basin).
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to specify units** in final answers — losing 0.5–1 mark each.
> 
> 7. **Confusing basin types** (check vs ring vs orchard) — a common 2-mark loss in 14-mark questions.
> 
> 8. **In Merriam's rule,** $S$ is taken as decimal (0.002 for 0.2%), not as percent. Wrong substitution loses all marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 1**

### ✅ Surface Irrigation — Core Concepts
- [x] Surface irrigation = gravity-driven, accounts for ~85% of global irrigation.
- [x] Three phases: **Advance → Storage → Recession**.
- [x] Three methods: **Border (sheet), Furrow (channels), Basin (plots)**.

### ✅ Efficiency Indices
- [x] $E_a = V_s / V_a$; $E_s = V_s / V_r$; $DU = Y_{25\%} / \bar{Y}$.
- [x] $CU = 100[1 - \sum\vert Y_i - \bar{Y}\vert/(n\bar{Y})]$.
- [x] **Mass balance identity:** $E_a + DPR + TWR = 100\%$.

### ✅ Border Irrigation
- [x] $W = 3$–$15$ m, $L = 60$–$400$ m, $S = 0.05$–$2\%$, $q = 2$–$5$ L/s/m.
- [x] Merriam's rule: $q = 0.006\,W\,S^{0.5}$.
- [x] Ideal = uniform sheet, $T_L = t_{\text{req}}$.

### ✅ Furrow Irrigation
- [x] $L_f = 50$–$400$ m, $W_f = 0.5$–$1.5$ m, $S_f = 0.1$–$1\%$.
- [x] **8 types:** Straight, Contour, Serrated, Corrugation, Dead-end, Open-end, Siphon, Gated-pipe.
- [x] US SCS: $q_{\text{max}} = 0.6/S_f$.
- [x] Ideal = overlapping elliptical wetting from adjacent furrows meeting at mid-point.

### ✅ Basin Irrigation
- [x] **5 types:** Check, Rice (Paddy), Orchard, Ring, Contour.
- [x] **Level field** (zero slope) — highest $E_a$ (85–95%).
- [x] Puddling depth ~ 5–10 cm for paddy.
- [x] Ideal = uniform horizontal water layer, 1D vertical infiltration.

### ✅ Kostiakov Infiltration
- [x] $Z = k t^a$.
- [x] $t_{\text{req}} = (d_g/k)^{1/a}$.
- [x] Sandy soil: high $k$, high $a$ → fast infiltration.
- [x] Clay soil: low $k$, low $a$ → slow infiltration.

### ✅ KTU Exam-Specific Quick Numbers
- [x] Field capacity range: 25–35% (loamy soils).
- [x] Root zone depth: 30 cm (shallow) to 120 cm (deep).
- [x] $E_a$ benchmark: 60% (poor) to 95% (basin).
- [x] $CU$ benchmark: ≥ 85% (excellent), < 70% (poor).

### ✅ Definitions to Memorize Verbatim
- [x] **Application Efficiency** — $V_{\text{stored}} / V_{\text{applied}}$.
- [x] **Christiansen's $CU$** — statistical measure of depth uniformity.
- [x] **Ideal Wetting Pattern** — $T_L = T_{\text{opportunity}} = T_{\text{infiltration required}}$.
- [x] **Border** — strip irrigation by sheet flow between parallel levees.
- [x] **Furrow** — small parallel channels for row crops.
- [x] **Basin** — level plots surrounded by bunds; flooded.
- [x] **Time Ratio Concept** — advance time should equal opportunity time.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Crop Water Requirements — Foundational Definition

**Crop Water Requirement (CWR)** is the total volume of water needed by a crop to grow optimally from sowing to harvest, accounting for all pathways of water loss (evapotranspiration) and gain (effective rainfall, soil moisture contribution). It is formally expressed in **millimeters (mm) of water depth per crop period** or in **cubic meters per hectare (m³/ha)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> *"Crop water requirement is defined as the total quantity of water required by a crop during its entire growth period, expressed as depth of water (mm) or volume per unit area (m³/ha). It is essentially the consumptive use adjusted for effective rainfall and soil moisture contribution."*

Mathematically:

$$\text{CWR} = \text{ET}_c + \text{Application Losses} \pm \text{Soil Moisture Contribution}$$

---

## 1.2 Infiltration and Movement of Water in Soil

**Infiltration** is the downward entry and movement of water from the surface into the soil profile. The **infiltration rate (f)** is the rate at which water enters the soil surface, typically expressed in **mm/hr** or **cm/hr**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of infiltration like pouring water onto a dry kitchen sponge. Initially, the sponge drinks water rapidly (high infiltration rate). As the sponge saturates, the rate slows down to a steady trickle (steady-state infiltration). The final, constant rate is the **basic infiltration rate**.

Three zones of soil water movement:
1. **Saturation zone** — all pores filled with water.
2. **Transmission zone** — water moves downward under gravity.
3. **Wetting zone** — transition between wet and dry soil (sharp moisture gradient).

**Soil-Water Movement Mechanisms:**
- **Saturated flow** — governed by **Darcy's Law**: $v = K \cdot i$
- **Unsaturated flow** — governed by **Richards' Equation** (extension of Darcy's Law)
- **Capillary rise** — upward movement due to surface tension

---

## 1.3 Soil-Water-Plant Relationship

This is the **continuum (SPAC — Soil-Plant-Atmosphere Continuum)** through which water moves from soil → root → stem → leaf → atmosphere.

| Soil Constant | Field Capacity (FC) | Wilting Point (WP) | Available Water (AW) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Definition** | Moisture after gravity drainage ceases | Moisture below which plants cannot extract water | FC − WP |
| **Soil Type Example (Sandy)** | ~10% | ~4% | ~6% |
| **Soil Type Example (Clay)** | ~35% | ~17% | ~18% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Readily Available Water (RAW):** A fraction (~50–70%) of the available water that the plant can extract without stress. When soil moisture drops below RAW, the plant begins to experience water stress.

**Key Soil Moisture Terms:**
- **Saturation (θₛ):** All pores filled with water.
- **Field Capacity (θ_fc):** Moisture held against gravity (~ −0.33 bar for loams).
- **Permanent Wilting Point (θ_pwp):** Moisture at −15 bar suction.
- **Hygroscopic Coefficient:** Moisture at −31 bar (below WP, unavailable to plants).
- **Total Available Water (TAW):** $\text{TAW} = (\theta_{fc} - \theta_{wp}) \times \text{Root Depth}$
- **Management Allowed Deficit (MAD):** Threshold below which irrigation must be applied.

---

## 1.4 Water Requirement of Crops

The water needed by a crop is governed by:
- **Crop type & variety** (paddy vs. sugarcane)
- **Climate** (temperature, humidity, wind, sunshine)
- **Growth stage** (Kc varies with stage)
- **Soil type & depth**
- **Agronomic practices**

**Classification by FAO Irrigation Manual:**

| Category | Definition |
|:---|:---|
| **Consumptive Use (Cu)** | Water used by crop as ET |
| **Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR)** | Cu − Effective Rainfall |
| **Gross Irrigation Requirement (GIR)** | NIR / Application Efficiency |
| **Field Irrigation Requirement (FIR)** | GIR + conveyance losses |

---

## 1.5 Evapotranspiration (ET) and Consumptive Use

**Evapotranspiration (ET)** is the combined process of:
- **Evaporation (E):** Water lost from soil & plant surfaces to atmosphere.
- **Transpiration (T):** Water absorbed by roots, moved through plant, and lost via stomata.

**Consumptive Use (Cu) ≈ ET** for practical engineering purposes (since ET is the primary consumptive loss in irrigated fields).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference ET (ET₀):** ET from a reference surface (grass, 0.12 m height, well-watered). It is a **climate parameter** (mm/day).
>
> **Crop ET (ET_c):** $\text{ET}_c = K_c \times \text{ET}_0$ where $K_c$ is the **crop coefficient**.

**Common ET Estimation Methods (KTU Expected):**
1. **Blaney-Criddle** (temperature-based, empirical)
2. **Penman-Monteith** (FAO-56 standard, combination method)
3. **Hargreaves-Samani** (temperature-based)
4. **Pan Evaporation Method** ($ET_c = K_p \times E_{pan}$)

---

## 1.6 Effective Rainfall

**Effective Rainfall (ER or Pe)** is the portion of total rainfall that is **useful for crop growth** — it infiltrates into the root zone and is stored in the soil.

$$\text{ER} = \text{Total Rainfall} - \text{Runoff} - \text{Deep Percolation} - \text{Evaporation (from rain)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a glass of water poured onto a sloped, rocky field. Not all the water stays on the field — some flows off as runoff, some drains through cracks, some evaporates from the hot soil. The remaining water that actually soaks into the root zone is the "effective rainfall."

---

## 1.7 Irrigation Requirement

**Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR):**

$$\text{NIR} = \text{ET}_c - \text{Effective Rainfall} \pm \text{Soil Moisture Depletion}$$

**Gross Irrigation Requirement (GIR):**

$$\text{GIR} = \frac{\text{NIR}}{\text{Application Efficiency } (\eta_a)}$$

For a field/farm/canal system:

$$\text{FIR} = \frac{\text{ET}_c - \text{ER}}{(\eta_c \cdot \eta_a \cdot \eta_s)}$$

where $\eta_c$ = conveyance efficiency, $\eta_a$ = application efficiency, $\eta_s$ = storage/field canal efficiency.

---

## 1.8 Soil Water Balance

The **Soil Water Balance Equation** is the bookkeeping of water in the root zone over a time interval:

$$\Delta S = P + I - \text{ET}_c - R - DP$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:---:|:---|
| $\Delta S$ | Change in root-zone soil moisture storage |
| $P$ | Precipitation (rainfall) |
| $I$ | Irrigation applied |
| $\text{ET}_c$ | Crop evapotranspiration |
| $R$ | Surface runoff |
| $DP$ | Deep percolation below root zone |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Treat the root zone as a **bank account**. Deposits are rain + irrigation. Withdrawals are ET + runoff + drainage. The closing balance (ΔS) must be ≥ 0 for the crop to avoid stress.

---

## 1.9 Yield Response to Water

**Core Concept:** Crop yield is directly proportional to water consumed (ET) — but only up to a point. Beyond optimum water, yield may drop due to waterlogging.

**Key Quantitative Relationships:**
- **Water Use Efficiency (WUE):** $\text{WUE} = \dfrac{Y}{\text{ET}_c}$ (kg/m³ or kg/ha-mm)
- **Crop Water Production Function:** Mathematical relationship between yield (Y) and water (ET or I).

**Two Important Models:**
1. **Stewart's Linear Model:** $Y = a + b \cdot \text{ET}_c$
2. **Jensen's Multiplicative Model:** $\dfrac{Y}{Y_m} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( \dfrac{\text{ET}_i}{\text{ET}_{m,i}} \right)^{\lambda_i}$

---

## 1.10 Production Functions

**Crop Water Production Function (CWPF)** is the mathematical expression relating crop yield (Y) to water input (Irrigation or ET).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students are expected to know the difference between the **Total Available Water (TAW)** approach, the **Deficit Irrigation (DI)** approach, and the **Yield-ET** functional models (linear, quadratic, multiplicative).

**Types of Production Functions (KTU syllabus):**
1. **Linear (Stewart):** $Y = a + b \cdot \text{ET}$
2. **Quadratic:** $Y = a + b \cdot \text{ET} + c \cdot \text{ET}^2$
3. **Multiplicative (Jensen):** Stage-wise stress sensitivity.
4. **FAO-AquaCrop Approach:** $Y = Y_x \cdot f(\text{ET}, \text{WP}, \text{stress factors})$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Infiltration Theory — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Infiltration Process Phases
1. **Wetting phase** — soil surface becomes wet.
2. **Transition phase** — infiltration capacity decreases rapidly.
3. **Steady-state phase** — approaches basic infiltration rate ($f_c$ or $f_0$).

### 2.1.2 Why Infiltration Rate Decreases with Time
- Surface pores get clogged by fine particles (**surface sealing**).
- Swelling of clay particles.
- Air entrapment reduces hydraulic conductivity.
- Gradual reduction in matric potential gradient.

### 2.1.3 Cumulative Infiltration (F)
**Cumulative infiltration** $F(t)$ is the total depth of water infiltrated over time $t$.

$$F = \int_0^t f(t) \, dt$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Infiltration Models

| Model | Equation | Parameters | Boundary / Units |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Horton's Equation** | $f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$ | $f_0$: initial rate, $f_c$: final rate, $k$: decay constant | $f$ in mm/hr, $t$ in hr, $k$ in hr⁻¹ |
| **Philip's Equation** | $f(t) = \frac{1}{2} S t^{-0.5} + A$ | $S$: sorptivity, $A$: hydraulic conductivity | $f$ in mm/hr, $t$ in hr |
| **Kostiakov's Equation** | $f(t) = a \cdot t^{-b}$ | $a$, $b$: empirical constants | $a$ in mm/hr⁽¹⁻ᵇ⁾, $b$ dimensionless |
| **Cumulative Horton** | $F(t) = f_c \cdot t + \dfrac{f_0 - f_c}{k}(1 - e^{-kt})$ | Derived by integration | $F$ in mm |
| **Cumulative Kostiakov** | $F(t) = \dfrac{a}{1-b} \cdot t^{(1-b)}$ | Integrated form | $F$ in mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Horton's equation is best for **surface ponded infiltration**. Philip's equation is theoretically rigorous for **early-time infiltration**. Kostiakov's is purely **empirical** and fails at large $t$.

### 2.2.1 Darcy's Law for Soil Water Movement

**Saturated Flow:**

$$Q = K_s \cdot A \cdot i = K_s \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta h}{L}$$

**Darcy's Velocity (v):**

$$v = K_s \cdot i$$

where $K_s$ = saturated hydraulic conductivity (cm/hr), $i$ = hydraulic gradient.

**Unsaturated Flow (Darcy-Buckingham Law):**

$$q = -K(\theta) \cdot \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial z}$$

where $K(\theta)$ = unsaturated hydraulic conductivity (function of water content), $\psi$ = matric potential.

---

## 2.3 Soil-Water-Plant Continuum — Operational Breakdown

### 2.3.1 Total Available Water (TAW)

$$\text{TAW} = \dfrac{(\theta_{fc} - \theta_{wp})}{100} \cdot \rho_b \cdot Z_r$$

For practical field estimation:

$$\text{TAW (mm)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \theta_{fc,i} - \theta_{wp,i} \right) \times Z_i \times 10$$

where:
- $\theta_{fc,i}$, $\theta_{wp,i}$ = volumetric moisture at field capacity & wilting point (fraction)
- $Z_i$ = thickness of soil layer (mm)
- $n$ = number of soil layers in root zone

### 2.3.2 Management Allowed Deficit (MAD)

$$\text{MAD} = p \cdot \text{TAW}$$

where $p$ = depletion fraction (typically 0.3–0.7 depending on crop).

### 2.3.3 Irrigation Timing
- Irrigation is applied when depletion $D \geq \text{MAD}$.
- **Depletion (D)** at any time: $D = \text{TAW} - \text{Current Available Water}$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Evapotranspiration (ET) Estimation

| Method | Equation | Use Case |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Blaney-Criddle** | $u = K \cdot \sum \dfrac{t \cdot p}{100}$ (monthly) | Humid / sub-humid regions |
| **Hargreaves-Samani** | $\text{ET}_0 = 0.0023 \cdot (T_{mean} + 17.8) \cdot (T_{max} - T_{min})^{0.5} \cdot R_a$ | Data-scarce situations |
| **Penman-Monteith (FAO-56)** | $\text{ET}_0 = \dfrac{0.408 \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \dfrac{900}{T+273} u_2 (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma (1 + 0.34 u_2)}$ | **FAO standard (most reliable)** |
| **Pan Evaporation** | $\text{ET}_c = K_p \cdot E_{pan}$ | Easy field estimation |
| **Crop ET** | $\text{ET}_c = K_c \cdot \text{ET}_0$ | All conditions |

### 2.4.1 Penman-Monteith Variables
- $R_n$ = net radiation (MJ/m²/day)
- $G$ = soil heat flux density (MJ/m²/day)
- $\Delta$ = slope of saturation vapor pressure curve (kPa/°C)
- $\gamma$ = psychrometric constant (kPa/°C)
- $u_2$ = wind speed at 2 m height (m/s)
- $e_s - e_a$ = vapor pressure deficit (kPa)
- $T$ = mean daily air temperature (°C)

### 2.4.2 Crop Coefficient (Kc) Stages
1. **Initial stage (Kc_ini):** ~0.3–0.5 (bare soil, low ET)
2. **Crop development (Kc_dev):** 0.7–1.0 (increasing canopy)
3. **Mid-season (Kc_mid):** ~1.0–1.2 (peak demand)
4. **Late season (Kc_late):** 0.5–0.9 (maturity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** For paddy (rice), $K_c$ can reach 1.20–1.30 at peak. For vegetables, $K_c \approx 1.0$. Always refer to **FAO-56 Tables**.

---

## 2.5 Effective Rainfall Estimation — Theory

### 2.5.1 Empirical Formulae (KTU Expected)

**USDA Soil Conservation Service (SCS) Method:**

$$\text{ER} = \begin{cases} P \cdot \left( \dfrac{125 - 0.2P}{125} \right) & \text{if } P \leq 250 \text{ mm} \\ 125 + 0.1P & \text{if } P > 250 \text{ mm} \end{cases}$$

(For monthly data, in mm)

**Fixed Percentage Method:**

$$\text{ER} = \alpha \cdot P$$

where $\alpha$ = coefficient depending on slope and rainfall (typically 0.7–0.9).

### 2.5.2 Why We Need Effective Rainfall
- Heavy rainfall may cause runoff and deep percolation → not all available to crop.
- Light rainfall may evaporate before infiltrating.
- Overestimating effective rainfall → under-irrigation → crop stress.
- Underestimating → over-irrigation → waterlogging and wastage.

---

## 2.6 Irrigation Requirement — Complete Framework

### 2.6.1 Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR)

$$\boxed{\text{NIR} = \text{ET}_c - P_e}$$

For the entire crop period:

$$\text{NIR} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{ET}_{c,i} - P_{e,i})$$

### 2.6.2 Gross Irrigation Requirement (GIR)

$$\boxed{\text{GIR} = \dfrac{\text{NIR}}{\eta_a}}$$

### 2.6.3 Volume of Water Required (Volumetric)

$$V = \dfrac{\text{GIR (mm)} \times A \text{ (m}^2\text{)}}{1000} \text{ m}^3$$

Equivalently:

$$V = \dfrac{\text{GIR (mm)} \times A \text{ (ha)} \times 10}{1} \text{ m}^3$$

since 1 mm depth over 1 ha = 10 m³.

### 2.6.4 Duty and Delta Relationship

$$\text{Duty (ha/cumec)} = \dfrac{8.64 \times \text{Base Period (days)}}{\Delta \text{ (m)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Irrigation requirement calculations directly feed into **canal design**, **reservoir sizing**, **pump selection**, and **drip/sprinkler design**.

---

## 2.7 Soil Water Balance — Detailed Theory

### 2.7.1 The General Water Balance

$$\Delta S = (P + I + C) - (\text{ET} + R + DP + GW)$$

where:
- $P$ = precipitation
- $I$ = irrigation
- $C$ = capillary rise from groundwater
- $R$ = runoff
- $DP$ = deep percolation
- $GW$ = groundwater outflow (lateral)
- $\text{ET}$ = evapotranspiration
- $\Delta S$ = change in root-zone storage

### 2.7.2 Components Explained

| Component | Symbol | Typical Range | Notes |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Effective Rainfall | $P_e$ | 0–200 mm/month | Crop-period dependent |
| Net Irrigation | $I_n$ | Crop-specific | Depth of water applied |
| Capillary Rise | $C$ | 0–5 mm/day | Significant in shallow water tables |
| Evapotranspiration | $\text{ET}_c$ | 2–10 mm/day | Kc × ET₀ |
| Runoff | $R$ | 0–30% of $P$ | Sloping fields → higher |
| Deep Percolation | $DP$ | 0–40% of $(P+I)$ | Sandy soils → higher |

### 2.7.3 Application: Irrigation Scheduling
- Set up water balance on a **daily or weekly** basis.
- When $\theta \leq \theta_{MAD}$ → trigger irrigation.
- Depth of irrigation = (FC − current moisture) × root depth.

---

## 2.8 Yield Response to Water — Theory

### 2.8.1 Water Use Efficiency (WUE)

$$\text{WUE} = \dfrac{Y_a}{\text{ET}_c}$$

$$\text{WUE}_{irrigation} = \dfrac{Y}{\text{Irrigation Applied}}$$

### 2.8.2 Deficit Irrigation Principle
- **DI** = deliberate under-irrigation during stress-tolerant growth stages.
- Increases WUE without major yield loss.
- Maximizes water productivity (kg/m³).

### 2.8.3 Why Yield Drops with Water Stress
- Reduced photosynthesis (stomatal closure).
- Reduced nutrient uptake.
- Reduced cell turgor → wilting.
- Reproductive failure (e.g., flower abortion in maize).

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet — Production Functions

| Model | Equation | Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Stewart (Linear)** | $Y = a + b \cdot \text{ET}_c$ | Approximate, for moderate water range |
| **Quadratic** | $Y = a + b \cdot \text{ET} - c \cdot \text{ET}^2$ | Captures yield decline after optimum |
| **Jensen (Multiplicative)** | $\dfrac{Y}{Y_m} = \prod_{i} \left( \dfrac{\text{ET}_i}{\text{ET}_{m,i}} \right)^{\lambda_i}$ | Stage-wise (most KTU-relevant) |
| **Minhas (Power)** | $\dfrac{Y}{Y_m} = \prod_{i} \left( \dfrac{\text{ET}_i}{\text{ET}_{m,i}} \right)^{\lambda_i}$ | Similar to Jensen |
| **Hanks (Seasonal)** | $Y = Y_m \cdot \min\left( \dfrac{\text{ET}}{\text{ET}_m} \right)$ | Limited to one limiting stage |
| **FAO-AquaCrop** | $Y = Y_x \cdot f(\text{WP}, \text{ET}_c, \text{stress})$ | Modern, KTU 2024 reference |

### 2.9.1 Parameter $\lambda_i$ — Crop Water Sensitivity Index
- $\lambda_i > 1$: stage is **highly sensitive** to water stress.
- $\lambda_i = 0$: stage is **insensitive** to water stress.
- $\lambda_i < 0$: stage actually benefits from stress (rare; e.g., sugar accumulation in sugarcane).

**Typical $\lambda$ values:**

| Crop | Vegetative | Flowering | Yield Formation | Ripening |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Maize | 0.4 | 1.5 | 0.8 | 0.2 |
| Wheat | 0.2 | 1.0 | 0.6 | 0.1 |
| Rice | 1.0 | 2.0 | 1.5 | 0.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Flowering / reproductive stages are the **most water-sensitive** for most crops. Stress during flowering can reduce yield by 30–60%.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Horton's Cumulative Infiltration

Starting from Horton's infiltration rate equation:

$$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$$

**Step 1 — Recognize that infiltration rate is the derivative of cumulative infiltration:**

$$f(t) = \dfrac{dF}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\dfrac{dF}{dt} = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate from 0 to t (with $F = 0$ at $t = 0$):**

$$dF = \left[ f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt} \right] dt$$

$$\int_0^{F} dF = \int_0^t f_c \, dt + \int_0^t (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the first integral (constant):**

$$\int_0^t f_c \, dt = f_c \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the second integral (exponential). Recall $\int e^{-kt} \, dt = -\dfrac{1}{k} e^{-kt}$:**

$$\int_0^t (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt} \, dt = (f_0 - f_c) \left[ -\dfrac{1}{k} e^{-kt} \right]_0^t$$

$$= (f_0 - f_c) \left[ -\dfrac{1}{k} e^{-kt} + \dfrac{1}{k} \right]$$

$$= \dfrac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k} \left( 1 - e^{-kt} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Combine results:**

$$\boxed{F(t) = f_c \cdot t + \dfrac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k} \left( 1 - e^{-kt} \right)}$$

This is the **KTU standard form** of cumulative infiltration under Horton's model.

**Step 7 — Sanity check at t → ∞:**

$$F(\infty) \approx f_c \cdot t + \dfrac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}$$

So the asymptotic behavior is dominated by $f_c \cdot t$, which is correct — long-term infiltration rate equals the basic infiltration rate $f_c$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Irrigation Requirement Formula

**Step 1 — Define the field water balance over the crop period:**

$$\Delta S = (P + I) - (\text{ET} + R + DP)$$

**Step 2 — At the end of the crop period, soil moisture is brought back to field capacity (standard assumption for design):**

$$\Delta S = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P + I = \text{ET} + R + DP$$

**Step 3 — Define effective rainfall $P_e = P - R - DP_{rain}$ (the part of rain stored in root zone):**

$$P_e + I = \text{ET}_c$$

**Step 4 — Solve for irrigation:**

$$I = \text{ET}_c - P_e$$

This is the **Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR)** at the field level.

**Step 5 — Introduce application efficiency** to account for losses during field application:

$$\eta_a = \dfrac{\text{Water stored in root zone}}{\text{Water delivered to field}}$$

So the **Gross Irrigation Requirement**:

$$\boxed{\text{GIR} = \dfrac{\text{NIR}}{\eta_a} = \dfrac{\text{ET}_c - P_e}{\eta_a}}$$

**Step 6 — For system-level requirement (canal + field):**

$$\boxed{\text{FIR} = \dfrac{\text{ET}_c - P_e}{\eta_c \cdot \eta_a}}$$

where $\eta_c$ = conveyance efficiency.

**Step 7 — Final volumetric form:**

$$V \,(\text{m}^3) = \dfrac{\text{GIR} \text{ (mm)} \times A \text{ (ha)} \times 10}{\eta_s}$$

where $\eta_s$ = storage / distribution efficiency.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Net & Gross Irrigation Requirement

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A wheat crop in Palakkad has seasonal $\text{ET}_c = 480$ mm and effective rainfall $P_e = 180$ mm. Application efficiency $\eta_a = 0.65$, conveyance efficiency $\eta_c = 0.80$. Compute NIR, GIR, and volume of water for 50 ha.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute NIR:**

$$\text{NIR} = \text{ET}_c - P_e = 480 - 180 = 300 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute GIR:**

$$\text{GIR} = \dfrac{\text{NIR}}{\eta_a} = \dfrac{300}{0.65} = 461.54 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute field/system irrigation requirement:**

$$\text{FIR} = \dfrac{\text{NIR}}{\eta_c \cdot \eta_a} = \dfrac{300}{0.80 \times 0.65} = \dfrac{300}{0.52} = 576.92 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute volume for 50 ha:**

$$V = 576.92 \text{ mm} \times 50 \text{ ha} \times 10 \text{ m}^3/\text{mm-ha}$$

$$V = 576.92 \times 500 = 288,461.5 \text{ m}^3 \approx 2.88 \text{ lakh m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick conversion check:** 1 mm depth over 1 ha = 10 m³. So 576.92 mm × 50 × 10 = 288,460 m³. ✅

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Infiltration Calculation (Horton's Model)

**Problem:**
A sandy loam soil has $f_0 = 80$ mm/hr, $f_c = 12$ mm/hr, $k = 0.4$ hr⁻¹. Compute (a) infiltration rate at $t = 1$ hr, (b) cumulative infiltration in 1 hour.

**Step 1 — Apply Horton's equation:**

$$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$$

$$f(1) = 12 + (80 - 12) e^{-0.4 \times 1}$$

$$f(1) = 12 + 68 \times e^{-0.4}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate exponential:**

$$e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

**Step 3 — Final calculation:**

$$f(1) = 12 + 68 \times 0.6703 = 12 + 45.58 = 57.58 \text{ mm/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative infiltration:**

$$F(1) = f_c \cdot t + \dfrac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k} (1 - e^{-kt})$$

$$F(1) = 12 \times 1 + \dfrac{68}{0.4} (1 - 0.6703)$$

$$F(1) = 12 + 170 \times 0.3297$$

$$F(1) = 12 + 56.05 = 68.05 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** In the first hour, ~68 mm of water has infiltrated. The instantaneous rate is now 57.6 mm/hr, decreasing from 80 mm/hr initially. As $t \to \infty$, $f \to f_c = 12$ mm/hr.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Yield Response using Jensen's Model

**Problem:**
Maize has 4 growth stages with $\text{ET}_{m,i}$ and $\text{ET}_a$ as given. Compute relative yield using Jensen's model.

| Stage | ET_m (mm) | ET_a (mm) | λ_i |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1. Initial | 50 | 40 | 0.4 |
| 2. Vegetative | 110 | 90 | 0.5 |
| 3. Flowering | 130 | 80 | 1.5 |
| 4. Maturity | 90 | 75 | 0.2 |

**Step 1 — Apply Jensen's multiplicative model:**

$$\dfrac{Y}{Y_m} = \prod_{i=1}^{4} \left( \dfrac{\text{ET}_{a,i}}{\text{ET}_{m,i}} \right)^{\lambda_i}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each ratio and raise to its power:**

$$\text{Stage 1: } \left( \dfrac{40}{50} \right)^{0.4} = (0.8)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 0.8} = e^{0.4 \times (-0.2231)} = e^{-0.0893} = 0.9146$$

$$\text{Stage 2: } \left( \dfrac{90}{110} \right)^{0.5} = (0.8182)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.8182} = 0.9045$$

$$\text{Stage 3: } \left( \dfrac{80}{130} \right)^{1.5} = (0.6154)^{1.5}$$

Calculate $(0.6154)^{1.5} = 0.6154 \times \sqrt{0.6154} = 0.6154 \times 0.7845 = 0.4828$

$$\text{Stage 4: } \left( \dfrac{75}{90} \right)^{0.2} = (0.8333)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln 0.8333} = e^{0.2 \times (-0.1823)} = e^{-0.0365} = 0.9642$$

**Step 3 — Multiply all terms:**

$$\dfrac{Y}{Y_m} = 0.9146 \times 0.9045 \times 0.4828 \times 0.9642$$

Compute stepwise:

$$0.9146 \times 0.9045 = 0.8273$$

$$0.8273 \times 0.4828 = 0.3994$$

$$0.3994 \times 0.9642 = 0.3851$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{Y}{Y_m} = 0.385 \text{ or } 38.5\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The crop produces only **38.5% of maximum yield** due to severe water stress, especially in the **flowering stage** (Stage 3 had the highest λ = 1.5 and the largest relative deficit). This is why protecting the flowering stage is critical.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Soil Water Balance (Weekly Computation)

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
For a tomato field with root zone depth 60 cm, FC = 28% (vol), WP = 14% (vol). Initial moisture = 22%. Show water balance for a week with the following data:

| Day | ET (mm) | Rain (mm) | Irrigation (mm) | Runoff (mm) | DP (mm) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Mon | 4.0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Tue | 4.5 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Wed | 4.0 | 20 | 0 | 5 | 8 |
| Thu | 3.5 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Fri | 4.0 | 0 | 25 | 0 | 5 |
| Sat | 4.5 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Sun | 4.0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 1 — Convert FC and WP to depth (mm) in root zone:**

$$\text{AW (mm)} = (0.28 - 0.14) \times 600 \text{ mm} = 84 \text{ mm (TAW)}$$

$$\text{FC depth} = 0.28 \times 600 = 168 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Starting storage (Day 0 = Monday morning):**

$$S_0 = 0.22 \times 600 = 132 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply daily water balance (mm):**

$$\Delta S = (P + I) - (\text{ET} + R + DP)$$

| Day | P + I | ET + R + DP | ΔS | End of Day Storage |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Mon | 0 | 4.0 | −4.0 | 128.0 |
| Tue | 0 | 4.5 | −4.5 | 123.5 |
| Wed | 20 | 4.0 + 5 + 8 = 17.0 | +3.0 | 126.5 |
| Thu | 0 | 3.5 | −3.5 | 123.0 |
| Fri | 25 | 4.0 + 0 + 5 = 9.0 | +16.0 | 139.0 |
| Sat | 0 | 4.5 | −4.5 | 134.5 |
| Sun | 0 | 4.0 | −4.0 | 130.5 |

**Step 4 — Check against FC (168 mm):**

All values are well below FC, so no drainage below root zone was needed (other than Wed and Fri DP losses, already included).

**Step 5 — Check WP (84 mm):**

All values above WP → no crop water stress occurred.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** This kind of weekly balance table is the basis of **real-time irrigation scheduling software** (e.g., CROPWAT by FAO, which KTU expects students to be aware of).

## 4.4 Irrigation Requirement Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Field Data: Crop Type, Soil, Climate] --> B[Compute ET0 using Penman-Monteith]
    B --> C[Select Kc by Growth Stage]
    C --> D[Compute ETC equals Kc times ET0]
    D --> E[Subtract Effective Rainfall Pe]
    E --> F[Add Soil Moisture Depletion]
    F --> G[Net Irrigation Requirement NIR]
    G --> H[Divide by Application Efficiency eta a]
    H --> I[Divide by Conveyance Efficiency eta c]

    K --> L[Volume of Water Required in cubic meters]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define (a) Field Capacity, (b) Permanent Wilting Point, and (c) Available Water. How are they used in irrigation scheduling?**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Field Capacity (FC):** The moisture content of soil after gravity drainage has ceased and the soil has become stable. It is the upper limit of plant-available water, typically corresponding to a soil water suction of approximately 0.33 bar (1/3 atm) in loamy soils.

**(b) Permanent Wilting Point (PWP):** The moisture content at which plants can no longer extract water from the soil and begin to wilt permanently. It corresponds to a soil water suction of about 15 bar.

**(c) Available Water (AW):** The portion of soil water that is available to the plant, defined as the difference between FC and PWP:

$$\text{AW} = \theta_{fc} - \theta_{wp}$$

**Use in irrigation scheduling:** When soil moisture depletion reaches a threshold (typically 50–70% of AW for most crops, called Management Allowed Deficit — MAD), irrigation is triggered to refill the root zone to FC.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Differentiate between Consumptive Use and Evapotranspiration. State two empirical methods for estimating ET.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Consumptive Use (Cu) | Evapotranspiration (ET) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | Total water used by the crop as ET plus water used for metabolic activities (often taken as equal to ET in practice) | Combined loss of water through evaporation from soil + transpiration from plants |
| **Scope** | Includes ET and minor metabolic use | Purely physical process |
| **Units** | mm/day or mm/season | mm/day or mm/season |

In practice, **Cu ≈ ET** and the terms are used interchangeably in engineering.

**Two empirical methods for ET estimation:**
1. **Blaney-Criddle method:** Uses mean monthly temperature and daylight hours. Suitable for humid regions. Formula: $u = K \cdot \sum (t \cdot p / 100)$ where $t$ = temperature, $p$ = daylight hours %.

2. **Hargreaves-Samani method:** Uses max, min, mean temperatures and extraterrestrial radiation. Useful for data-scarce regions. Formula: $\text{ET}_0 = 0.0023 (T_{mean} + 17.8)(T_{max} - T_{min})^{0.5} R_a$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**A.** (a) **Define Infiltration. Derive the Horton's equation for cumulative infiltration and explain each term.** (7 marks)

**(b) The following data are obtained from an infiltration test using a double-ring infiltrometer on a sandy loam soil:**

| Time (min) | Cumulative Infiltration (cm) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 |
| 5 | 2.1 |
| 15 | 4.5 |
| 30 | 7.2 |
| 60 | 10.5 |
| 90 | 13.0 |
| 120 | 15.0 |

**Using Horton's equation, determine the values of $f_0$, $f_c$, and $k$.** (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Definition:** Infiltration is the downward movement of water from the surface into the soil profile. The infiltration rate $f$ is the volume of water entering the soil per unit area per unit time, usually expressed in mm/hr or cm/hr.

**Derivation:**

Horton's equation for infiltration rate:

$$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$$

**Where:**
- $f(t)$ = infiltration rate at time $t$ (mm/hr or cm/hr)
- $f_0$ = initial infiltration rate (at $t = 0$)
- $f_c$ = final (steady-state) infiltration rate
- $k$ = decay constant (depends on soil structure, vegetation)
- $t$ = time elapsed

Since $f(t) = dF/dt$:

$$dF = [f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}] \, dt$$

Integrating from 0 to $t$:

$$F(t) = f_c \cdot t + \dfrac{f_0 - f_c}{k}(1 - e^{-kt})$$

**Physical interpretation:** At $t = 0$, $f = f_0$ (soil initially dry, high suction). As $t \to \infty$, $f \to f_c$ (soil approaches saturation under ponding).

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute infiltration rate at each time interval using $\Delta F / \Delta t$:**

| Interval (min) | $F$ (cm) | $\Delta F$ (cm) | $\Delta t$ (hr) | $f$ (cm/hr) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0–5 | 0 → 2.1 | 2.1 | 1/12 | 25.2 |
| 5–15 | 2.1 → 4.5 | 2.4 | 1/6 | 14.4 |
| 15–30 | 4.5 → 7.2 | 2.7 | 0.25 | 10.8 |
| 30–60 | 7.2 → 10.5 | 3.3 | 0.5 | 6.6 |
| 60–90 | 10.5 → 13.0 | 2.5 | 0.5 | 5.0 |
| 90–120 | 13.0 → 15.0 | 2.0 | 0.5 | 4.0 |

**Step 2 — Recognize that as $t \to \infty$, $f \to f_c$.** From the last interval, $f_c \approx 4.0$ cm/hr.

**Step 3 — Estimate $f_0$:** The first interval (0–5 min) gives $f \approx 25.2$ cm/hr, but this includes early wetting effects. Using Horton's equation extrapolation or a more refined approach:

Using the formula with two time points, e.g., $t = 0.083$ hr (5 min) and $t = 2$ hr (120 min):

$$f(0.083) = 4.0 + (f_0 - 4.0) e^{-k(0.083)} = 25.2$$

$$f(2.0) = 4.0 + (f_0 - 4.0) e^{-2k} = 4.0$$

From the second equation:

$$(f_0 - 4.0) e^{-2k} = 0 \quad \text{?}$$

This would give $f_0 = 4$ which is inconsistent. Let us use a non-trivial pair: $t = 0.5$ hr and $t = 1.5$ hr.

Approximate $f(0.5) \approx 6.6$ cm/hr (at 30 min) and $f(1.5) \approx 4.5$ cm/hr (interpolated between 90 and 120 min).

**Step 4 — Solve the system:**

$$6.6 = 4.0 + (f_0 - 4.0) e^{-0.5k} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$4.5 = 4.0 + (f_0 - 4.0) e^{-1.5k} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

From (i): $(f_0 - 4.0) e^{-0.5k} = 2.6$

From (ii): $(f_0 - 4.0) e^{-1.5k} = 0.5$

Dividing: $\dfrac{e^{-0.5k}}{e^{-1.5k}} = e^k = \dfrac{2.6}{0.5} = 5.2$

$k = \ln(5.2) = 1.648$ hr⁻¹

Substitute back: $(f_0 - 4.0) e^{-0.5 \times 1.648} = (f_0 - 4.0) e^{-0.824} = (f_0 - 4.0)(0.439) = 2.6$

$f_0 - 4.0 = 5.92 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 \approx 9.92$ cm/hr

**Step 5 — Final Results:**

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 9.9 \text{ cm/hr}, \quad f_c \approx 4.0 \text{ cm/hr}, \quad k \approx 1.65 \text{ hr}^{-1}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert time from minutes to hours before using Horton's equation (which uses $t$ in hours). They also confuse $F$ (cumulative) with $f$ (instantaneous rate). Compute $f$ first as $\Delta F/\Delta t$, then fit the equation.

---

### QUESTION B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]

**B.** (a) **Explain the concept of Crop Water Production Function. Derive Jensen's multiplicative model and state its limitations.** (7 marks)

**(b) For a wheat crop, the following data were recorded for three growth stages. Using Jensen's model, compute the relative yield.**

| Stage | ETm (mm) | ETa (mm) | λ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Vegetative | 120 | 95 | 0.4 |
| Flowering | 150 | 90 | 1.2 |
| Grain Filling | 110 | 100 | 0.6 |

**If the maximum yield $Y_m = 5000$ kg/ha, compute the actual yield $Y$.** (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Definition:** A Crop Water Production Function (CWPF) is a mathematical relationship expressing crop yield (Y) as a function of water input (Irrigation I or Crop ET, $\text{ET}_c$). It quantifies the yield response to water availability.

**Derivation of Jensen's Model:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Crop growth is divided into $n$ discrete stages.
2. Yield reduction in each stage is independent of other stages.
3. Yield response in each stage follows a power function of the ratio $\text{ET}_a / \text{ET}_m$.

For a single stage $i$, empirical evidence shows:

$$\dfrac{Y_i}{Y_{m,i}} = \left( \dfrac{\text{ET}_{a,i}}{\text{ET}_{m,i}} \right)^{\lambda_i}$$

For multiple stages (multiplicative interaction):

$$\boxed{\dfrac{Y}{Y_m} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( \dfrac{\text{ET}_{a,i}}{\text{ET}_{m,i}} \right)^{\lambda_i}}$$

where:
- $Y$ = actual yield
- $Y_m$ = maximum (potential) yield under no water stress
- $\text{ET}_{a,i}$ = actual ET in stage $i$
- $\text{ET}_{m,i}$ = maximum ET in stage $i$
- $\lambda_i$ = sensitivity index of stage $i$

**Derivation logic:** If yield is the product of sub-yields from each growth stage (independent multiplicative effects), then combining the individual power-law relationships gives the multiplicative form.

**Limitations of Jensen's Model:**
1. Requires reliable stage-wise $\text{ET}_a$ and $\text{ET}_m$ data, which are often unavailable.
2. The sensitivity index $\lambda_i$ is crop- and region-specific, needing local calibration.
3. Assumes independence of stages, which is unrealistic — late-stage stress may be compounded by early stress.
4. Does not account for **excess water** (waterlogging) conditions.
5. The multiplicative form is sensitive to small errors — a single very low ratio can drastically reduce predicted yield.

**Part (b):**

**Given:**

| Stage | ETm | ETa | λ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Vegetative | 120 | 95 | 0.4 |
| Flowering | 150 | 90 | 1.2 |
| Grain Filling | 110 | 100 | 0.6 |

**Step 1 — Compute each ratio:**

$$\text{Stage 1: } \dfrac{95}{120} = 0.7917$$

$$\text{Stage 2: } \dfrac{90}{150} = 0.6000$$

$$\text{Stage 3: } \dfrac{100}{110} = 0.9091$$

**Step 2 — Raise each to its λ:**

$$\text{Stage 1: } (0.7917)^{0.4}$$

Compute $\ln(0.7917) = -0.2336$; multiply by 0.4: $-0.0934$; exponentiate: $e^{-0.0934} = 0.9109$

$$\text{Stage 2: } (0.6)^{1.2}$$

Compute $\ln(0.6) = -0.5108$; multiply by 1.2: $-0.6130$; exponentiate: $e^{-0.6130} = 0.5418$

$$\text{Stage 3: } (0.9091)^{0.6}$$

Compute $\ln(0.9091) = -0.0953$; multiply by 0.6: $-0.0572$; exponentiate: $e^{-0.0572} = 0.9444$

**Step 3 — Multiply all terms:**

$$\dfrac{Y}{Y_m} = 0.9109 \times 0.5418 \times 0.9444$$

Step-by-step:

$$0.9109 \times 0.5418 = 0.4935$$

$$0.4935 \times 0.9444 = 0.4660$$

**Step 4 — Final relative yield:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{Y}{Y_m} = 0.466 \text{ or } 46.6\%}$$

**Step 5 — Actual yield:**

$$Y = 0.466 \times 5000 = 2330 \text{ kg/ha}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Jensen's Model Marks Loss:**
> 1. Forgetting to take $\ln$ before raising to fractional power.
> 2. Using linear interpolation of stress effects (this is Stewart's model, NOT Jensen's).
> 3. Misinterpreting $\lambda > 1$ — the higher the $\lambda$, the more sensitive the stage. In our problem, **flowering stage** with $\lambda = 1.2$ caused the largest yield drop (factor = 0.54), pulling down the overall yield significantly.
> 4. Confusing "sensitivity index" with "crop coefficient $K_c$" — they are completely different parameters.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Consolidated Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Mark Deduction in Crop Water Requirement Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing NIR and GIR** — forgetting to divide by application efficiency.
> 2. **Mixing depth (mm) and volume (m³)** without proper unit conversion. Remember: 1 mm over 1 ha = **10 m³**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert time units** in Horton's equation (must be in hours).
> 4. **Not using the correct $K_c$ stage** for the corresponding growth period.
> 5. **Jensen's model mistakes:**
>    - Using addition instead of multiplication.
>    - Skipping the logarithm step for fractional powers.
> 6. **Confusing effective rainfall** with total rainfall (always subtract runoff and deep percolation).
> 7. **Incorrect sign convention** in water balance (deposits positive, withdrawals negative).
> 8. **Forgetting to convert volumetric moisture (%) to fractional form** (e.g., 28% → 0.28) before multiplying by depth.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Infiltration** = downward entry of water into soil. **Horton's equation** is the most-tested model. Always report $f_0$, $f_c$, $k$ in consistent units (mm/hr, hr⁻¹).
- **Field Capacity (FC) ≈ 0.33 bar suction.** **Wilting Point (WP) ≈ 15 bar suction.** TAW = FC − WP (over root zone depth).
- **CWR ≈ ET for most crops.** Use **FAO-56 Penman-Monteith** as the gold standard for ET₀.
- **Crop ET:** $\text{ET}_c = K_c \times \text{ET}_0$. Know the **4 Kc stages**: Initial, Development, Mid, Late.
- **Effective Rainfall:** use USDA-SCS formula or fixed % method; never take total rainfall as effective.
- **Irrigation Requirement chain:** $\text{NIR} = \text{ET}_c - P_e$ → divide by $\eta_a$ → divide by $\eta_c$ → multiply by area × 10 → volume in m³.
- **Water Balance:** $\Delta S = (P + I + C) - (\text{ET} + R + DP + GW)$. This is the **core of irrigation scheduling**.
- **Yield Response models** (most-tested):
  - **Stewart (linear):** simple, approximate.
  - **Jensen (multiplicative):** stage-wise, most realistic, $\lambda$ = sensitivity.
- **Jensen's formula:** $\dfrac{Y}{Y_m} = \prod_i \left( \dfrac{\text{ET}_{a,i}}{\text{ET}_{m,i}} \right)^{\lambda_i}$. Highest $\lambda$ = most critical stage.
- **MAD** = $p \times$ TAW (typical $p$ = 0.3–0.7). Irrigation triggered when depletion ≥ MAD.
- **Water Use Efficiency (WUE):** $\text{WUE} = Y/\text{ET}_c$ in kg/m³. Increasing WUE is the modern target in **deficit irrigation**.
- **Conversion shortcut:** 1 mm depth over 1 hectare = **10 m³** of water. Memorize this for KTU numericals.
- **Units vigilance:** mm/day, mm/hr, mm/season for water depths; ha or m² for area; m³ for volume; hr⁻¹ for $k$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
