topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET632 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Irrigation Water Distribution: Canal Network and Canal Regulation; Methods of Distribution - Supply Based and Demand Based; Delivery of Water to Farms; Measurement of Water; Scheduling of Irrigation - Criteria and Constraints; Frequency and Interval of Irrigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Irrigation Water Distribution — Formal Definition

**Irrigation Water Distribution** is the systematic and engineered conveyance of water from the source (reservoir, river, or barrage) through a hierarchical network of canals and control structures to individual agricultural fields, ensuring that the *right quantity*, at the *right time*, and with the *right frequency* reaches the crops to satisfy their consumptive demand while minimizing conveyance and application losses.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Water distribution in irrigation engineering refers to the operational framework governing the allocation, delivery, measurement, and scheduling of irrigation water from the main canal head to the individual farm outlet, encompassing both engineering (canal network design) and managerial (regulation & scheduling) components.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The City Water Supply Model"

Imagine the irrigation system as a **municipal water supply network for an entire city**:

- The **dam/barrage** is the **water treatment plant** (source).
- The **main canal** is the **primary transmission pipeline**.
- The **branch and distributary canals** are the **district/zone mains**.
- The **minors and watercourses** are the **street-level distribution pipes**.
- The **farm outlet (mogha)** is the **household tap connection**.
- **Canal regulation** is analogous to the **valve operations and pumping schedules** maintained by the city corporation to ensure equitable supply.
- **Water measurement** is the **water meter** at each consumer end.
- **Scheduling of irrigation** is the **rationing timetable** (e.g., "water supply only between 6 AM–9 AM") that the city enforces during scarcity.

Just as a city cannot give every household unlimited water 24×7, an irrigation system must ration water among thousands of farmers based on crop needs, soil type, and available supply.

## 1.3 Core Sub-Topics at a Glance

| Sub-Topic | Engineering Purpose | Key Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Canal Network** | Physical conduit hierarchy | Conveyance efficiency |
| **Canal Regulation** | Operational control of flow | Equitable distribution |
| **Supply-Based Distribution** | Allotment from upstream | Predictable, low flexibility |
| **Demand-Based Distribution** | Allotment to match crop need | High flexibility, complex |
| **Delivery to Farms** | Last-mile conveyance via watercourse | Field application losses |
| **Measurement of Water** | Volumetric / flow rate assessment | Charging & auditing |
| **Scheduling of Irrigation** | Time-table of water release | Matching demand-supply |
| **Frequency & Interval** | How often and how long to irrigate | Soil-water-crop dynamics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The entire distribution chain is governed by the **water balance equation**: *Water In = Crop Consumptive Use + Conveyance Losses + Application Losses + Drainage/Seepage Loss + Groundwater Recharge*. Failure in any link breaks the equity of distribution.

## 1.4 Standard Units & Conventions Used in KTU Examinations

- **Discharge (Q):** expressed in **cubic metres per second (cumec or m³/s)** or **litres per second (L/s)**. **1 cumec = 1000 L/s**.
- **Area:** **hectare (ha)** where 1 ha = 10,000 m² = 2.471 acres.
- **Depth of water (Δ or d):** measured in **cm** or **mm** over the field area.
- **Volume:** **ha-m** (hectare-metre) where 1 ha-m = 10,000 m³.
- **Duty (D):** expressed as **hectares per cumec (ha/cumec)**.
- **Base period (B):** measured in **days**.
- **Frequency (f):** dimensionless number of irrigations per base period.
- **Interval (I):** measured in **days**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Canal Network — Hierarchical Anatomy

An irrigation canal network is laid out in a **funnel-down hierarchy** (decreasing discharge, increasing number of channels):

1. **Main Canal (or Carrier Channel)** — Direct off-take from the reservoir headworks. Carries the full design discharge to the command area. Usually aligned along the **contour or ridge** to command maximum area on both sides.
2. **Branch Canal** — Off-take from the main canal. Branches off at major division points called **regulators**.
3. **Distributary Canal** — Off-take from a branch. Runs along the **watershed ridge** of the sub-command. Sized typically for **5–30 cumec**.
4. **Minor Canal** — Smaller off-take from a distributary, serving a group of villages. Sized typically for **0.5–5 cumec**.
5. **Sub-Minor / Watercourse (Khal)** — The **terminal channel** that actually delivers water to individual farm outlets (**moghas**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A **mogha** is a small, fixed opening (often a pipe outlet or notched weir) at the head of a watercourse that controls the discharge entering a farmer's field. Its calibration and proper sizing are critical for equitable distribution.

### 2.1.1 Alignment Principles
- **Main canal** → aligned on the **contour** (water flows perpendicular to slope).
- **Branch & distributary** → aligned on the **watershed ridge** so that they can command areas on both sides by gravity flow.
- This arrangement minimizes cross-drainage works and pumping costs.

## 2.2 Canal Regulation — Operational Control

**Canal regulation** is the procedure of controlling the flow of water in the canal system at various control points to achieve the desired distribution pattern. It is the *heart* of irrigation management.

### 2.2.1 Types of Regulation
- **Structural Regulation:** Achieved by physical structures such as **gates, weirs, falls, cross-regulators, and head regulators**.
- **Non-Structural (Administrative) Regulation:** Achieved through **warabandi** (rotational supply), **tail-rotation**, **shejpali** (administrative rosters), and **area capacity curves**.

### 2.2.2 Cross-Regulator vs Head Regulator
- A **Head Regulator** is built at the off-take of a branch/distributary from the parent canal. It controls the **inflow** into the off-taking channel.
- A **Cross Regulator** is built across the main/branch canal downstream of an off-take. It raises the upstream water level so that the head regulator of the branch can draw its share even during low-flow periods in the parent canal.

## 2.3 Methods of Distribution — Supply Based vs Demand Based

### 2.3.1 Supply-Based (Rationing) Distribution

Water is distributed according to the **availability of supply** rather than the actual crop demand. The system is operated at a **fixed discharge**, and the area to be irrigated is decided based on the water in hand.

- **Characteristics:** Continuous flow (or fixed rotation), low operational complexity.
- **Advantage:** Simple, predictable, low administrative overhead.
- **Disadvantage:** Wastage during low-demand periods; shortage during peak demand.
- **Example:** Many Indian canal systems historically used a **full-supply level (FSL)** approach.

### 2.3.2 Demand-Based (Flexible) Distribution

Water is supplied **as and when demanded** by the farmers/crops, matching the actual consumptive use and the irrigation schedule.

- **Characteristics:** Intermittent flow, on-request supply, needs robust communication.
- **Advantage:** Highly efficient, water-saving, matches crop water requirement precisely.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires sophisticated measurement, telemetry, and trained staff; not feasible for large gravity systems with many farmers.
- **Example:** Drip-irrigated high-value crop command areas in Israel, parts of Maharashtra (e.g., PIMPRI irrigation).

### 2.3.3 Hybrid — Rotational Distribution (Warabandi)

The most prevalent Indian method. A **time-based roster (warabandi)** is prepared dividing the available flow among farmers in proportion to their land holdings. Each farmer gets the channel for a fixed duration (e.g., once every 7–14 days), with the **tail-enders getting water first** to compensate for higher seepage losses in the watercourse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Point:** The **warabandi system** is the backbone of Indian canal distribution. It is essentially a *compromise* between supply-based and demand-based methods, combining the predictability of supply-based rationing with the fairness of demand-based prioritization.

## 2.4 Delivery of Water to Farms

The **last-mile delivery** from the government-owned watercourse to the farmer's field is governed by:

1. **Outlet (Mogha) Design** — sized for a discharge proportional to the area served (typically 0.03–0.06 cumec per 10 hectares).
2. **Watercourse Conveyance** — unlined earthen channel with seepage losses of **20–40 %**.
3. **Field Channels / Field Ditches** — branch out within the farm to deliver water uniformly to each check basin or furrow.
4. **Application Methods** — border strip, furrow, basin, sprinkler, drip.

**Conveyance Efficiency (ηc)**, **Application Efficiency (ηa)**, and **Distribution Uniformity (DU)** define the quality of delivery.

## 2.5 Measurement of Water

Water measurement is essential for **equitable distribution, volumetric assessment for water charges, and auditing of losses**. Methods are classified as:

| Method | Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Weirs (Sharp-crested)** | Flow measurement | Distributary/minor head |
| **Flumes (Parshall, Cut-throat)** | Flow measurement | Free-flow situations, low head loss |
| **Orifices / Meter gates** | Flow measurement | Mogha / farm outlet |
| **Current meters** | Velocity measurement | Large canals, rivers |
| **Floats** | Approximate velocity | Quick field estimates |
| **Volumetric tanks** | Small flows | Research plots, drip systems |
| **Pipe-flow meters** | Pressure-pipe flow | Pumped systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rehbock weir** and **Parshall flume** are the most widely used standard measurement devices in modern KTU-level problems.

## 2.6 Scheduling of Irrigation

**Irrigation scheduling** is the decision-making process of determining **when** to irrigate and **how much** water to apply. It bridges the soil-water-crop continuum.

### 2.6.1 Criteria for Scheduling
- **Soil moisture status** — irrigate when soil moisture depletes to ~50 % of available water.
- **Climatic approach (IW/CPE ratio)** — cumulative pan evaporation based, popular in India.
- **Critical stage approach** — irrigate at sensitive phenological stages (e.g., flowering, grain-filling).
- **Plant indicator approach** — visible wilting symptoms.

### 2.6.2 Constraints
- **Water availability constraint** — limited reservoir storage.
- **Conveyance capacity constraint** — channel cannot carry peak demand.
- **Labour & energy constraint** — night irrigation, pump hours.
- **Soil & topographic constraint** — infiltration rate, slope.
- **Economic & institutional constraint** — water rates, governance.

## 2.7 Frequency and Interval of Irrigation

- **Frequency of Irrigation (f)** = Total depth of water required during base period ÷ Depth of water applied per irrigation.
- **Interval between Irrigations (I)** = Base period (B) ÷ Number of irrigations (N).
- **Alternatively:** I = Readily Available Soil Moisture (RAM) ÷ Daily Consumptive Use (CU).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always write the **units** in the final answer. *Frequency* is a pure number; *Interval* must be expressed in **days**.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume of water required | $V = \dfrac{\Delta \times A}{100}$ | $\Delta$ in cm, $A$ in hectares, $V$ in ha-m |
| 2 | Volume in cubic metres | $V_{\text{m}^3} = 10000 \times \Delta_{\text{m}} \times A_{\text{ha}}$ | $\Delta$ in metres, $A$ in ha |
| 3 | Duty–Delta relationship | $\Delta = \dfrac{8.64 \times B}{D}$ | $\Delta$ in cm, $B$ in days, $D$ in ha/cumec |
| 4 | Base period from duty | $B = \dfrac{\Delta \times D}{8.64}$ | same units as above |
| 5 | Discharge required | $Q = \dfrac{A \times \Delta}{8.64 \times B}$ | $Q$ in cumec, $A$ in ha |
| 6 | Frequency of irrigation | $f = \dfrac{\Delta_{\text{total}}}{d_{\text{net}}}$ | dimensionless; $\Delta_{\text{total}}$ over base period, $d_{\text{net}}$ depth per irrigation |
| 7 | Interval between irrigations | $I = \dfrac{B}{f} = \dfrac{\text{RAM}}{\text{CU}_{\text{day}}}$ | $I$ in days |
| 8 | Rectangular weir discharge | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m |
| 9 | V-notch (triangular) weir | $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} C_d \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m |
| 10 | Cipolletti weir | $Q = 1.84 \, L \, H^{3/2}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, English practice |
| 11 | Parshall flume (free flow) | $Q = 2.2 \, W \, H^{1.55}$ (FPS) | $W$ throat width in ft, $Q$ in cfs |
| 12 | Orifice discharge | $Q = C_d \, a \, \sqrt{2g h}$ | $h$ = head over orifice centre |
| 13 | IW/CPE ratio scheduling | $d = (\text{IW/CPE}) \times \text{CPE}_{\text{cum}}$ | $d$ = depth of irrigation water |
| 14 | Conveyance efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{W_{\text{delivered}}}{W_{\text{diverted}}} \times 100$ | percent |
| 15 | Application efficiency | $\eta_a = \dfrac{W_{\text{stored in root zone}}}{W_{\text{delivered to field}}} \times 100$ | percent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Conversion:** **1 cumec-day = 8.64 hectare-metres = 86,400 m³**. This factor is the single most important constant in KTU irrigation numerics.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

These principles drive the design of every major Indian irrigation project — from the **Indira Gandhi Canal (Rajasthan)** with its supply-based warabandi system, to the **PIMPRI demand-based drip command (Maharashtra)**. Globally, Israel’s **Netafim demand-based drip scheduling** saves over 50 % water compared to surface flooding. Modern systems integrate **IoT soil-moisture sensors, SCADA-regulated gates, and satellite-based IW/CPE modeling** — all rooted in the same fundamental duty-delta-frequency relationships covered in this module.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Duty–Delta–Base Period Relationship (The Holy Trinity of Irrigation)

### 3.1.1 Starting Concept
- **Duty (D):** the area (in hectares) that can be irrigated by a continuous discharge of **1 cumec** running for the entire base period.
- **Delta (Δ):** the total depth of water (in cm) required by the crop over its base period.
- **Base Period (B):** the time (in days) from first irrigation to the last (or to crop maturity).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider **1 cumec** of water flowing continuously for a duration equal to the base period $B$ (in days).

**Step 1 — Compute total volume supplied:**
A discharge of 1 cumec means 1 m³/s. Over $B$ days, the total seconds = $B \times 24 \times 3600 = 86400 \, B$ seconds.

$$V_{\text{supplied}} = 1 \times 86400 \, B = 86400 \, B \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Express volume in hectare-metres (ha-m):**
Since $1 \text{ ha-m} = 10000 \text{ m}^3$:

$$V_{\text{supplied}} = \dfrac{86400 \, B}{10000} = 8.64 \, B \;\; \text{ha-m}$$

**Step 3 — Area irrigated by definition of duty = D hectares:**
So, the volume that covers area $D$ ha to depth $\Delta$ m is:

$$V_{\text{required}} = D \times \Delta \;\; \text{ha-m} \quad (\text{with } \Delta \text{ in metres})$$

**Step 4 — Equate supply and demand (assuming 100 % efficiency for the fundamental relationship):**

$$D \times \Delta = 8.64 \, B \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta = \dfrac{8.64 \, B}{D} \;\; \text{(metres)}$$

**Step 5 — Convert delta from metres to centimetres (the conventional KTU unit):**
Since $1 \text{ m} = 100 \text{ cm}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{8.64 \times B_{\text{days}}}{D_{\text{ha/cumec}}}}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for duty or base period in alternative forms:**

$$D = \dfrac{8.64 \, B}{\Delta} \quad \text{and} \quad B = \dfrac{\Delta \, D}{8.64}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU irrigation papers. Memorize the constant **8.64** with its unit: *ha-m per cumec-day*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frequency and Interval from Soil-Water Balance

### 3.2.1 Conceptual Setup
Let a crop require a total depth $\Delta_{\text{total}}$ (cm) over a base period of $B$ days. Each irrigation applies a **net depth** $d_{\text{net}}$ (cm) to the root zone.

**Step 1 — Number of irrigations (frequency):**

$$f = \dfrac{\Delta_{\text{total}}}{d_{\text{net}}}$$

**Step 2 — Interval (time between two successive irrigations):**

$$I = \dfrac{B}{f} = \dfrac{B \times d_{\text{net}}}{\Delta_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 3 — Soil-moisture-based alternative derivation:**
The Readily Available Moisture (RAM) is the portion of soil water that plants can extract without stress. Let $w_{\text{RAM}}$ be the depth-equivalent of RAM in mm. Let $C_u$ be the daily consumptive use in mm/day.

$$I = \dfrac{w_{\text{RAM}}}{C_u}$$

This formula is often used in KTU problems involving **field-capacity, wilting-point, and root-zone depth** data.

**Step 4 — Quantitative example worked out:**
Suppose a wheat crop has:
- Base period $B = 120$ days
- Total delta $\Delta_{\text{total}} = 45$ cm
- Net depth per irrigation $d_{\text{net}} = 7.5$ cm
- Readily available moisture $w_{\text{RAM}} = 60$ mm
- Daily consumptive use $C_u = 5$ mm/day

**Method 1:**
$$f = \dfrac{45}{7.5} = 6 \text{ irrigations}$$
$$I = \dfrac{120}{6} = 20 \text{ days}$$

**Method 2 (soil-moisture check):**
$$I = \dfrac{60 \text{ mm}}{5 \text{ mm/day}} = 12 \text{ days}$$

The discrepancy indicates that the **net depth of 7.5 cm per irrigation is supplying more water than just the RAM**, i.e., there is some deep percolation or over-irrigation. The KTU board answer would highlight this trade-off.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rectangular Weir Discharge (for Water Measurement)

A sharp-crested rectangular weir is one of the most common measurement devices in KTU problems.

**Step 1 — Idealized velocity through a strip at depth h below the free surface:**
By Torricelli's law, the theoretical velocity is:
$$v = \sqrt{2 g h}$$

**Step 2 — Discharge through an elemental horizontal strip of area $L \cdot dh$:**

$$dQ = v \cdot L \cdot dh = L \sqrt{2g} \, h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from the crest ($h=0$) to the upstream head ($h=H$):**

$$Q_{\text{ideal}} = L \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh = L \sqrt{2g} \left[ \dfrac{2}{3} h^{3/2} \right]_0^H = \dfrac{2}{3} L \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce a discharge coefficient $C_d$ (typically 0.62 to 0.66 for sharp-crested weirs) to account for the velocity of approach, contraction, and viscous effects:**

$$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \, C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$

For $C_d = 0.62$ and using $g = 9.81$ m/s², the constant $\tfrac{2}{3} C_d \sqrt{2g} \approx 1.84$ in FPS units, yielding the engineering formula:

$$Q_{\text{cfs}} = 1.84 \, L_{\text{ft}} \, H_{\text{ft}}^{3/2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Problems commonly give weir dimensions and ask for discharge. Always state the assumed $C_d$ value, otherwise marks are deducted.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — V-Notch (Triangular) Weir

A V-notch weir is preferred for **small discharges** because of higher accuracy at low flows.

**Step 1 — Elemental strip width at depth h:**
At a height $h$ above the vertex, the width of the elemental strip in a V-notch of half-angle $\theta/2$ is:

$$b = 2 \, h \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Discharge through the strip:**

$$dQ = b \cdot \sqrt{2g h} \cdot dh = 2 \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, h^{3/2} \, dh$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $0$ to $H$:**

$$Q_{\text{ideal}} = 2 \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H h^{3/2} \, dh = 2 \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \cdot \dfrac{2}{5} H^{5/2}$$

$$Q_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{4}{5} \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply $C_d$ (typically 0.58–0.62 for V-notch):**

$$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{8}{15} C_d \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $H^{5/2}$ for V-notch and $H^{3/2}$ for rectangular?** Because the V-notch is a *variable-width* device — the area exposed to flow increases linearly with depth, raising the moment-arm dependence from 1.5 to 2.5.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Orifice Discharge (Mogha Sizing)

A pipe outlet (mogha) in a canal acts essentially as a **submerged orifice**.

$$Q = C_d \, a \, \sqrt{2 g h}$$

where:
- $a$ = area of the orifice (m²)
- $h$ = effective head causing flow (m)
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (0.60 to 0.65 for submerged orifices)

**Mogha Sizing Example (KTU-style):**
A distributary is to deliver 0.05 cumec to a 10-hectare chak. Design the orifice if the head difference between water level in the distributary and the watercourse is 0.20 m.

$$a = \dfrac{Q}{C_d \sqrt{2 g h}} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.62 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.20}} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.62 \times 1.981} = \dfrac{0.05}{1.228} \approx 0.0407 \text{ m}^2$$

If a circular orifice is used, diameter $D = \sqrt{4a/\pi} = \sqrt{4 \times 0.0407 / 3.1416} \approx 0.228 \text{ m} = 228 \text{ mm}$.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Chart of Distribution Methods

| Attribute | Supply-Based | Demand-Based | Warabandi (Rotational) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Discharge type** | Continuous or fixed rotation | Intermittent, on-demand | Time-rotation, intermittent |
| **Flexibility** | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Equity** | Reasonable | Excellent (if well-managed) | Good (tail-rotation bias) |
| **Water-saving** | Low (excess at times) | High | Moderate |
| **Operational complexity** | Low | Very high | Moderate |
| **Measurement need** | Basic | High (real-time SCADA) | Basic to moderate |
| **Suitability** | Large gravity systems | Compact high-value cropping | Indian subcontinent norm |
| **KTU exam frequency** | Common | Common | Most common |

## 3.7 Worked-Out Numerical — Combined Application

**Problem:** A canal command area of **4000 hectares** grows wheat with base period **120 days**. The duty of wheat at the head of the watercourse is **900 ha/cumec**. Seepage losses in the watercourse are **25 %**. Determine: (a) the total volume of water required, (b) the discharge required at the head of the distributary, and (c) the average frequency of irrigation if 6 cm is the net depth per irrigation.

**Step (a) — Total volume (delta calculation):**
$$\Delta = \dfrac{8.64 \times B}{D} = \dfrac{8.64 \times 120}{900} = \dfrac{1036.8}{900} = 1.152 \text{ m} = 115.2 \text{ cm}$$

Total volume = $\Delta \times A$ in ha-m:
$$V = 1.152 \times 4000 = 4608 \text{ ha-m} = 4.608 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step (b) — Discharge at distributary head (after losses):**
Effective duty at the farm = 900 ha/cumec. To deliver at the farm, the head discharge must compensate 25 % seepage.

$$D_{\text{head}} = D_{\text{farm}} \times (1 - \text{loss fraction}) = 900 \times 0.75 = 675 \text{ ha/cumec}$$

$$Q_{\text{head}} = \dfrac{A}{D_{\text{head}}} = \dfrac{4000}{675} = 5.93 \text{ cumec}$$

**Step (c) — Frequency of irrigation:**
$$f = \dfrac{\Delta_{\text{total (cm)}}}{d_{\text{net (cm)}}} = \dfrac{115.2}{6} = 19.2 \approx 19 \text{ irrigations}$$

This means approximately **19 irrigations** over 120 days, i.e., one irrigation every **120/19 ≈ 6.3 days**.

## 4.1 Canal Network Hierarchy (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sourceA[Reservoir or Barrage Headworks] --> mainChnlA[Main Canal FSL controlled]
    mainChnlA --> xr1A{Cross Regulator No 1}
    xr1A --> brn1A[Branch Canal North]
    xr1A --> maincontA[Main Canal Continues South]
    maincontA --> xr2A{Cross Regulator No 2}
    xr2A --> brn2A[Branch Canal South]
    brn1A --> hr1A[Head Regulator for Distributary 1]
    brn1A --> hr2A[Head Regulator for Distributary 2]
    hr1A --> dist1A[Distributary Canal D1]
    hr2A --> dist2A[Distributary Canal D2]
    dist1A --> min1A[Minor Canal M1]
    dist1A --> min2A[Minor Canal M2]
    min1A --> wc1A[Watercourse Khal 1]
    min1A --> wc2A[Watercourse Khal 2]
    min2A --> wc3A[Watercourse Khal 3]
    wc1A --> mog1A[Mogha Outlet Farm A]
    wc1A --> mog2A[Mogha Outlet Farm B]
    wc2A --> mog3A[Mogha Outlet Farm C]
    wc3A --> mog4A[Mogha Outlet Farm D]
    mog1A --> field1A[Field A Application]
    mog2A --> field2A[Field B Application]
    mog3A --> field3A[Field C Application]
    mog4A --> field4A[Field D Application]
    xr1A:::regulatorNode
    xr2A:::regulatorNode
    hr1A:::regulatorNode
    hr2A:::regulatorNode
    classDef regulatorNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **yellow diamond nodes** are **regulators** (cross or head). All other rectangles are canal segments, with arrows indicating the direction of flow. Discharge reduces progressively as flow is split at every off-take.

## 4.2 Water Distribution Logic — Warabandi Rotational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startStep[Start of Warabandi Week] --> listFarmers[Compile List of all Farmers in Watercourse]
    listFarmers --> sortTailFirst[Sort List Tail-end Farmer First]
    sortTailFirst --> allocateTime[Allocate Time Proportional to Land Holding]
    allocateTime --> notifyFarmers[Notify All Farmers by Roster Display]
    notifyFarmers --> releaseWater[Release Water from Mogha]
    releaseWater --> flowWater[Water Flows Down Watercourse]
    flowWater --> farm1Take[Farm 1 Takes Share in Allotted Time]
    farm1Take --> farm2Take[Farm 2 Takes Share Next]
    farm2Take --> farmNTake[Farm N Takes Share Last at Head]
    farmNTake --> closeMogha[Close Mogha for Next Cycle]
    closeMogha --> waitInterval[Wait for Interval Period]
    waitInterval --> startStep
    releaseWater:::waterNode
    farm1Take:::farmNode
    farm2Take:::farmNode
    farmNTake:::farmNode
    classDef waterNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000
    classDef farmNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Scheduling Decision Logic — IW/CPE Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    obsStart[Observe Daily Pan Evaporation CPE in mm] --> cumCPE[Compute Cumulative CPE since Last Irrigation]
    cumCPE --> ratioCheck{Is Cumulative CPE equal to or greater than Target IW divided by IW-CPE Ratio?}
    ratioCheck --> noAction[No irrigation needed yet, continue monitoring]
    noAction --> obsStart
    ratioCheck --> yesIrrigate[Trigger Irrigation Event]
    yesIrrigate --> applyDepth[Apply Net Depth equal to IW to the Field]
    applyDepth --> recordDate[Record Date and Depth in Irrigation Log]
    recordDate --> resetCPE[Reset Cumulative CPE Counter to Zero]
    resetCPE --> obsStart
    yesIrrigate:::triggerNode
    applyDepth:::triggerNode
    classDef triggerNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Supply Based vs Demand Based

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph supplySide[Supply Based System]
        supSrc[Reservoir Fixed Discharge Q] --> supChan[Main Canal Always Full FSL]
        supChan --> supDiv[Distributaries Run Continuously]
        supDiv --> supField[Farmer Uses What Comes, May Waste or Store]
    end
    subgraph demandSide[Demand Based System]
        demSrc[Reservoir Variable Pumping or Gate Operation] --> demChan[Main Canal Flow Modulated by SCADA]
        demChan --> demDiv[Distributary Delivers Only on Request]
        demDiv --> demField[Farmer Requests When Crop Needs, Precise Volume]
    end
    supField:::lossNode
    demField:::saveNode
    classDef lossNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef saveNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Block Architecture of a Measurement Station (Parshall Flume)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    upChan[Upstream Channel at Flume Inlet] --> convSec[Converging Section Length 2 by 1 ratio]
    convSec --> throatSec[Throat Section of Width W, level floor]
    throatSec --> divSec[Diverging Section Length 3 by 1 ratio mild slope]
    divSec --> downChan[Downstream Channel]
    throatSec --> gaugePt[Staff Gauge at 2 by 3 W upstream of throat]
    gaugePt --> readH[Read Head H in metres]
    readH --> applyEq[Apply Q equals 2.2 W H to 1.55 FPS]
    applyEq --> outDischarge[Compute Discharge in cumec]
    applyEq:::calcNode
    outDischarge:::calcNode
    classDef calcNode fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the terms: (i) Duty, (ii) Delta, and (iii) Base Period as used in irrigation engineering. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Duty:** The area (in hectares) that can be irrigated by a continuous supply of **1 cumec** of water running for the entire base period. It is expressed in **ha/cumec**.
- **Delta (Δ):** The **total depth of water** (in cm or m) required by a crop to mature, supplied over the entire base period. It is the **total consumptive use** plus field application losses.
- **Base Period (B):** The time period (in **days**) from the **first watering** of a crop at sowing to its **last watering** before harvest.

**Relationship:**
$$\Delta_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{8.64 \times B_{\text{days}}}{D_{\text{ha/cumec}}}$$

This is derived by equating the volume supplied by 1 cumec in $B$ days ($86400 \, B$ m³ $= 8.64 B$ ha-m) to the volume required to irrigate area $D$ ha to depth $\Delta$ m ($D \times \Delta$ ha-m), then converting $\Delta$ to cm.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Supply-Based and Demand-Based methods of irrigation water distribution.**

**Model Answer:**

| Attribute | Supply-Based Distribution | Demand-Based Distribution |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating philosophy** | Water is distributed as per **available supply** | Water is supplied as per **crop demand** |
| **Discharge pattern** | Continuous or fixed rotation | Intermittent, on-request |
| **Flexibility** | Low | High |
| **Water-use efficiency** | Low (wastage during low demand) | High (matches actual need) |
| **Operational complexity** | Simple | Requires SCADA / telemetry |
| **Suitability** | Large gravity-fed canal systems | Compact, high-value cropping |
| **Examples in India** | Most traditional canal systems (warabandi variant) | PIMPRI command (Maharashtra), pilot drip blocks |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]
**(a) Describe the hierarchical canal network in a typical Indian irrigation project. Explain the functions of head regulators and cross regulators with neat sketches. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A distributary carries a discharge of 5 cumec at its head. It serves a gross command area of 5000 hectares. The effective rainfall contribution is 4 cm per month and the crop consumptive use is 12 cm per month over a base period of 100 days. Seepage loss in the watercourse is 20 % and the field application efficiency is 70 %. Determine: (i) the net duty at the head, (ii) the volume of water to be diverted at the distributary head, and (iii) the frequency of irrigation if the net depth per irrigation is 6 cm. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Canal Network Hierarchy (top-down):**

1. **Main Canal** — Carries water from the barrage/reservoir headworks. Aligned along the **contour** to command both sides.
2. **Branch Canal** — Off-take from the main canal at a major division point.
3. **Distributary Canal** — Off-take from a branch; runs along the **watershed ridge**. Discharge usually 0.5–30 cumec.
4. **Minor Canal** — Smaller off-take serving a cluster of 2–5 villages.
5. **Sub-Minor / Watercourse (Khal)** — The **terminal channel**, typically 1–3 km long, unlined, and carries the water finally to the farm outlet (mogha).

**Head Regulator:**
- Built at the **off-take point** of a branch/distributary from the parent canal.
- Controls the **inflow** into the off-taking channel.
- Prevents excessive drawal that might starve downstream users.
- Equipped with vertical lift gates and sometimes a silt-excluder upstream.

**Cross Regulator:**
- Built **across the parent canal** (downstream of an off-take).
- Raises the upstream water level to ensure the head regulator of the off-take can draw its **share** even when the parent canal is running at low discharge.
- Maintains the **full-supply level (FSL)** in the parent canal upstream.

**Sketch (textual):**

```
                |--- Head Regulator ---[ Distributary continues down ]
Parent Canal ===|====X (Cross Regulator)==========================
                |   (raises upstream water level)
                |--- Distributary branches off
```

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given Data:**
- $Q_{\text{head}} = 5$ cumec
- $A = 5000$ ha
- Rainfall $P_{\text{eff}} = 4$ cm/month
- Consumptive use $C_u = 12$ cm/month
- Base period $B = 100$ days
- Seepage loss = 20 % → Conveyance efficiency $\eta_c = 80 \% = 0.80$
- Field application efficiency $\eta_a = 70 \% = 0.70$

**Step 1 — Net consumptive use (delta) over the base period:**

$$\Delta_{\text{net}} = C_u - P_{\text{eff}} = 12 - 4 = 8 \text{ cm/month}$$

For 100 days $\approx 3.33$ months:

$$\Delta_{\text{net,total}} = 8 \times \dfrac{100}{30} = 8 \times 3.333 = 26.67 \text{ cm} = 0.2667 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Volume required at the field (gross delta adjusted for application efficiency):**

$$\Delta_{\text{gross at field}} = \dfrac{\Delta_{\text{net,total}}}{\eta_a} = \dfrac{26.67}{0.70} = 38.10 \text{ cm} = 0.3810 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Effective duty at the mogha (farm):**

$$D_{\text{farm}} = \dfrac{8.64 \times B}{\Delta_{\text{farm}}} = \dfrac{8.64 \times 100}{38.10} = \dfrac{864}{38.10} \approx 22.68 \text{ ha/cumec}$$

**Step 4 — Duty at the distributary head (compensating seepage):**

$$D_{\text{head}} = D_{\text{farm}} \times \eta_c = 22.68 \times 0.80 = 18.14 \text{ ha/cumec}$$

**Step 5 — Verify discharge:**

$$Q = \dfrac{A}{D_{\text{head}}} = \dfrac{5000}{18.14} = 275.6 \text{ cumec}$$

**The given discharge of 5 cumec is insufficient to irrigate the entire 5000 ha under the stated crop water demand.** This suggests the canal is intended for partial irrigation, or the base period and crop demand assumptions need revision — a key discussion point for the 7-mark allocation.

**Step 6 — Frequency of irrigation:**

$$f = \dfrac{\Delta_{\text{gross at field (cm)}}}{d_{\text{net (cm)}}} = \dfrac{38.10}{6} = 6.35 \approx 7 \text{ irrigations}$$

So, roughly **7 irrigations** over 100 days, one every **$\approx 14$ days**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – July 2024]
**(a) What is meant by irrigation scheduling? Explain the IW/CPE ratio method of scheduling with its criteria and constraints. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A V-notch weir with a 90° angle is installed in a watercourse. The head over the vertex is observed to be 25 cm. If the coefficient of discharge is 0.60, determine the discharge through the weir. If the same discharge is to be measured using a rectangular weir with a coefficient of discharge of 0.62, find the head over the rectangular weir of crest length 0.50 m. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Definition:**
**Irrigation scheduling** is the decision of **when to irrigate** and **how much water to apply** to a crop, based on soil-moisture, climate, and crop-growth conditions, to optimize yield and water-use efficiency.

**IW/CPE Ratio Method (Pruitt, popular in Indian agriculture):**
- **IW** = Irrigation Water depth to be applied (in mm).
- **CPE** = Cumulative Pan Evaporation since the previous irrigation (in mm).
- **IW/CPE ratio** is a crop-specific constant (e.g., wheat ≈ 1.0, rice ≈ 1.5, cotton ≈ 0.75).
- **When CPE reaches the value of IW / (IW/CPE), the next irrigation is triggered.**

**Example:** For wheat with IW = 60 mm and IW/CPE = 1.0, irrigate when CPE = 60 mm.

**Criteria for Scheduling:**
1. Soil moisture status (irrigate at 50 % depletion of available water).
2. Crop critical stages (flowering, grain-filling for wheat; knee-high for sugarcane).
3. Climatic approach (IW/CPE, Penman ET-based).
4. Plant-based indicators (visible wilting).

**Constraints:**
1. Limited water supply from reservoir.
2. Conveyance capacity of canals (cannot meet peak simultaneous demand).
3. Labour availability (night irrigation, festivals).
4. Soil and topographic limitations (infiltration, slope, waterlogging risk).
5. Economic (water charges, electricity for pumps).
6. Institutional/governance issues (warabandi roster conflicts).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Discharge through the 90° V-notch weir:**

Given: $\theta = 90°$, $H = 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$, $C_d = 0.60$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$

$$\tan(45°) = 1.0$$
$$H^{5/2} = (0.25)^{2.5} = (0.25)^2 \times \sqrt{0.25} = 0.0625 \times 0.5 = 0.03125 \text{ m}^{5/2}$$
$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \times 0.60 \times 1.0 \times 4.429 \times 0.03125$$

$$Q = 0.5333 \times 4.429 \times 0.03125$$

$$Q = 2.3619 \times 0.03125 = 0.0738 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 73.8 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 2 — Find head over rectangular weir of length 0.50 m:**

For the rectangular weir:
$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$$

Given: $Q = 0.0738$ m³/s, $C_d = 0.62$, $L = 0.50$ m.

$$0.0738 = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.50 \times 4.429 \times H^{3/2}$$

$$0.0738 = 0.4133 \times 4.429 \times H^{3/2} = 1.8305 \, H^{3/2}$$

$$H^{3/2} = \dfrac{0.0738}{1.8305} = 0.0403$$

$$H = (0.0403)^{2/3}$$

Using logarithms:
$$\log(0.0403) = -1.3945$$
$$\tfrac{2}{3} \times (-1.3945) = -0.9297$$
$$H = 10^{-0.9297} = 0.1176 \text{ m} \approx 11.76 \text{ cm}$$

**Verification:** $H^{3/2} = (0.1176)^{1.5} = 0.1176 \times \sqrt{0.1176} = 0.1176 \times 0.3430 = 0.0403$ ✓

**Result:** The rectangular weir of crest length 0.50 m requires a head of **≈ 11.76 cm** to pass the same discharge as the 90° V-notch weir at 25 cm head.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the coefficient $C_d$** while writing the weir formula → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** between $H$ (cm vs m) and $Q$ (cumec vs L/s) → **−1 mark** minimum, often leads to numerical chaos.
> 3. **Using $\tan(\theta)$ instead of $\tan(\theta/2)$** in the V-notch formula → **−2 marks**, this is a *favourite trap*.
> 4. **In scheduling problems**, failing to distinguish between *net* and *gross* delta → **−3 marks**.
> 5. **In duty-delta problems**, using $B$ in years or months instead of days → entire answer becomes 30× wrong → **lose all 7 marks**.
> 6. **Confusing "frequency" with "interval"** — frequency is a *number* (dimensionless), interval is a *time* (days).
> 7. **Skipping the assumptions** (e.g., $C_d$ value, $g = 9.81$ m/s²) → examiner may deduct **1 mark per missing assumption**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Duty = area irrigated per unit discharge** (ha/cumec); **Delta = total depth** (cm); **Base period = time** (days).
- [x] **Master formula:** $\Delta_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{8.64 \times B}{D}$ — the holy trinity of irrigation.
- [x] **Conversion constants:** 1 cumec-day = 8.64 ha-m = 86,400 m³; 1 ha = 10,000 m².
- [x] **Warabandi = rotational time-based rationing**, tail-enders first, in proportion to land holding.
- [x] **Supply-based = push system** (water is pushed by availability); **Demand-based = pull system** (water is pulled by need).
- [x] **Head regulator** controls inflow into off-taking channel; **Cross regulator** maintains FSL in parent canal.
- [x] **Rectangular weir:** $Q = \tfrac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ — best for medium to large flows.
- [x] **V-notch weir:** $Q = \tfrac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$ — best for small flows.
- [x] **Parshall flume:** Free-flow formula $Q = 2.2 W H^{1.55}$ in FPS — preferred for low-head-loss measurement.
- [x] **IW/CPE ratio scheduling:** trigger irrigation when cumulative CPE reaches IW ÷ (IW/CPE ratio).
- [x] **Frequency** $f = \Delta_{\text{total}} / d_{\text{net}}$ (dimensionless); **Interval** $I = B / f$ or $I = \text{RAM}/C_u$ (in days).
- [x] **Conveyance efficiency (ηc)** = water delivered / water diverted; **Application efficiency (ηa)** = water stored in root zone / water delivered.
- [x] **Conjunctive use** of surface and groundwater is implicitly assumed in modern scheduling.
- [x] **Equitable distribution** is the central goal of regulation — a question often asked in KTU board exams for 2-3 marks definition.
- [x] **Last-mile delivery** losses can be **20–40 %** in unlined watercourses — a hot KTU topic for management-related questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systems Classification: Rehabilitation and Modernization, Performance Indicators, Improving System Performance, and Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET632 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Systems Classification: Rehabilitation and Modernization, Performance Indicators, Improving System Performance, and Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Systems Classification of Irrigation Projects

### Formal Academic Definition
An **Irrigation System** is a planned and engineered hydraulic infrastructure that captures, conveys, regulates, distributes, and applies water from a source (river, reservoir, well, or canal) to agricultural fields to compensate for the deficit in effective rainfall. The **classification of systems** establishes the typology under which engineering design, command area allocation, water delivery scheduling, and performance evaluation are structured.

The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) and the Ministry of Water Resources (MoWR) of India classify irrigation projects administratively based on **Culturable Command Area (CCA)**. Internationally, the FAO, IWMI, and USBR classify systems based on hydraulic delivery, source type, and management responsibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 2, the classification focuses on operational and managerial typology relevant to performance evaluation and R&M interventions — *not* just administrative size.

### Standard Administrative Classification (India)

| Basis | Class | CCA Range |
|---|---|---|
| By Area (CCA) | Minor Irrigation Project | < 2,000 ha |
| By Area (CCA) | Medium Irrigation Project | 2,000 – 10,000 ha |
| By Area (CCA) | Major Irrigation Project | > 10,000 ha |

### Hydraulic & Operational Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Typology** used in Performance Evaluation Frameworks (Alegre, IWMI):

- **Diversion (Run-of-River) Systems** — water drawn directly from a river by a barrage/weir; no storage.
- **Storage (Reservoir) Systems** — water impounded upstream and released as per crop demand.
- **Lift (Pump) Systems** — water lifted mechanically to a higher elevation.
- **Gravity-Flow Systems** — water conveyed by gravitational gradient through canals.
- **Pressurized Systems** — Drip and sprinkler networks, typically for high-value crops.
- **Direct (Tank-fed) and Indirect (Canal-fed) Systems** — tank = local storage; canal = regional network.
- **Public (Government) and Private (Farmer-owned) Systems** — varies by ownership and O&M responsibility.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of an irrigation system like the **plumbing of a large apartment building**:
- The **reservoir/river** is the municipal water tank.
- The **main canal** is the building's riser pipe.
- The **distributaries and minors** are the floor-level branch pipes.
- The **watercourse (field channel)** is the pipe entering your individual flat.
- The **farmers** are the end-users (apartments).

If the tank leaks (conveyance loss), the floor valves are stuck (poor regulation), or the pipes have corrosion (aging infrastructure), every flat receives either *too much, too little, or no water at the wrong time*. **Rehabilitation** is like fixing the leaks and replacing corroded pipes back to original working condition. **Modernization** is like installing automatic pressure-balancing valves and smart meters — going *beyond* the original design to deliver better, more equitable service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric:** The **1 cumec (cubic metre per second)** of water sustained for 1 day = **86,400 m³/day** = **86.4 ha·m/day** (assuming a unit duty of 1 ha·m per cumec-day for planning estimates). This conversion is the bedrock of Indian irrigation engineering calculations.

---

## 1.2 Rehabilitation and Modernization (R&M)

### Definitions
- **Rehabilitation:** The process of restoring an irrigation and drainage system to its *originally designed* functional condition, or to a condition where it can perform reliably to its *originally intended* level of service. It typically involves **structural repairs** (lining cracks, replacing gates, desilting canals) and is essentially **defensive** in nature.
- **Modernization:** The process of upgrading and improving an irrigation system through **managerial, technical, and institutional changes** to enhance water delivery service, productivity, and sustainability, *going beyond* the original design capacity. It incorporates **automation, demand-based scheduling, GIS/GPS monitoring, and farmer participation**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **40-year-old car**:
- **Rehabilitation** = engine overhaul, brake replacement, body denting-painting. The car runs again, but it's still a 40-year-old design.
- **Modernization** = installing a touchscreen infotainment, ABS, hybrid drivetrain, and adaptive cruise control. The car is now *better than new*.

> [!NOTE]
> In irrigation engineering, R&M is often written as **"R&M"** to indicate that rehabilitation *without* modernization is a half-measure that does not address fundamental design or management flaws.

### Need for R&M
1. **Aging infrastructure** — most Indian major/medium projects are 50–100+ years old.
2. **Siltation of reservoirs and canals** — reduces live storage and section area.
3. **System performance degradation** — conveyance efficiency drops to 30–40% in many Indian systems (versus 70% design).
4. **Increasing water scarcity** — demands more efficient use of available water.
5. **Farmer dissatisfaction** — tail-enders often get < 30% of the water allocated.
6. **Climate change adaptation** — variable rainfall patterns require flexible delivery.

---

## 1.3 Performance Indicators

### Definition
**Performance Indicators (PIs)** are quantitative metrics derived from measurable physical, hydrological, agricultural, and economic parameters that describe *how well* an irrigation system is functioning relative to its intended objectives.

Performance assessment is broadly classified into:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Framework (Alegre et al., FAO, 2000):**
> - **Internal Indicators** — system-internal hydraulic and service performance.
> - **External Indicators** — outcomes outside the system (agricultural, economic, environmental, social).

### Key Internal Performance Indicators
1. **Water Delivery Performance**
   - **Adequacy (PA)** — is enough water delivered?
   - **Efficiency (PEff)** — is water used productively?
   - **Equity (PE)** — is water shared fairly?
   - **Reliability (PR)** — is the supply dependable?
   - **Timeliness / Flexibility (PT)** — can delivery be tailored to demand?
2. **Operational Efficiency**
   - Conveyance Efficiency, Application Efficiency, Distribution Uniformity
3. **Infrastructure Condition** — physical state of structures and channels.

### Key External Performance Indicators
1. **Agricultural Productivity** — yield (kg/ha), cropping intensity (%).
2. **Economic Performance** — output/input ratio, gross/net farm income, IRR.
3. **Environmental Performance** — water table depth, soil salinity, GHG emissions.
4. **Social Indicators** — beneficiary satisfaction, equity of water access.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **hospital's ICU**:
- **Internal indicators** = patient's vital signs (BP, SpO₂, heart rate) — measurable inside the ICU.
- **External indicators** = patient's long-term recovery, return to work, quality of life — outcomes beyond the ICU.
- An ICU can have perfect vitals (good internal performance) but the patient may still not recover (poor external performance) due to underlying disease. Similarly, good hydraulic performance does not guarantee high agricultural productivity if extension services, market access, or soil health are poor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Global Benchmark:** The **IWMI (International Water Management Institute)** Performance Assessment Framework and the **MASSCOTE** (Mapping System and Services for Canal Operation Techniques) methodology developed by FAO are the most widely referenced international benchmarks for irrigation performance assessment.

---

## 1.4 Improving System Performance

Performance improvement is the *purpose* of R&M and is achieved through a **layered set of interventions**:
1. **Technical/Engineering** — canal lining, automation, SCADA, modernized outlets.
2. **Managerial** — rotational water distribution (warabandi), volumetric supply, irrigation scheduling.
3. **Institutional** — Water Users' Associations (WUAs), Participatory Irrigation Management (PIM).
4. **Agronomic** — crop choice, deficit irrigation, precision farming.
5. **Policy/Regulatory** — water pricing, allocation rules, conjunctive use mandates.

---

## 1.5 Constraints in Performance Improvement

Constraints are the **barriers — physical, financial, institutional, social, legal, or environmental** — that limit the extent, speed, or effectiveness of performance improvement interventions.

Categories:
- **Physical / Technical** — topography, soil, silt load, infrastructure rigidity.
- **Financial** — capital cost of R&M, O&M budget shortfalls, low cost recovery.
- **Institutional / Governance** — fragmented agencies, weak PIM, low accountability.
- **Social / Cultural** — resistance to change, entrenched free-rider behavior.
- **Policy / Legal** — outdated irrigation acts, unclear water rights, political interference.
- **Environmental** — ecological flows, water quality, climate variability.
- **Knowledge / Capacity** — shortage of trained staff, poor data systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework for Performance Indicators

The modern theory of irrigation performance rests on the **service-oriented paradigm** (Alegre & Pereira, FAO, 2000). It states that the *purpose* of an irrigation system is to deliver a **service** to users — not merely to deliver a *volume* of water. Service is multi-dimensional and is decomposed into:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service Dimensions (Alegre Framework):**
> 1. **Quantity** → Adequacy
> 2. **Quality** → Salinity/Sediment
> 3. **Reliability** → Temporal dependability
> 4. **Equity** → Spatial fairness
> 5. **Timeliness / Flexibility** → Match with crop demand
> 6. **Continuity** → Duration of supply
> 7. **Efficiency** → Avoiding waste

### 2.1.1 Adequacy (PA)
- **Definition:** Ratio of *actual water delivered* to *water required by crops*, expressed at a chosen level (system, project, distributary, outlet).
- **Range:** 0 to ≥ 1 (theoretically capped at 1.0 for analysis).
- **Interpretation:** PA < 1 = under-supply (deficit); PA > 1 = over-supply (surplus/waste).
- **Why it matters:** Under-supply stresses crops; over-supply causes waterlogging and salinity.

### 2.1.2 Equity (PE)
- **Definition:** The degree to which water is distributed *fairly* across users along a canal.
- **Most common metric:** Based on the **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** of discharges at outlets along a distributary.
- **Why it matters:** Low equity → head-reach users get excess, tail-reach users go dry → social conflict and reduced project benefits.

### 2.1.3 Reliability (PR)
- **Definition:** Probability (or temporal consistency) that the system delivers the *expected* quantity at the *expected* time, season after season.
- **Measured as:** 1 minus the CV of seasonal deliveries over multiple years.
- **Why it matters:** Farmers invest in irrigated agriculture only if supply is dependable.

### 2.1.4 Timeliness (PT)
- **Definition:** The match between *actual* and *desired* timing of water delivery.
- **Why it matters:** A crop at flowering stage cannot wait for water.

### 2.1.5 Efficiency (PEff)
- **Definition:** The fraction of *diverted* water that is *productively used* by the crop.
- **Sub-components:** Conveyance Efficiency (Ec), Application Efficiency (Ea), Distribution Uniformity (DU).

---

## 2.2 Performance Indicator Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are standard, internationally accepted (IWMI / FAO / ASCE / CIDA references) and form the **core of KTU exam answers** on this topic.

| # | Indicator | Formula (Mathematical Expression) | Variable Meaning | Unit / Range | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conveyance Efficiency (Ec) | $E_c = \dfrac{V_{outlet}}{V_{source}} \times 100$ | $V_{outlet}$ = volume at outlet; $V_{source}$ = volume diverted at head | % (40–80) | Standard Hyd. Eng. |
| 2 | Application Efficiency (Ea) | $E_a = \dfrac{V_{rootzone}}{V_{field}} \times 100$ | $V_{rootzone}$ = water stored in root zone; $V_{field}$ = water delivered to field | % (50–80) | ASCE |
| 3 | Water Distribution Uniformity (DU) | $DU = \dfrac{\bar{d}_{lq}}{\bar{d}} \times 100$ | $\bar{d}_{lq}$ = avg depth in lowest quarter; $\bar{d}$ = avg depth | % | Kruse (1978) |
| 4 | Christiansen Coefficient of Uniformity (CU) | $CU = \left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert}{n \bar{d}}\right) \times 100$ | $d_i$ = depth at $i$-th point; $n$ = number of points | % | Christiansen (1942) |
| 5 | Adequacy (PA) | $P_A = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{required}} \times 100$ | $Q_{actual}$ = supplied; $Q_{required}$ = demanded | % | Molden & Gates (1990) |
| 6 | Equity / CIDA (PE) | $P_E = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \min\!\left(\dfrac{Q_i}{Q_{req,i}}, 1\right)$ — tail-to-head ratio variant: $P_E = \dfrac{Q_{25\%}}{Q_{75\%}}$ | $Q_i$ = supply at outlet i; $Q_{req,i}$ = demand at i | dimensionless | CIDA (1987) |
| 7 | Reliability (PR) | $P_R = 1 - CV_Q$ where $CV_Q = \dfrac{\sigma_Q}{\bar{Q}}$ | $\sigma_Q$ = std. dev. of supply; $\bar{Q}$ = mean supply | dimensionless (0–1) | IWMI |
| 8 | Relative Water Supply (RWS) | $RWS = \dfrac{\text{Total Water Supply}}{\text{Crop Demand}}$ | Supply and demand in volume/depth | dimensionless | Perry (1996) |
| 9 | Overall Project Efficiency (Ep) | $E_p = E_c \times E_a \times 100^{-1}$ | Composite of conveyance and application | % | Standard |
| 10 | Irrigation Ratio (IR) | $IR = \dfrac{\text{Area actually irrigated}}{\text{Designed CCA}} \times 100$ | Measures utilization | % | Standard |
| 11 | Cropping Intensity (CI) | $CI = \dfrac{\text{Total cropped area}}{\text{CCA}} \times 100$ | Measures intensity of land use | % (>100 possible) | Agricultural Stat. |
| 12 | Water Productivity (WP) | $WP = \dfrac{\text{Yield (kg)}}{\text{ET_c (m}^3\text{)}}$ | kg per m³ | kg/m³ | IWMI |
| 13 | Sustainability Index (SI) | $SI = f(\text{env.}, \text{econ.}, \text{social indicators})$ | Composite qualitative index | dimensionless | Various |
| 14 | Internal Rate of Return (IRR) | NPV $= 0$ from $\sum \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | $B_t$ = benefit, $C_t$ = cost at year t | % | Economics |
| 15 | Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum PV(Benefits)}{\sum PV(Costs)}$ | Discounted values | dimensionless | Economics |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often confuse **CU (Christiansen)** with **DU (Keller)**. Remember: **CU uses all observed values** (n points), **DU uses only the lowest 25% of values**.

---

## 2.3 Rehabilitation vs Modernization — Comparative Theory

| Aspect | Rehabilitation | Modernization |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Restore original function | Improve function beyond original |
| **Philosophy** | Defensive / "catch-up" | Proactive / "leapfrog" |
| **Focus** | Physical structures | Physical + Managerial + Institutional |
| **Time horizon** | Short-to-medium (1–5 yr) | Medium-to-long (5–20 yr) |
| **Cost** | Lower (CAPEX only) | Higher (CAPEX + training + reforms) |
| **Tools** | Desilting, lining, gate repair | Automation, SCADA, GIS, WUAs, PIM |
| **Outcome** | Recovers design capacity | Achieves new service levels |
| **Performance gains** | Marginal | Substantial (2–5× yield gains reported) |
| **Risk** | Low | Higher (institutional resistance) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite Question:** "Differentiate between Rehabilitation and Modernization with examples." This is a *guaranteed* short-answer (3 marks) on most boards.

---

## 2.4 Improving System Performance — Theoretical Pillars

The performance improvement theory is built on **four mutually reinforcing pillars**:

1. **Pillar 1 — Infrastructure Modernization**
   - Canal lining (HDPE, PCC, geomembranes)
   - Cross-regulators, modernized outlets (e.g., adjustable proportional modules, Neyrpic, Crump-de Gruyter)
   - Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (**SCADA**)
   - GIS-based canal network mapping
   - Automated gates and pumps
   - Water measurement structures (Parshall flumes, cut-throat flumes)

2. **Pillar 2 — Water Management Reform**
   - Volumetric supply instead of area-based
   - Demand-driven delivery instead of supply-driven
   - Warabandi / rotational distribution
   - Deficit irrigation strategies
   - Real-time scheduling (DSS — Decision Support Systems)

3. **Pillar 3 — Institutional Reform**
   - **PIM** (Participatory Irrigation Management) — government policy of India since the 1990s.
   - **WUAs** (Water Users' Associations) at outlet/minor level.
   - Turnover of O&M responsibility to farmers.
   - Transparent water accounting and conflict resolution mechanisms.

4. **Pillar 4 — Agronomic and Demand Management**
   - Crop diversification to low-water crops.
   - Laser land levelling.
   - Mulching, deficit irrigation.
   - Conjunctive use of surface and groundwater.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why all four pillars?** The literature consistently shows that **physical R&M alone** yields < 20% of the potential performance gain. **Combined interventions** (PIM + modernization + agronomy) yield 60–80% of potential gain. This is the central lesson of the World Bank's Irrigation Sector Review studies.

---

## 2.5 Constraints — Theoretical Categories

The constraints literature (Molden et al., 2007; IWMI; FAO) classifies barriers into **eight categories**:

1. **Technical / Physical** — topography, soil permeability, siltation, infrastructure rigidity.
2. **Financial / Economic** — insufficient O&M budget, low water charges, low BCR for marginal upgrades.
3. **Institutional** — fragmented mandates (irrigation dept, WRD, agriculture dept), staff shortage, weak PIM.
4. **Policy / Legal** — outdated acts (e.g., 19th-century Canal Acts), unclear entitlements.
5. **Social / Cultural** — entrenched tail-end deprivation, gender inequity, free-rider attitudes.
6. **Knowledge / Information** — poor monitoring, lack of data, no real-time feedback.
7. **Environmental** — pollution of source, climate change, falling water tables, salinity.
8. **Political** — populist free water, political interference in O&M appointments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Hook:** The constraints are not isolated — they **interact** (e.g., low water charges → financial constraint → low O&M → infrastructure decay → technical constraint → poor performance → social unrest → political constraint). This is the **"Vicious Cycle of Irrigation Underperformance"** — a high-yield diagram for the exam.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Government of India** uses these indicators in the **Command Area Development & Water Management (CADWM)** program.
- **World Bank** projects (e.g., Andhra Pradesh Water Sector Improvement, Haryana Water Resources Consolidation) use these indicators in **Monitoring & Evaluation (M&E)**.
- **WUAs** use simple versions (e.g., flow at outlet vs. flow at head) for **bottom-up water budgeting**.
- **International benchmarking** allows comparison between systems in India, China, Morocco, Mexico, and Australia.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU)

### 3.1.1 Statement
For $n$ observation points in a field, let $d_i$ be the depth of water infiltrated (mm) at point $i$ and $\bar{d}$ be the mean depth. The **Christiansen Coefficient of Uniformity (CU)** is the most widely used measure of how uniform the application depth is.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the Mean Depth.**

The arithmetic mean of the observed infiltration depths:

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i
$$

This is the *expected* or *average* depth of water applied across the field. The mean is a simple, unbiased estimator of central tendency for $n$ equal-area observations.

**Step 2 — Define the Absolute Deviation.**

For each observation point, the absolute deviation of the observed depth from the mean is:

$$
\delta_i = \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert
$$

The absolute value is used (not the square) because Christiansen's original 1942 formulation uses a linear penalty — the *sum of absolute deviations*. This makes CU more sensitive to **systematic deviations** (a single dry spot pulls CU down more than the mean square error would).

**Step 3 — Sum the Absolute Deviations.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert
$$

This total represents the *cumulative* departure of all observations from the mean. If all depths were identical (perfect uniformity), this sum would be **zero**.

**Step 4 — Compute the Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD).**

Dividing the total deviation by the number of points gives the per-point average deviation:

$$
MAD = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert
$$

This normalizes the sum so that CU is independent of sample size.

**Step 5 — Express the MAD as a Fraction of the Mean.**

Dividing the MAD by the mean depth gives a dimensionless **relative deviation**:

$$
\text{Relative MAD} = \frac{MAD}{\bar{d}} = \frac{1}{n\bar{d}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert
$$

This represents the *average percentage by which observations deviate from the mean*.

**Step 6 — Define Uniformity as 1 minus the Relative MAD.**

A field with **zero deviation** has Relative MAD = 0, i.e., *perfect* uniformity. The complement to 1 is therefore the *uniformity*:

$$
CU_{\text{fraction}} = 1 - \frac{1}{n\bar{d}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert
$$

**Step 7 — Express as a Percentage.**

Multiplying by 100 gives the standard percentage form:

$$
\boxed{CU = \left( 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert}{n \bar{d}} \right) \times 100}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A sprinkler irrigation system applies the following depths (mm) at 8 observation points: 18, 20, 22, 19, 24, 17, 21, 23. Calculate CU.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum d_i &= 18 + 20 + 22 + 19 + 24 + 17 + 21 + 23 = 164 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\bar{d} &= \frac{164}{8} = 20.5 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\sum \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert &= \vert 18-20.5 \vert + \vert 20-20.5 \vert + \vert 22-20.5 \vert + \vert 19-20.5 \vert + \vert 24-20.5 \vert + \vert 17-20.5 \vert + \vert 21-20.5 \vert + \vert 23-20.5 \vert \\[4pt]
&= 2.5 + 0.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 3.5 + 3.5 + 0.5 + 2.5 = 16.0 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
CU &= \left( 1 - \frac{16.0}{8 \times 20.5} \right) \times 100 = \left( 1 - \frac{16.0}{164.0} \right) \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= (1 - 0.0976) \times 100 = 90.24\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A CU of 90.24% indicates *good* sprinkler uniformity (typical design value is ≥ 85% for line-source sprinklers).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Distribution Uniformity (DU) from a 16-Grid Observation

### 3.2.1 Statement
The **Distribution Uniformity (DU)** proposed by Keller & Bliesner (1990) uses the *lowest one-quarter* of observations, not the full set, to assess how well the driest portion of the field is irrigated. This is more critical because crop stress is governed by the *worst-irrigated* sub-area, not the average.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Rank Observations.**

Sort all observed depths $d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n$ in **ascending order** (smallest first):

$$
d_{(1)} \leq d_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq d_{(n)}
$$

The ascending order is essential — we want the **lowest 25%** of values.

**Step 2 — Identify the Lowest Quarter.**

For $n$ observations, the lowest quarter consists of the first $\frac{n}{4}$ points. For example:
- $n = 16$ → lowest 4 points ($d_{(1)}, d_{(2)}, d_{(3)}, d_{(4)}$)
- $n = 20$ → lowest 5 points
- $n = 12$ → lowest 3 points

**Step 3 — Compute the Mean of the Lowest Quarter.**

$$
\bar{d}_{lq} = \frac{4}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n/4} d_{(i)}
$$

The factor $\frac{4}{n}$ arises because the number of points in the lowest quarter is $\frac{n}{4}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the Mean of All Observations.**

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i
$$

**Step 5 — Form the Ratio.**

The distribution uniformity is the ratio of the *lowest quarter mean* to the *overall mean*:

$$
\boxed{DU = \frac{\bar{d}_{lq}}{\bar{d}} \times 100}
$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** 16 catch-can observations (mm) from a sprinkler test, sorted ascending:
2.1, 2.3, 2.4, 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 3.0, 3.1, 3.2, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, 3.6, 3.7

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{d}_{lq} &= \frac{2.1 + 2.3 + 2.4 + 2.5}{4} = \frac{9.3}{4} = 2.325 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\sum_{i=1}^{16} d_i &= \frac{16 \times (2.1 + 3.7)}{2} = 8 \times 5.8 = 46.4 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\bar{d} &= \frac{46.4}{16} = 2.9 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
DU &= \frac{2.325}{2.9} \times 100 = 80.17\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A DU of 80% means the lowest quarter of the field receives only 80% of the average depth — the driest corners are under-irrigated by ~20%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conveyance Efficiency from Measured Discharges

### 3.3.1 Statement
The **conveyance efficiency** of a canal reach is the ratio of water volume (or integrated discharge) reaching the tail of the reach to the volume (or integrated discharge) entering the head. Seepage, evaporation, and operational losses account for the difference.

### 3.3.2 Derivation for a Steady-State Reach

**Step 1 — Define Inflow and Outflow.**

Let $Q_{in}$ = discharge at the head of the reach (m³/s), $Q_{out}$ = discharge at the tail of the reach (m³/s), measured over a time interval $T$ (seconds).

**Step 2 — Express Volumes.**

Volumes entering and leaving are:

$$
V_{in} = Q_{in} \times T \qquad V_{out} = Q_{out} \times T
$$

**Step 3 — Express Losses.**

The loss (seepage + evaporation + operational spillage) is:

$$
V_{loss} = V_{in} - V_{out} = (Q_{in} - Q_{out}) \times T
$$

**Step 4 — Define Efficiency.**

Efficiency is the *useful* fraction (the water that successfully reaches the next downstream user):

$$
\boxed{E_c = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100}
$$

For a non-steady reach, integrate over time:

$$
E_c = \frac{\int_0^T Q_{out}(t)\, dt}{\int_0^T Q_{in}(t)\, dt} \times 100
$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Example
**Problem:** A 4 km unlined canal reach carries 2.50 cumec at the head. After 24 hours of observation, the integrated outflow at the tail is 1.75 cumec (assumed steady). The seepage loss coefficient is to be computed as percentage loss per km.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_c &= \frac{1.75}{2.50} \times 100 = 70\% \\[4pt]
\text{Total loss} &= 30\% \text{ over } 4 \text{ km} \\[4pt]
\text{Loss per km} &= \frac{30}{4} = 7.5\%/\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comment:** A loss of 7.5% per km is *very high* and confirms the urgent need for lining. Standard unlined losses in Indian alluvial canals are 1–3% per km.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equity Index Using Coefficient of Variation

### 3.4.1 Statement
The **Equity (PE)** of water distribution along a canal is most commonly measured using the coefficient of variation of discharges received at outlets. Low CV → high equity.

### 3.4.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Record Outlet Discharges.**

Let $Q_i$ = discharge (L/s) actually delivered at outlet $i$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ along a distributary.

**Step 2 — Compute the Mean.**

$$
\bar{Q} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} Q_i
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Standard Deviation.**

$$
\sigma_Q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (Q_i - \bar{Q})^2}
$$

The $(N-1)$ denominator is **Bessel's correction** for an unbiased estimator of population standard deviation from a sample.

**Step 4 — Compute the Coefficient of Variation.**

$$
CV_Q = \frac{\sigma_Q}{\bar{Q}}
$$

**Step 5 — Define Equity.**

A CV of 0 implies perfect equity; larger CV implies greater inequity. The equity index is therefore the complement:

$$
\boxed{P_E = 1 - CV_Q}
$$

(Alternatively, the **CIDA** equity formula: $P_E = 1 - \dfrac{\sigma_Q}{\bar{Q}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}}$)

### 3.4.3 Worked Example
**Problem:** A distributary has 5 outlets. Designed discharge: 100, 80, 60, 50, 40 L/s (head to tail). Actual delivered: 130, 95, 60, 35, 20 L/s. Compute Equity (PE).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q} &= \frac{130 + 95 + 60 + 35 + 20}{5} = \frac{340}{5} = 68 \text{ L/s} \\[4pt]
\sigma_Q &= \sqrt{\frac{(62)^2 + (27)^2 + (-8)^2 + (-33)^2 + (-48)^2}{4}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{3844 + 729 + 64 + 1089 + 2304}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{8030}{4}} = \sqrt{2007.5} = 44.81 \text{ L/s} \\[4pt]
CV_Q &= \frac{44.81}{68} = 0.659 \\[4pt]
P_E &= 1 - 0.659 = 0.341
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** $P_E = 0.34$ indicates **very poor equity** (head-reach users receive 6.5× the discharge of tail-reach users). This distributary urgently needs re-regulation or proportional outlets.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Reliability Index from Multi-Year Data

### 3.5.1 Statement
**Reliability (PR)** is the temporal consistency of supply. It is measured as 1 minus the CV of deliveries over $m$ irrigation seasons at a single outlet (or the whole system).

### 3.5.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Record Multi-Year Deliveries.**

Let $S_j$ = water supplied in year/season $j$ for $j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$.

**Step 2 — Compute Mean and Standard Deviation.**

$$
\bar{S} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} S_j \qquad \sigma_S = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m-1} \sum_{j=1}^{m} (S_j - \bar{S})^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the CV of Supply.**

$$
CV_S = \frac{\sigma_S}{\bar{S}}
$$

**Step 4 — Define Reliability.**

$$
\boxed{P_R = 1 - CV_S}
$$

### 3.5.3 Worked Example
**Problem:** Seasonal water supply (Mm³) at a system outlet over 6 years: 12, 14, 10, 15, 11, 13. Find Reliability.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{S} &= \frac{12+14+10+15+11+13}{6} = \frac{75}{6} = 12.5 \text{ Mm}^3 \\[4pt]
\sigma_S &= \sqrt{\frac{(-0.5)^2 + (1.5)^2 + (-2.5)^2 + (2.5)^2 + (-1.5)^2 + (0.5)^2}{5}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.25 + 2.25 + 6.25 + 6.25 + 2.25 + 0.25}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{17.5}{5}} = \sqrt{3.5} = 1.871 \text{ Mm}^3 \\[4pt]
CV_S &= \frac{1.871}{12.5} = 0.1497 \\[4pt]
P_R &= 1 - 0.1497 = 0.8503
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A reliability of 0.85 means the system delivers a *fairly* consistent volume year-on-year, with ~15% temporal variation. Most Indian systems score 0.50–0.75.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Overall Project Efficiency (Composite)

### 3.6.1 Statement
The **Overall Project Efficiency (Ep)** is the product of the sequential efficiencies of water transport from source to root zone.

### 3.6.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify Sequential Stages.**

Water flows through:
- Main canal → Conveyance Efficiency $E_c$
- Distributary/Minor → Conveyance Efficiency $E_{cd}$
- Watercourse → Conveyance Efficiency $E_{cw}$
- Field application → Application Efficiency $E_a$

**Step 2 — Apply Sequential Multiplication.**

If $E_1$ is the fraction of source water reaching stage 2, and $E_2$ is the fraction reaching stage 3, then the *combined* efficiency is the product:

$$
E_p = E_1 \times E_2 \times \cdots \times E_n
$$

For the standard 4-stage model:

$$
\boxed{E_p = E_c \times E_{cd} \times E_{cw} \times E_a}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Percentage.**

Often each efficiency is expressed as a fraction (e.g., 0.7), then multiplied and converted to a percentage:

$$
E_p\,(\%) = (E_c \times E_{cd} \times E_{cw} \times E_a) \times 100
$$

### 3.6.3 Worked Example
**Problem:** A canal system has the following: Main canal Ec = 85%, Distributary Ecd = 90%, Watercourse Ecw = 92%, Application Ea = 65%. Find overall project efficiency.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_p &= 0.85 \times 0.90 \times 0.92 \times 0.65 \\[4pt]
&= 0.85 \times 0.90 = 0.765 \\[4pt]
&= 0.765 \times 0.92 = 0.7038 \\[4pt]
&= 0.7038 \times 0.65 = 0.4575 \\[4pt]
E_p &= 45.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Only **45.75%** of the water diverted at the source reaches the crop root zone. This is typical of unmodernized Indian systems and underscores the need for R&M.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — R&M Approaches

| Approach | Capital Cost | Time to Implement | Yield Gain | Sustainability Risk | Recommended When |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lining only** | Medium | 2–3 yr | 5–10% | Low | Seepage losses are dominant |
| **Lining + outlets** | High | 3–5 yr | 10–20% | Medium | Water-guzzler system, equity poor |
| **SCADA + automation** | Very High | 5–7 yr | 15–30% | Medium | Large system, urban water competition |
| **PIM + WUA** | Low | 2–5 yr | 10–25% | Low (if PIM is genuine) | System has low cost recovery |
| **Deficit irrigation** | Very Low | Immediate | –5% to +10% (per drop basis) | High agronomic | Water-scarce basins |
| **Crop diversification** | Low | 3–5 yr | Variable | Medium | Market access available |
| **Conjunctive use** | High | 5–10 yr | 20–40% | Medium | Aquifer is healthy |
| **Integrated R+M+PIM+Agro** | Very High | 5–10 yr | 30–60% | Low | Donor / World Bank supported |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "How to improve system performance?" — *do not* list only technical fixes. A complete answer **must include institutional, managerial, and agronomic components** to score full marks.
