# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Boundary Conditions in a Seepage Domain

The diagram below maps out how each boundary condition interfaces with the governing Laplace equation inside the 2D seepage domain under a typical hydraulic structure (e.g., an earth dam or concrete weir on a permeable foundation).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["2D Seepage Domain D in Soil Medium"] --> B["Governing PDE inside D"]
    B["Governing PDE inside D"] --> C["Laplace Equation: d2h/dx2 + d2h/dy2 = 0"]
    A --> D["Boundary Surfaces of D"]
    D --> D1["Boundary 1: Impervious Base"]
    D --> D2["Boundary 2: Plane of Symmetry (if any)"]
    D --> D3["Boundary 3: Free Water Surface / Phreatic Line"]
    D --> D4["Boundary 4: Upstream Reservoir Face"]
    D --> D5["Boundary 5: Downstream Reservoir Face"]
    D --> D6["Boundary 6: Seepage Face on Downstream Slope"]
    D1 --> E1["Neumann: dh/dn = 0  -->  Zero normal flux"]
    D2 --> E2["Neumann: dh/dn = 0  -->  Mirror symmetry, same as impervious"]
    D3 --> E3["Dirichlet: h = y  -->  p = 0, streamline"]
    D4 --> E4["Dirichlet: h = H1  -->  Water at rest, equipotential"]
    D5 --> E5["Dirichlet: h = H2  -->  Water at rest, equipotential"]
    D6 --> E6["Dirichlet: h = y  -->  p = 0, same as free surface"]
    E1 --> F["Uniqueness of Solution"]
    E2 --> F["Uniqueness of Solution"]
    E3 --> F["Uniqueness of Solution"]
    E4 --> F["Uniqueness of Solution"]
    E5 --> F["Uniqueness of Solution"]
    E6 --> F["Uniqueness of Solution"]
    F --> G["Output: Head Field h(x,y), Pressure Field p(x,y), Flow Net, Exit Gradient"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Cross-Section — An Earth Dam on Permeable Foundation

The diagram below illustrates where each of the five boundary conditions is physically applied on a typical earth dam section.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Upstream Reservoir]
        U["h = H1 (water at rest, Dirichlet)"]
    end
    subgraph A2[Earth Dam Body]
        P["Phreatic line / Free water surface: h = y, Dirichlet"]
        S["Seepage face on downstream slope: h = y, Dirichlet"]
    end
    subgraph A3[Downstream Reservoir]
        D["h = H2 (water at rest, Dirichlet)"]
    end
    subgraph A4[Foundation Soil]
        I["Impervious base: dh/dn = 0, Neumann"]
        SY["Plane of symmetry (if any): dh/dn = 0, Neumann"]
    end
    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    A2 --- A4
```

## 4.3 Boundary Condition Classification Flowchart

Use this compact decision tree to identify which boundary condition applies in a given seepage problem:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{"Is the boundary surface a SOLID material that NO water can cross?"}
    Q -- YES --> A1["Impervious Layer"]
    Q -- NO --> Q2{"Is the surface a MIRROR PLANE of geometric & hydraulic symmetry?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> A2["Plane of Symmetry"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"Does the boundary have atmospheric pressure p = 0?"}
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{"Is the water essentially STATIC beyond the boundary?"}
    Q4 -- YES --> A4["Water at Rest"]
    Q4 -- NO --> A5["Re-evaluate: unknown or moving water boundary"]
    Q3 -- YES --> Q5{"Is the location of the surface ITSELF unknown a priori?"}
    Q5 -- YES --> A3["Free Water Surface (phreatic)"]
    Q5 -- NO --> A6["Seepage Surface (known geometry)"]
    A1 --> R["Apply dh/dn = 0 (Neumann)"]
    A2 --> R
    A3 --> R2["Apply h = y (Dirichlet)"]
    A4 --> R3["Apply h = h0 constant (Dirichlet)"]
    A6 --> R2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Q: State and explain the boundary conditions applicable at (i) an impervious layer and (ii) a free water surface in a 2D seepage problem governed by the Laplace equation.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Impervious Layer:** No water flows across this boundary, so the normal component of Darcy velocity must vanish.

$$v_n = -K \frac{\partial h}{\partial n} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial h}{\partial n} = 0$$

This is a **Neumann (second-type)** boundary condition. Physically, the impervious surface itself is a **streamline**, and the **equipotential lines run parallel** to it.

**(ii) Free Water Surface:** The pore-water pressure is atmospheric ($p = 0$, gauge). Therefore, from $h = p/\gamma_w + y$:

$$h = y$$

This is a **Dirichlet (first-type)** boundary condition. The free water surface is also a streamline; the location of the surface is *not known a priori* and must be determined as part of the solution (a non-linear problem).

**(3 marks: 1 for statement of impervious, 1 for derivation/equation, 1 for free surface.)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Q: Differentiate between a free water surface and a seepage surface. Why are both expressed by the same equation $h = y$?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Free Water Surface (Phreatic Line) | Seepage Surface |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Geometric location** | Inside the flow domain — position is **unknown** initially | A **known, fixed** physical boundary (e.g., downstream slope of dam) |
| **Determination** | Must be found as part of the solution | Pre-defined by the structure geometry |
| **Physical extent** | Forms the upper limit of the saturated flow region | The portion of the downstream face through which water exits |

**Why the same equation?** Both are surfaces on which the pore-water pressure equals atmospheric pressure ($p = 0$, gauge). Using $h = p/\gamma_w + y$, we get $h = y$ on both. The difference lies in *how* the boundary is treated in the numerical/analytical solution, not in the local constitutive equation.

**(3 marks: 1.5 for difference, 1.5 for justification of same equation.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Problems (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch of an earth dam resting on a permeable foundation of finite depth, identify and label all the typical boundary conditions used to solve the Laplace equation for steady-state seepage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A concrete weir is founded on a 6 m thick permeable soil layer underlain by an impervious stratum. Upstream head = 5 m, downstream head = 1 m. The structure is symmetric about its centerline. By applying the five typical boundary conditions, set up the mathematical problem that determines the head distribution $h(x, y)$ in the foundation soil. Clearly state the PDE and the conditions on each boundary. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question Choice A

**(a) Identification of Boundary Conditions on an Earth Dam (7 marks):**

A 2D cross-section of an earth dam on a permeable foundation has the following boundary segments:

**B1 — Upstream submerged face (entry face):** The soil-reservoir interface on the upstream side, where water from the reservoir enters the soil. Since the reservoir is essentially at rest, this is a **Dirichlet boundary** with $h = H_1$ (constant head equal to upstream water depth).

**B2 — Impervious base (bottom of permeable layer):** The soil-bedrock interface. Water cannot flow into the bedrock, so this is a **Neumann boundary** with $\partial h / \partial n = 0$.

**B3 — Phreatic line / Free water surface (top streamline inside the dam):** The upper boundary of the saturated zone inside the dam body, where $p = 0$. This is a **Dirichlet boundary** with $h = y$. Its position is **unknown** a priori and must be solved iteratively.

**B4 — Seepage face (downstream slope above tail water):** The portion of the downstream slope between the point where the phreatic line emerges and the tail-water level. Water seeps out at atmospheric pressure, so this is a **Dirichlet boundary** with $h = y$ (location of this surface is fixed by the geometry of the downstream slope).

**B5 — Downstream submerged face (entry/exit face below tail water):** Where the downstream slope meets the tail-water region. Since the tail water is essentially static, this is a **Dirichlet boundary** with $h = H_2$ (constant head equal to downstream water depth).

**B6 — Plane of symmetry (if applicable):** For a dam with symmetric cross-section, the vertical centerline is a **plane of symmetry** with $\partial h / \partial n = 0$ (Neumann). This allows the analyst to model only half the domain, reducing computational effort.

**Mark distribution (7 marks):** Sketch (2 marks) + 5 boundary labels with correct equations (1 mark each = 5 marks).

**(b) Mathematical Setup of the Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Governing PDE (1 mark):** Inside the permeable foundation (a 2D domain $D$):

$$\frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \text{inside } D$$

**Step 2 — Domain specification (1 mark):** Let the origin be at the center of the impervious base, directly below the centerline of the weir. The foundation occupies $0 \le x \le L$ (where $L$ is the half-width of the structure, by symmetry) and $0 \le y \le D$ where $D = 6$ m. (For full width, $-L \le x \le L$.)

**Step 3 — Boundary conditions (5 marks, 1 each):**

**BC1 — Upstream vertical face (at $x = -L$, $0 \le y \le D$):** Water at rest in the reservoir,

$$h(-L, y) = H_1 = 5 \text{ m} \quad \text{(Dirichlet)}$$

**BC2 — Downstream vertical face (at $x = L$, $0 \le y \le D$):** Water at rest in the tail-water,

$$h(L, y) = H_2 = 1 \text{ m} \quad \text{(Dirichlet)}$$

**BC3 — Impervious base (at $y = 0$, $-L \le x \le L$):** No flow into the impervious stratum,

$$\left.\frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = 0 \quad \text{(Neumann)}$$

**BC4 — Top of permeable layer below the structure (at $y = D$, beneath the weir base):** This is a fixed impervious surface (concrete of the weir), so

$$\left.\frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right|_{y=D} = 0 \quad \text{(Neumann)}$$

**BC5 — Plane of symmetry (at $x = 0$, $0 \le y \le D$):** By symmetry, the head gradient normal to the centerline vanishes:

$$\left.\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} = 0 \quad \text{(Neumann)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question Choice A):**
> - Students frequently **omit the plane of symmetry** boundary condition, treating only the upstream and downstream faces. This loses **1 mark** under the "BC5" slot.
> - A common error is to apply $h = H_1$ on the *top* surface (at $y = D$) where the weir base sits, but it should be a Neumann condition (no flow through the concrete).
> - Forgetting to specify the **domain coordinates** explicitly also costs a mark; KTU examiners expect a clear PDE + domain + boundary conditions setup, not just equations in isolation.

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define hydraulic head. Show that for steady-state flow of an incompressible fluid through a saturated, homogeneous, isotropic porous medium, the head satisfies the Laplace equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2D seepage problem of flow under a sheet pile wall driven into a 10 m thick permeable soil layer that rests on an impervious base, with water levels at 6 m on the upstream side and 1 m on the downstream side, identify the location and the mathematical form of each of the five typical boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question Choice B

**(a) Derivation of Laplace Equation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** *Hydraulic head* $h$ at a point in a saturated porous medium is defined as the mechanical energy per unit weight of water, measured with respect to a chosen datum. It has three components:

$$h = \frac{p}{\gamma_w} + y + \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

For groundwater flow, velocities are extremely small (Darcy's regime), so the velocity head $v^2/2g$ is negligible. Thus:

$$h \approx \frac{p}{\gamma_w} + y$$

**Darcy's law (2 marks):** For an isotropic, homogeneous medium with hydraulic conductivity $K$:

$$v_x = -K\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}, \quad v_y = -K\frac{\partial h}{\partial y}$$

**Continuity (2 marks):** For steady, incompressible flow, the divergence of velocity vanishes:

$$\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Substitution (1 mark):** Substituting Darcy's law:

$$-K\left(\frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2}\right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

This is the **two-dimensional Laplace equation**.

**(b) Boundary Conditions for Sheet Pile Wall (7 marks):**

A sheet pile wall is a thin impermeable barrier driven into a permeable soil. The 2D flow domain is the soil mass around the pile, bounded above by the upstream and downstream ground surfaces and below by the impervious base.

**Take origin at the tip of the sheet pile**, with $x$ horizontal and $y$ vertical (positive upward). The pile extends from $y = 0$ (tip) to $y = $ pile height (top, above ground). The soil layer is 10 m thick.

| # | Boundary | Mathematical Condition | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | **Impervious base** of soil layer | $\partial h/\partial y = 0$ at $y = -10$ m | Neumann |
| 2 | **Impervious sheet pile surface** (both sides of pile) | $\partial h/\partial n = 0$ along the pile | Neumann |
| 3 | **Plane of symmetry** (vertical line through the pile tip, for symmetric geometry) | $\partial h/\partial x = 0$ at $x = 0$ | Neumann |
| 4 | **Upstream ground surface** (water at rest in reservoir) | $h = H_1 = 6$ m | Dirichlet |
| 5 | **Downstream ground surface** (water at rest in tail water) | $h = H_2 = 1$ m | Dirichlet |

**Note on the seepage face:** In a sheet pile problem with no downstream free water surface inside the soil mass (no phreatic line is drawn inside the soil in the standard analysis), the *exit face* on the downstream side at the ground level can be modelled as a **seepage face** with $h = y$ (if the downstream water level is below ground) — but the dominant KTU treatment treats the upstream and downstream ground surfaces as Dirichlet boundaries (water at rest).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question Choice B):**
> - **Mark loss trigger 1:** Writing $h = y$ for the *impervious base*. The base has $p \neq 0$ and is solid; the correct condition is $\partial h/\partial n = 0$.
> - **Mark loss trigger 2:** Calling the *upstream water body* a "free water surface." It is water *at rest*, hence an *equipotential* Dirichlet boundary with $h = H_1$, not a phreatic line.
> - **Mark loss trigger 3:** Omitting the derivation of the Laplace equation and jumping directly to the result. The full 3-step derivation (Darcy → continuity → substitution) is worth **4 marks** in part (a) of this question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Laplace equation** $\nabla^2 h = 0$ governs steady-state, incompressible, isotropic groundwater flow. It has infinitely many mathematical solutions; **boundary conditions** make the physical solution unique.
- **Two families of boundary conditions exist:** Dirichlet (head prescribed) and Neumann (flux prescribed). The five conditions in the syllabus fall into one of these two families.
- **Impervious layer** $\Rightarrow$ $\partial h/\partial n = 0$ (Neumann). The surface acts as a **streamline**; equipotentials are parallel to it.
- **Plane of symmetry** $\Rightarrow$ $\partial h/\partial n = 0$ (Neumann). Mathematically equivalent to an impervious boundary, but the *origin* is geometric, not material. Allows **halving** the computational domain.
- **Free water surface** (phreatic line) $\Rightarrow$ $h = y$ (Dirichlet) because $p = 0$ on it. The surface's **location is unknown a priori** — this makes the problem *non-linear* in the strict sense.
- **Water at rest** $\Rightarrow$ $h = h_0 = $ constant (Dirichlet, uniform). Models upstream/downstream reservoirs as **equipotential** regions.
- **Seepage surface** $\Rightarrow$ $h = y$ (Dirichlet). Same equation as the free water surface, but the surface's **location is known** (it's the downstream slope of the dam). This is a *linear* problem.
- **Darcy's law** in vector form $\vec{v} = -K \nabla h$ combined with **continuity** $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ **gives** the Laplace equation — this is the canonical derivation.
- **The pressure head** $p/\gamma_w$ is the difference between total head and elevation head: $p/\gamma_w = h - y$.
- **Equipotential lines and streamlines are orthogonal** in an isotropic medium — this is the basis of the **flow net** method.
- **Maximum principle:** The head inside the domain can never exceed the maximum, nor fall below the minimum, of the heads prescribed on the boundary. This guarantees uniqueness of solution.
- **Units to remember:** $h$ in **m**, $K$ in **m/s** (or **cm/s**), $v$ in **m/s**, $\gamma_w$ = **9.81 kN/m³** (or **9810 N/m³**).
- **KTU board trick:** Whenever a question says "set up the problem" or "apply boundary conditions," always write **(i)** the PDE, **(ii)** the domain, and **(iii)** all the boundary equations — in that order, with a labelled sketch. Most students lose marks for missing one of the three.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Takeaway for KTU 2024:** The boundary condition is not just a mathematical formality — it is the **physical fingerprint** of the engineering scenario. Train yourself to look at any seepage drawing and *immediately* identify which of the five conditions applies to each line on the figure. This single skill will earn you the majority of marks on Module 3 boundary-condition questions.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET632 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dupit-Forchheimer Theory: Steady Flow Above an Impervious Horizontal Boundary - Dupit's Equation and Water Table Subject to Recharge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Dupuit-Forchheimer Theory** is a classical steady-state analytical framework used in groundwater hydraulics to describe unconfined flow through a homogeneous, isotropic porous medium situated above a horizontal impervious base. The theory was independently developed by **Jules Dupuit (1863)** and later refined by **Philipp Forchheimer (1901)** to overcome the mathematical intractability of the rigorous Laplace equation for free-surface (phreatic) flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Dupuit-Forchheimer assumptions state that for unconfined flow with a free water table above an impervious horizontal base, the hydraulic gradient $i$ is approximated as equal to the slope of the free surface ($i = -dh/dx$), and the flow lines are assumed to be **horizontal and equipotential surfaces are vertical**, thereby reducing a 2-D problem to a 1-D analytical solution.

The theory yields two fundamental working equations in KTU examinations:

1. **Dupuit's Equation (No Recharge):** $q = \dfrac{k}{2L}\left(h_1^2 - h_2^2\right)$
2. **Water Table Subject to Recharge:** $h^2 = h_0^2 - \dfrac{2q_0}{k}x - \dfrac{w}{k}x^2$

Where:
- $q$ = **discharge per unit width** [m²/s or m³/s per m]
- $k$ = **hydraulic conductivity** of the aquifer [m/s]
- $h_1, h_2$ = **phreatic surface elevations** above the impervious base at sections 1 and 2 [m]
- $L$ = **horizontal distance** between sections [m]
- $w$ = **recharge rate** (vertical infiltration) per unit horizontal area [m/s]

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Sand Tray with a Garden Hose":**
> 
> Imagine a long, transparent rectangular tray filled with uniform **coarse sand**, resting on a sealed, perfectly flat floor (the impervious boundary). You pour water into one end from a tap (recharge) and let it drain out at the other end through a small outlet. If you could see through the tray, the wet top surface would form a curved, dome-like shape — this is the **water table**.
> 
> Dupuit's great insight was to say: *"For practical engineering purposes, instead of describing the exact curved flow paths of water particles zig-zagging through the sand pores, assume every water particle moves perfectly horizontally, and the slope of the water table itself is the driving force."*
> 
> This is like telling a beginner swimmer: instead of calculating the exact 3-D looping path of water in a whirlpool, just look at the slope of the surface — the steeper the slope, the faster the flow. **That simple slope IS the hydraulic gradient for this theory.**

### 1.3 Physical Significance in Irrigation Engineering

- **Designing subsurface agricultural drainage systems** in flat alluvial plains (e.g., Kuttanad, Palakkad, and Thrissur regions of Kerala)
- **Estimating groundwater mound** heights under recharge ponds, percolation tanks, and check-dams
- **Spacing lateral drains** in paddy-cum-fish culture fields
- **Predicting seepage losses** from unlined canals resting on porous soil

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module explicitly requires deriving **Dupuit's equation** for steady flow *without recharge* and *extending* it to a water table that receives **uniform vertical recharge (w)**. Both cases assume a **horizontal impervious base** at depth $D$ below some datum.

---

## 1.4 Standard Constants and Symbols Used

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:--------:|
| $k$ | Hydraulic conductivity | **m/s** |
| $q$ | Discharge per unit width | **m²/s** or **m³/day per m** |
| $h$ | Saturated depth above impervious base | **m** |
| $w$ | Recharge rate per unit area | **m/s** or **mm/day** |
| $L$ | Horizontal spacing/distance | **m** |
| $i$ | Hydraulic gradient | dimensionless |
| $v$ | Darcy velocity | **m/s** |
| $D$ | Depth of impervious base | **m** |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Dupuit-Forchheimer Assumptions (Foundation Block)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board examiner **always** awards a partial mark for stating assumptions clearly. Memorize them in this exact logical order:

1. **Homogeneous & Isotropic Aquifer:** The porous medium has uniform hydraulic conductivity $k$ in all directions.
2. **Steady-State Flow:** The water table profile and discharge do not change with time ($\partial h/\partial t = 0$).
3. **Horizontal Flow Lines:** Every water particle moves in a perfectly horizontal direction, regardless of the curvature of the phreatic surface. This is the most restrictive (and most useful) assumption.
4. **Vertical Equipotentials:** The hydraulic head at any vertical section is constant, meaning all points at a given $x$ have the same head $h(x)$.
5. **Hydraulic Gradient ≈ Water-Table Slope:** The Darcy gradient $i$ equals the slope of the free surface:
$$i = -\dfrac{dh}{dx}$$
6. **Darcy's Law Valid:** Flow remains laminar, so $v = k \cdot i$ still applies at every point.
7. **Negligible Capillary Fringe:** The water table is treated as a sharp boundary between saturated and unsaturated zones.

---

## 2.2 Physical Setup — Flow Above Horizontal Impervious Base

```
    Section 1                              Section 2
   (x = 0)                                 (x = L)
   h = h₁                                  h = h₂
    │  ╲         Water Table              ╱  │
    │   ╲ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ╱   │
    │    ╲ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ╱    │
    │     ╲___q___w___w___w___w___q___╱     │
    │      saturated soil (k)              │
    │                                      │
    │      ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬         │
              Impervious Base (D)
```

- The **phreatic surface** is the upper boundary of the saturated zone.
- The **impervious base** is the lower boundary (no flow can cross it).
- **Recharge** $w$ enters vertically through the top surface (mimicking rainfall or irrigation infiltration).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the single most important revision block for your KTU 2024 exam.** Memorize the equation column, the applicability, and the boundary conditions.

| # | Equation | Applicability | Boundary Conditions |
|:-:|:---------|:--------------|:-------------------:|
| 1 | $q = -k \cdot h \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dx}$ | Governing Darcy-Dupuit ODE (general) | Depends on problem |
| 2 | $q = \dfrac{k}{2L}\left(h_1^2 - h_2^2\right)$ | Steady flow, **no recharge** ($w = 0$) | $h(0) = h_1$, $h(L) = h_2$ |
| 3 | $h^2 - h_0^2 = -\dfrac{2q_0 x}{k} - \dfrac{w x^2}{k}$ | Steady flow **with recharge** $w$, origin at left edge | $h(0) = h_0$ |
| 4 | $h^2 = h_0^2 - \dfrac{2q_0}{k}x - \dfrac{w}{k}x^2$ | Same as (3), solved for $h$ | $h(0) = h_0$ |
| 5 | $h^2 = h_L^2 + \dfrac{w}{k}(L^2 - x^2) + \dfrac{2q_L}{k}(L - x)$ | Recharge problem, **origin at drain** | $h(L) = h_L$ |
| 6 | $h_x^2 = h_1^2 + \dfrac{w}{k}(L-x)x$ | Symmetric drain (mid-point water table, $h_1$ at drain, $h_0$ at mid) | Symmetric BCs |
| 7 | $q_0 = \dfrac{k(h_1^2 - h_2^2)}{2L} - \dfrac{wL}{2}$ | Discharge at upstream section with recharge | At $x=0, L$ |
| 8 | $h_{max} = \sqrt{h_1^2 + \dfrac{wL^2}{4k}}$ | Maximum water table height at mid-point between drains (symmetric) | $dh/dx = 0$ at mid |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict Symbol Discipline:** In KTU scripts, **never** confuse $w$ (recharge rate) with $W$ (well discharge or width). They look identical in handwriting but mean very different things. The unit of $w$ is **m/s** (or mm/day), not m³/s.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Dupuit's Equation is Used |
|:-----------|:------------------------------|
| Subsurface drainage of paddy fields | Quick estimation of drain spacing $L$ for a desired mid-point water table height |
| Recharge mound under percolation tank | Predicts how high the water table rises under a pond — important for water-logging prevention |
| Canal seepage estimation | Calculates loss from unlined canals resting on pervious soil |
| Salinity control in coastal Kerala | Determines drain depth to keep phreatic surface below crop root zone |
| Aquifer characterization | Confirms whether observed water-table profiles follow a parabolic shape (Dupuit signature) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dupuit's Equation (Without Recharge)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most expected derivation** in any KTU Irrigation & Drainage exam. Practice writing it on a blank sheet until you can reproduce it in under 8 minutes.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider steady, unconfined flow through a homogeneous porous medium bounded above by a free water table and below by a horizontal impervious stratum. Let:
- Section 1 be at $x = 0$ with water-table elevation $h = h_1$ above the impervious base.
- Section 2 be at $x = L$ with $h = h_2$.
- A control volume of unit width (perpendicular to the page) and thickness $dx$ located between sections $x$ and $x + dx$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Darcy's Law at a generic section $x$**

The depth of saturated flow at section $x$ is $h$. By Dupuit's assumption, the hydraulic gradient equals the slope of the water table:
$$i = -\dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

The Darcy velocity at section $x$ is therefore:
$$v_x = k \cdot i = -k\dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Discharge per unit width through the vertical section**

The discharge per unit width $q_x$ equals the Darcy velocity times the saturated cross-section thickness $h$:
$$q_x = v_x \cdot h = -k \cdot h \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Steady-State Continuity (No Recharge, No Storage)**

Since there is no recharge, the discharge $q_x$ is the **same at every section** (continuity in steady state):
$$q_x = q = \text{constant}$$

Therefore:
$$q = -k \cdot h \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

**Step 4 — Separate Variables**

Move all $h$ terms to one side and $x$ to the other:
$$q \, dx = -k \cdot h \, dh$$

**Step 5 — Integrate between the two boundary sections**

Integrate from $x = 0$ (where $h = h_1$) to $x = L$ (where $h = h_2$):
$$q \int_{0}^{L} dx = -k \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h \, dh$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the Integrals**

Left side:
$$q \cdot L = qL$$

Right side (using $\int h \, dh = h^2/2$):
$$-k \left[\dfrac{h^2}{2}\right]_{h_1}^{h_2} = -\dfrac{k}{2}\left(h_2^2 - h_1^2\right) = \dfrac{k}{2}\left(h_1^2 - h_2^2\right)$$

**Step 7 — Equate and Solve for $q$**

$$qL = \dfrac{k}{2}\left(h_1^2 - h_2^2\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,q = \dfrac{k}{2L}\left(h_1^2 - h_2^2\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the famous **Dupuit equation** (also called the **Dupuit-Parabolic equation** because the water-table profile is a parabola in $x$).

### 3.1.3 The Water-Table Profile Equation (No Recharge)

Integrating once between $x$ and $L$ (instead of $0$ and $L$):
$$q(L - x) = \dfrac{k}{2}\left(h^2 - h_2^2\right)$$

Substituting $q = \dfrac{k}{2L}\left(h_1^2 - h_2^2\right)$:

$$\dfrac{k}{2L}\left(h_1^2 - h_2^2\right)(L - x) = \dfrac{k}{2}\left(h^2 - h_2^2\right)$$

Cancelling $k/2$:

$$\dfrac{(L - x)}{L}\left(h_1^2 - h_2^2\right) = h^2 - h_2^2$$

$$\boxed{\,h^2 = h_2^2 + \left(h_1^2 - h_2^2\right)\left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right)\,}$$

This is an **inverted parabola** in $x$ when plotted as $h^2$ vs $x$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Water Table Subject to Uniform Recharge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This extension is the **second most expected KTU question** from this module. The recharge $w$ represents rainfall infiltration or upward capillary inflow.

### 3.2.1 Modified Setup

- A **uniform vertical recharge** $w$ [m/s] enters the saturated zone per unit horizontal area.
- The origin is taken at the **left boundary** ($x = 0$).
- At $x = 0$, the water-table height is $h_0$ and the discharge per unit width leaving the section to the left is $q_0$.
- The problem is **steady**, so storage is zero.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Discharge at a generic section $x$**

Between $x = 0$ and $x = x$, the cumulative recharge entering the control volume is $w \cdot x$ per unit width. By continuity, the discharge at $x$ is:
$$q_x = q_0 + wx$$

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy-Dupuit Law at section $x$**

$$q_x = -k \cdot h \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

Combining:
$$q_0 + wx = -k \cdot h \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Separate Variables**

Rearranging:
$$(q_0 + wx) \, dx = -k \cdot h \, dh$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $x = 0$ to $x = x$**

On the right, the head changes from $h_0$ to $h(x)$:
$$\int_{0}^{x} (q_0 + w\xi) \, d\xi = -k \int_{h_0}^{h} h \, dh$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Left-Side Integral**

$$\int_{0}^{x} (q_0 + w\xi) \, d\xi = \left[q_0 \xi + \dfrac{w \xi^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{x} = q_0 x + \dfrac{w x^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the Right-Side Integral**

$$-k \int_{h_0}^{h} h \, dh = -k \left[\dfrac{h^2}{2}\right]_{h_0}^{h} = -\dfrac{k}{2}\left(h^2 - h_0^2\right) = \dfrac{k}{2}\left(h_0^2 - h^2\right)$$

**Step 7 — Equate and Solve for $h^2$**

$$q_0 x + \dfrac{w x^2}{2} = \dfrac{k}{2}\left(h_0^2 - h^2\right)$$

$$h_0^2 - h^2 = \dfrac{2 q_0 x}{k} + \dfrac{w x^2}{k}$$

$$\boxed{\,h^2 = h_0^2 - \dfrac{2 q_0}{k}x - \dfrac{w}{k}x^2\,}$$

### 3.2.3 The Symmetric Drain Case (Highly Tested in KTU)

A very common KTU problem: two parallel drains at $x = 0$ and $x = L$, each with water depth $h_1$, with a **groundwater mound** rising to $h_0$ at the mid-point. By symmetry, $q_0 = 0$ at $x = 0$ (no flow crosses the mid-plane). The general equation reduces to:

$$h^2 = h_1^2 + \dfrac{w}{k}(L - x)x$$

The **maximum water-table elevation** occurs at $x = L/2$:
$$h_{max}^2 = h_1^2 + \dfrac{w L^2}{4k}$$

$$\boxed{\,h_{max} = \sqrt{h_1^2 + \dfrac{w L^2}{4k}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **KTU-favourite formula** for designing agricultural drain spacing $L$:
> $$L = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{k\left(h_{max}^2 - h_1^2\right)}{w}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix — What to Use When

| Given Quantity | Required Quantity | Use This Equation |
|:--------------|:------------------|:------------------|
| $k, L, h_1, h_2$ | Discharge $q$ (no recharge) | $q = \dfrac{k}{2L}(h_1^2 - h_2^2)$ |
| $k, L, h_1, h_2, w$ | Discharge at section 1 | $q_1 = \dfrac{k(h_1^2 - h_2^2)}{2L} - \dfrac{wL}{2}$ |
| $k, h_1, w, L$ | Mid-point mound height | $h_{max} = \sqrt{h_1^2 + wL^2/(4k)}$ |
| $k, h_1, h_{max}, w$ | Required drain spacing | $L = 2\sqrt{k(h_{max}^2 - h_1^2)/w}$ |
| $k, w, h_0$ | Water table at distance $x$ | $h^2 = h_0^2 - (2q_0 x)/k - (w x^2)/k$ |
