topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET632 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unsteady State Drainage Equations: Glover-Dum Equation - Application; Concept of Kraijenhoff Vande Leur Mass Land Equation - Application and Analysis for Constant Recharge and Intermittent Recharge Cases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Big Picture: Why "Unsteady State" Matters

In agricultural drainage, the water table between two parallel subsurface drains is rarely in equilibrium. After every irrigation or rain event, the water table **rises**; after the event stops, it **falls (recedes)** under the influence of gravity. Because the water table height and the drain discharge both change with time, the flow regime is **unsteady (transient)**. The Glover–Dum and Kraijenhoff van de Leur equations are the two classical analytical tools used to mathematically predict *how fast* this rise and fall occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECET632 – Module 3):**
> The Board Examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between a **falling-head problem (no recharge, Glover–Dum)** and a **fluctuating-head problem (with recharge, Kraijenhoff v.d. Leur)**. Marks are lost when students apply the wrong equation.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Terminology)

### (A) Glover–Dum Equation
The Glover–Dum equation is the **exponential-decay solution of the linearised Boussinesq equation** for the water table falling between two parallel drains under the assumption of **zero recharge**. It expresses the water-table height above drain level as an exponential function of time.

**Governing Differential Equation (Boussinesq linearised form):**

$$\frac{\partial H}{\partial t} \;=\; \frac{K \cdot \bar{D}}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} H}{\partial x^{2}}$$

where $H$ = water-table height above drain, $K$ = hydraulic conductivity, $\bar{D}$ = equivalent depth (Hooghoudt), and $\mu$ = drainable porosity (specific yield).

### (B) Kraijenhoff van de Leur Mass-Land Equation
A **lumped (0-D) water-balance model** for the *entire* tract of land between two drains. It treats the area as a single "tank" and tracks the mean water-table height $\bar{H}$ as a function of time, considering net recharge (rainfall + irrigation − ET). It is the **macroscopic (mass-balance) counterpart** of the microscopic Glover–Dum analysis.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Leaking Bathtub"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (First-Time Learner Friendly):**
> Imagine a **bathtub filled with water** with a small hole at the bottom (the drain).
> - **Glover–Dum case:** The tap is OFF. Water simply leaks out. The water level falls in an **exponential decay** — fast initially, slow later. This is *pure falling head* with no recharge.
> - **Kraijenhoff v.d. Leur case:** The tap is TURNED ON (rainfall/irrigation adding water) while the leak continues. The water level now *rises* if inflow > outflow, *falls* if outflow > inflow, and reaches a *steady plateau* when inflow = outflow. This is *fluctuating head with recharge*.

The **drainage factor $\alpha$** is the "leakiness constant" — it tells you *how fast* the tub empties. A large $\alpha$ means a very leaky tub (sandy soil, closely-spaced drains); a small $\alpha$ means a sluggish tub (clay soil, wide drain spacing).

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants / Parameters (Bolded for Recall)

| Symbol | Name | Typical Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | **Drainage factor / Decay constant** | $\text{day}^{-1}$ | Reciprocal of the time constant of the system |
| $\mu$ | **Drainable porosity (specific yield)** | dimensionless (m³/m³) | Volume of water released per unit volume of soil |
| $K$ | **Hydraulic conductivity** | m/day | Ease of water flow through soil |
| $D$ | **Equivalent depth / Aquifer thickness** | m | Distance from drain to impermeable layer (or Hooghoudt equivalent) |
| $L$ | **Drain spacing** | m | Distance between two parallel drains |
| $R$ | **Recharge rate** | m/day | Net vertical addition of water to water table |
| $q$ | **Drain discharge (flux)** | m/day | Volumetric flow per unit area leaving through drains |
| $P$ | **Rainfall / Irrigation depth** | m | Total water added during a recharge event |
| $t_{p}$ | **Duration of recharge event** | day | Length of the rainfall/irrigation pulse |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Boussinesq Assumption

Both equations originate from **Boussinesq's equation (1877)** for unconfined flow toward drains. The critical assumption that makes these equations mathematically tractable is the **Dupuit–Forchheimer assumption** — flow is essentially horizontal and the hydraulic gradient equals the slope of the free surface, $\partial H/\partial x$.

---

## 2.2 The Glover–Dum Equation (Zero Recharge)

### 2.2.1 Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

1. **Start with the lumped mass balance** for a unit area of land between drains:
   $$\mu \frac{d\bar{H}}{dt} = -q_{drain}$$
   (water leaving via drains, no inflow).

2. **Assume drain discharge is proportional to mean water-table height** (linearised outflow from Glover's 1978 work):
   $$q_{drain} = \alpha \mu \bar{H}$$

3. **Combine to get a first-order ODE:**
   $$\frac{d\bar{H}}{dt} = -\alpha \bar{H}$$

4. **Solve with initial condition** $\bar{H}(0) = H_0$:
   $$\boxed{\;\bar{H}(t) = H_0 \, e^{-\alpha t}\;}$$

5. **Discharge from drains at time $t$:**
   $$\boxed{\;q(t) = \alpha \mu H_0 \, e^{-\alpha t} = q_0 \, e^{-\alpha t}\;}$$

6. **Cumulative volume drained up to time $t$:**
   $$V(t) = \int_0^t q(\tau) d\tau = \mu H_0 \left(1 - e^{-\alpha t}\right)$$

### 2.2.2 The Drainage Factor $\alpha$ — Engineering Formula

For **two parallel drains** with spacing $L$ and effective aquifer depth $D$ above an impermeable base:

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi^{2} K D}{\mu L^{2}}$$

For **Hooghoudt's equivalent depth** $d$ (when impermeable layer is shallow and radial flow near drains dominates):

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi^{2} K d}{\mu L^{2}}$$

### 2.2.3 Why $\alpha$ Controls Everything

- The system has a **time constant** $\tau = 1/\alpha$.
- After $t = \tau$, only $\approx 36.8\%$ of the initial head remains.
- After $t = 4.6/\alpha$ (≈ $5\tau$), the head is essentially zero ($\approx 0.99\%$ remains).

---

## 2.3 The Kraijenhoff van de Leur Mass-Land Equation

### 2.3.1 The Governing Mass Balance

For a unit area of land bounded by two parallel drains, the **net rate of change of storage** equals **inflow − outflow**:

$$\mu \frac{d\bar{H}}{dt} = R - q_{drain} = R - \alpha \mu \bar{H}$$

This is a **first-order linear non-homogeneous ODE** with constant coefficients.

### 2.3.2 CASE 1 — Constant Continuous Recharge

Recharge $R$ is applied continuously. The solution of the ODE with initial condition $\bar{H}(0) = H_0$ is:

$$\boxed{\;\bar{H}(t) = \frac{R}{\alpha \mu}\left(1 - e^{-\alpha t}\right) + H_0 \, e^{-\alpha t}\;}$$

**Steady-state (long time, $t \to \infty$):**

$$\boxed{\;\bar{H}_{\infty} = \frac{R}{\alpha \mu}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of $R/(\alpha\mu)$:** This is the equilibrium water-table height. It is the height at which the drain outflow exactly balances the recharge. Engineers design drainage systems so that $\bar{H}_{\infty}$ stays **below the crop-root zone**.

### 2.3.3 CASE 2 — Intermittent Recharge (Pulsed Rainfall / Irrigation)

A recharge event of intensity $R$ acts for a finite duration $t_p$, after which recharge stops ($R = 0$).

**Phase 1 — During recharge ($0 \le t \le t_p$):**
Same as Case 1:
$$\bar{H}(t) = \frac{R}{\alpha \mu}\left(1 - e^{-\alpha t}\right) + H_0 \, e^{-\alpha t}$$

**Peak water table** reached at the end of the recharge period:
$$\boxed{\;\bar{H}_{peak} = \frac{R}{\alpha \mu}\left(1 - e^{-\alpha t_p}\right) + H_0 \, e^{-\alpha t_p}\;}$$

**Phase 2 — After recharge stops ($t > t_p$):**
Pure Glover–Dum decay from the peak:
$$\boxed{\;\bar{H}(t) = \bar{H}_{peak} \, e^{-\alpha (t - t_p)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** The recharge term in the mass balance must be expressed in **m/day** (rate), NOT in total depth $P$ (m). If the question gives $P$ in mm and $t_p$ in days, you must compute $R = P/t_p$ first.

### 2.3.4 Special Case — Single Rainfall Pulse of Total Depth $P$

If total rainfall depth $P$ occurs instantaneously (or uniformly) over the whole area, the rise in water table is $\Delta H = P/\mu$. The peak head becomes:

$$\bar{H}_{peak} = H_0 + \frac{P}{\mu}$$

This instantaneous-rise result is often used as a conservative design assumption.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Equation | Name | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{H}(t) = H_0 e^{-\alpha t}$ | **Glover–Dum Falling Head** | No recharge; pure recession |
| 2 | $q(t) = \alpha \mu H_0 e^{-\alpha t}$ | **Drain Discharge (Glover–Dum)** | Find drain flow during recession |
| 3 | $V(t) = \mu H_0 (1-e^{-\alpha t})$ | **Cumulative Drainage Volume** | Total water removed in time $t$ |
| 4 | $\alpha = \pi^{2} K D / (\mu L^{2})$ | **Drainage Factor (deep)** | For parallel drains over deep impermeable layer |
| 5 | $\alpha = \pi^{2} K d / (\mu L^{2})$ | **Drainage Factor (Hooghoudt)** | For shallow impermeable layer (use Hooghoudt's $d$) |
| 6 | $\bar{H}(t) = \frac{R}{\alpha\mu}(1-e^{-\alpha t}) + H_0 e^{-\alpha t}$ | **Kraijenhoff v.d. Leur (Constant R)** | Recharge applied continuously |
| 7 | $\bar{H}_{\infty} = R/(\alpha\mu)$ | **Steady-State Head** | Long-term equilibrium with constant R |
| 8 | $\bar{H}_{peak} = \frac{R}{\alpha\mu}(1-e^{-\alpha t_p}) + H_0 e^{-\alpha t_p}$ | **Peak Head (Intermittent)** | After a finite recharge event |
| 9 | $\bar{H}(t) = \bar{H}_{peak} e^{-\alpha(t-t_p)}$ | **Recession After Recharge** | For $t \ge t_p$, post-recharge decay |
| 10 | $\bar{H}_{peak} = H_0 + P/\mu$ | **Instantaneous Recharge Rise** | Conservative design, total depth $P$ added at once |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Convention for KTU Examinations:** $K$ in m/day, $D$ and $L$ in m, $\mu$ dimensionless, $\alpha$ in day⁻¹, $R$ in m/day, $P$ in m (or mm → divide by 1000). Mixing units is the **#1 cause of numerical error** in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Equation Used |
|---|---|
| **Design of subsurface drainage in paddy fields (Kuttanad, Keralabackwaters)** | Kraijenhoff v.d. Leur with constant $R$ to check steady-state $H$ |
| **Transient simulation after a storm event (Western Ghats lateritic soils)** | Intermittent recharge case (Case 2) |
| **Predicting time to lower water table for crop sowing** | Glover–Dum recession curve |
| **Calibration of $\alpha$ from field data** | Plot $\ln(H_t/H_0)$ vs $t$; slope $= -\alpha$ |
| **Salinity control in arid irrigated lands** | Both — to prevent capillary rise of salts |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Glover–Dum Equation from Boussinesq PDE

### Step 1: Governing PDE
The **Boussinesq equation** for unsteady saturated flow in an unconfined aquifer toward parallel drains, with the Dupuit–Forchheimer assumption:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(H \frac{\partial H}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\mu}{K} \frac{\partial H}{\partial t}$$

### Step 2: Linearisation (Boussinesq's key step)
Boussinesq observed that for a slowly falling water table, the variation in $H$ is small compared to the mean depth $D$. Replacing $H$ in the diffusion term with a constant mean $\bar{D}$:

$$D \frac{\partial^{2} H}{\partial x^{2}} \approx \frac{\mu}{K} \frac{\partial H}{\partial t}$$

### Step 3: Separation of Variables
Assume $H(x,t) = X(x)\,T(t)$. Substituting:

$$D \, X''(x) \, T(t) = \frac{\mu}{K} \, X(x) \, T'(t)$$

Separating:

$$\frac{X''(x)}{X(x)} = \frac{\mu}{K D} \cdot \frac{T'(t)}{T(t)} = -\lambda^{2}$$

(negative constant for a bounded, decaying solution).

### Step 4: Solve the spatial ODE
$$X''(x) + \lambda^{2} X(x) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; X(x) = A\cos(\lambda x) + B\sin(\lambda x)$$

Boundary conditions: drains at $x = 0$ and $x = L$ are at atmospheric pressure, so $H = 0$ there.

- At $x = 0$: $X(0) = 0 \Rightarrow A = 0$.
- At $x = L$: $X(L) = 0 \Rightarrow \sin(\lambda L) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda L = n\pi$.

The **fundamental mode** (lowest eigenvalue) corresponds to $n = 1$:

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{\pi}{L}$$

### Step 5: Solve the temporal ODE
$$\frac{T'(t)}{T(t)} = -\frac{K D \lambda^{2}}{\mu} = -\alpha$$

where we define the **drainage factor**:

$$\alpha = \frac{K D \pi^{2}}{\mu L^{2}}$$

Solution: $T(t) = C e^{-\alpha t}$.

### Step 6: Assemble and Take the Spatial Mean
The full solution for the lowest mode is:

$$H(x,t) = B \sin\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right) e^{-\alpha t}$$

The **mean water-table height** over $[0, L]$ is:

$$\bar{H}(t) = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L H(x,t)\,dx = \frac{B e^{-\alpha t}}{L}\left[-\frac{L}{\pi}\cos\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right)\right]_0^L = \frac{2 B}{\pi} e^{-\alpha t}$$

Let $H_0 = \frac{2B}{\pi}$ be the initial mean height. Then:

$$\boxed{\;\bar{H}(t) = H_0\, e^{-\alpha t}\;}$$

This is the **Glover–Dum equation**. The exact same result was derived independently by **Dum (1954)** and confirmed by **Glover (1978)** using a slightly different averaging procedure.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Kraijenhoff van de Leur Mass-Land Equation

### Step 1: Define the Control Volume
Take a control volume of unit plan area $\Delta A = 1\ \text{m}^2$ bounded above by the water table and below by the impermeable layer (mean depth $D$).

### Step 2: Write the Mass Balance
Rate of change of stored water = Recharge in − Drainage out:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\mu \bar{H}\right) = R - q_{drain}$$

### Step 3: Relate Drainage to Water-Table Height
From the Glover analysis, the mean drain discharge per unit area equals $\alpha \mu \bar{H}$:

$$q_{drain} = \alpha \mu \bar{H}$$

### Step 4: Form the Linear ODE
Substitute:

$$\mu \frac{d\bar{H}}{dt} = R - \alpha\mu \bar{H}$$

$$\frac{d\bar{H}}{dt} + \alpha \bar{H} = \frac{R}{\mu}$$

### Step 5: Solve Using Integrating Factor $e^{\alpha t}$
Multiply both sides by $e^{\alpha t}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\bar{H} e^{\alpha t}\right) = \frac{R}{\mu} e^{\alpha t}$$

Integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$\bar{H}(t) e^{\alpha t} - \bar{H}(0) = \frac{R}{\mu \alpha}\left(e^{\alpha t} - 1\right)$$

### Step 6: Rearrange
$$\bar{H}(t) = \frac{R}{\alpha\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\alpha t}\right) + H_0 e^{-\alpha t}$$

This is the **Kraijenhoff van de Leur equation for constant recharge**. As $t \to \infty$:

$$\bar{H}_\infty = \frac{R}{\alpha\mu}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Intermittent Recharge (Pulsed)

### Step 1: Define the Two Phases
- **Phase 1** ($0 \le t \le t_p$): Recharge rate $R$ is constant. Solution is exactly Derivation 2.
- **Phase 2** ($t > t_p$): Recharge stops, $R = 0$. Solution is exactly Glover–Dum.

### Step 2: Compute the Peak
At $t = t_p$:

$$\bar{H}_{peak} = \frac{R}{\alpha\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\alpha t_p}\right) + H_0 e^{-\alpha t_p}$$

### Step 3: Recession Equation
For $t > t_p$:

$$\bar{H}(t) = \bar{H}_{peak}\, e^{-\alpha (t - t_p)}$$

### Step 4: Total Water Removed After Recharge
Integrating $q_{drain} = \alpha\mu \bar{H}$ from $t_p$ to $t$:

$$V_{after} = \int_{t_p}^{t} \alpha\mu \bar{H}_{peak} e^{-\alpha(\tau - t_p)} d\tau = \mu \bar{H}_{peak} \left(1 - e^{-\alpha(t - t_p)}\right)$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (Model):** A tract of land between parallel drains spaced $L = 50\ \text{m}$ apart has $K = 0.5\ \text{m/day}$, drainable porosity $\mu = 0.05$, and an equivalent depth $D = 5\ \text{m}$. After a heavy storm, the water table is initially at $H_0 = 1.2\ \text{m}$ above the drains. Compute (a) the drainage factor $\alpha$, (b) the time for the water table to drop to $0.3\ \text{m}$, and (c) the cumulative volume drained per unit area in that time.

**Solution:**

**(a) Drainage factor:**

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi^{2} K D}{\mu L^{2}} = \frac{(9.8696)(0.5)(5)}{(0.05)(2500)} = \frac{24.674}{125} = 0.1974\ \text{day}^{-1}$$

**(b) Time to reach $H = 0.3$ m:**

From $H(t) = H_0 e^{-\alpha t}$:

$$0.3 = 1.2 \, e^{-0.1974 t} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{-0.1974 t} = 0.25$$

$$-0.1974\, t = \ln(0.25) = -1.3863 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t = \frac{1.3863}{0.1974} \approx 7.02\ \text{days}$$

**(c) Cumulative volume drained:**

$$V = \mu H_0 (1 - e^{-\alpha t}) = 0.05 \times 1.2 \times (1 - 0.25) = 0.06 \times 0.75 = 0.045\ \text{m} = 45\ \text{mm}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Glover–Dum vs Kraijenhoff v.d. Leur)

| Feature | Glover–Dum | Kraijenhoff v.d. Leur |
|---|---|---|
| **Recharge** | Zero ($R = 0$) | Non-zero ($R \ne 0$, constant or pulsed) |
| **Dimensionality** | 1-D (spatial profile + time) | 0-D (lumped mean height) |
| **Mathematical form** | Exponential decay | Exponential approach to plateau (or decay after pulse) |
| **Peak water table** | Starts at $H_0$, monotonically decreases | Can rise above $H_0$ during recharge |
| **Steady state** | $H \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ | $H \to R/(\alpha\mu) \ne 0$ |
| **Typical use** | Post-rainfall recession | Long-term water-table fluctuation modelling |
| **Required inputs** | $\alpha, H_0$ | $\alpha, H_0, R, t_p, \mu$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Unified Drainage Model

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **logical flow** of the Glover–Dum and Kraijenhoff van de Leur models, showing how recharge, soil-aquifer properties, and boundary conditions feed into the water-table height and drain discharge outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Soil and Aquifer Properties: K, mu, D, L] --> calcA[Compute Drainage Factor alpha = pi^2 K D / mu L^2]
    inputB[Initial Water Table Height H0] --> junction1{Is there Recharge?}
    inputC[Recharge Input: R, tp, P] --> junction1
    junction1 -- No R = 0 --> branch1[Glover-Dum Branch]
    junction1 -- Yes R exists --> branch2[Kraijenhoff Branch]
    calcA --> branch1
    calcA --> branch2
    branch1 --> eq1[H of t = H0 exp negative alpha t]
    branch1 --> eq2[q of t = alpha mu H0 exp negative alpha t]
    branch1 --> eq3[V of t = mu H0 1 minus exp negative alpha t]
    branch2 --> caseA[Case 1 Constant Recharge]
    branch2 --> caseB[Case 2 Intermittent Recharge]
    caseA --> eq4[H of t = R over alpha mu times 1 minus exp negative alpha t plus H0 exp negative alpha t]
    caseA --> eq5[Steady State H infinity = R over alpha mu]
    caseB --> phase1[Phase 1: 0 to tp, recharge on, use Case 1 equation]
    caseB --> phase2[Phase 2: t greater than tp, recharge off, use Glover-Dum from peak]
    caseB --> eq6[H peak = R over alpha mu times 1 minus exp negative alpha tp plus H0 exp negative alpha tp]
    phase2 --> eq7[H of t = H peak exp negative alpha times t minus tp]
    eq1 --> outA[Output: Water Table Height vs Time]
    eq2 --> outB[Output: Drain Discharge vs Time]
    eq3 --> outC[Output: Cumulative Drainage Volume]
    eq4 --> outA
    eq5 --> outA
    eq6 --> outA
    eq7 --> outA
    outA --> design[Engineering Decision: Check H versus Root Zone Depth]
    outB --> design
    outC --> design
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Time Evolution of Water Table

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialState: H equals H0 at t equals 0
    InitialState --> RechargePhase: Recharge starts R over 0
    InitialState --> RecessionOnly: No recharge Glover-Dum case
    RechargePhase --> PeakReached: t equals tp
    PeakReached --> DrainageOnly: Recharge stops
    DrainageOnly --> SteadyState: Constant R long time H approaches R over alpha mu
    DrainageOnly --> ZeroHead: Glover-Dum long time H approaches 0
    SteadyState --> [*]
    ZeroHead --> [*]
```

## 4.3 ASCII Cross-Section Sketch (For Visualisation in Exam Answer Books)

> *Draw this in your KTU answer sheet to score full marks in descriptive parts.*

```
         Water Table (at t=0)  ───────────── H0
                                     |         |
                                     |  mu*D   |   Soil column between drains
                                     |         |   (drainable porosity = mu)
   ┌────────────┐             ┌──────▼─────────▼──────┐             ┌────────────┐
   │ Subsurface │             │    Saturated Zone      │             │ Subsurface │
   │   Drain 1  │─────────────│   (Water Table Falling)│─────────────│   Drain 2  │
   └────────────┘             │   V(t) drained out     │             └────────────┘
   x = 0                      └────────────────────────┘             x = L
                              │ Impermeable Layer     │
                              └────────────────────────┘
                                Depth D below drain level
```

**For intermittent recharge** — overlay a second, higher line showing $H_{peak}$ reached at $t = t_p$, after which the water table decays exponentially back down.

---

## 4.4 Schematic of Phases in Intermittent Recharge

| Phase | Time Interval | Governing Equation | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase 1 — Recharge ON | $0 \le t \le t_p$ | Kraijenhoff (constant $R$) | Water table **rises** from $H_0$ toward $H_{peak}$ |
| Phase 2 — Recharge OFF | $t > t_p$ | Glover–Dum from $H_{peak}$ | Water table **falls** exponentially to 0 (or to next event) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"State the Glover–Dum equation for unsteady state drainage. Define the drainage factor $\alpha$ and write the expression for $\alpha$ in terms of drain spacing $L$, hydraulic conductivity $K$, drainable porosity $\mu$, and equivalent depth $D$."**

**Model Answer:**

The Glover–Dum equation describes the exponential decay of the mean water-table height between two parallel drains under zero recharge:

$$\bar{H}(t) = H_0 \, e^{-\alpha t}$$

where $H_0$ = initial water-table height above the drain (m), $t$ = elapsed time (days), and $\alpha$ = drainage factor (day⁻¹).

**Drainage Factor:** $\alpha$ is the reciprocal of the time constant of the drainage system; it quantifies how rapidly the water table falls. For parallel drains over a deep impermeable base:

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi^{2} K D}{\mu L^{2}}$$

For shallow impermeable layers, the Hooghoudt equivalent depth $d$ replaces $D$:

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi^{2} K d}{\mu L^{2}}$$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**"Write the Kraijenhoff van de Leur mass-land equation for a drainage system subjected to a constant recharge rate $R$. State the physical meaning of the steady-state head $H_\infty = R/(\alpha\mu)$ and list any three field parameters required for its evaluation."**

**Model Answer:**

The Kraijenhoff van de Leur equation for a tract of land with continuous recharge $R$ (m/day) is:

$$\bar{H}(t) = \frac{R}{\alpha\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\alpha t}\right) + H_0 e^{-\alpha t}$$

where $\bar{H}(t)$ = mean water-table height at time $t$, $H_0$ = initial water-table height, $\alpha$ = drainage factor, $\mu$ = drainable porosity.

**Physical Meaning of $H_\infty = R/(\alpha\mu)$:** This is the **long-term equilibrium water-table height** at which the drain outflow $\alpha\mu\bar{H}$ exactly balances the recharge inflow $R$. Above this level, the water table would drop; below it, the water table would rise. Designers ensure $H_\infty$ stays below the crop-root zone.

**Three Field Parameters Required:**
1. Drainable porosity $\mu$ (from soil texture and lab test)
2. Hydraulic conductivity $K$ (from auger-hole or pumping test)
3. Drain spacing $L$ and equivalent depth $D$ (from soil profile and layout)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Derive the Glover–Dum equation starting from the linearised Boussinesq equation.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A subsurface drainage system in the Kuttanad region of Kerala has the following data: drain spacing $L = 40\ \text{m}$, hydraulic conductivity $K = 0.8\ \text{m/day}$, drainable porosity $\mu = 0.06$, equivalent depth $D = 4\ \text{m}$, and initial water-table height $H_0 = 1.5\ \text{m}$ above drain level. Calculate (i) the drainage factor $\alpha$, (ii) the time required for the water table to fall to 0.4 m, and (iii) the drain discharge per unit area at the end of 5 days.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

The linearised Boussinesq equation, assuming the Dupuit–Forchheimer approximation, is:

$$D \frac{\partial^{2} H}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{\mu}{K} \frac{\partial H}{\partial t}$$

Assume a separable solution $H(x,t) = X(x) T(t)$:

$$D X''(x) T(t) = \frac{\mu}{K} X(x) T'(t) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{X''}{X} = \frac{\mu}{K D}\frac{T'}{T} = -\lambda^{2}$$

The spatial ODE gives $X(x) = A\cos(\lambda x) + B\sin(\lambda x)$. With boundary conditions $H = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$:

$$X(0) = 0 \Rightarrow A = 0; \quad X(L) = 0 \Rightarrow \sin(\lambda L) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pi/L$$

The temporal ODE $T' + \alpha T = 0$ with $\alpha = \pi^{2} K D/(\mu L^{2})$ gives $T(t) = e^{-\alpha t}$.

The full lowest-mode solution: $H(x,t) = B \sin(\pi x/L) e^{-\alpha t}$.

Averaging over $[0, L]$:

$$\bar{H}(t) = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L B\sin\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right)e^{-\alpha t}dx = \frac{2B}{\pi} e^{-\alpha t}$$

Let $H_0 = 2B/\pi$ be the initial mean height. The Glover–Dum equation is:

$$\boxed{\;\bar{H}(t) = H_0 e^{-\alpha t}\;}$$

**Model Answer for (b):**

**(i) Drainage factor:**

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi^{2} K D}{\mu L^{2}} = \frac{9.8696 \times 0.8 \times 4}{0.06 \times 1600} = \frac{31.583}{96} = 0.329\ \text{day}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Time to fall to $H = 0.4$ m:**

$$0.4 = 1.5 \, e^{-0.329 t} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{-0.329 t} = 0.2667$$

$$-0.329\, t = \ln(0.2667) = -1.3218 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t = \frac{1.3218}{0.329} = 4.02\ \text{days}$$

**(iii) Drain discharge at $t = 5$ days:**

$$q(5) = \alpha \mu H_0 e^{-\alpha \times 5} = 0.329 \times 0.06 \times 1.5 \times e^{-0.329 \times 5}$$

$$q(5) = 0.0296 \times e^{-1.645} = 0.0296 \times 0.1930 = 0.00571\ \text{m/day} = 5.71\ \text{mm/day}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of the Kraijenhoff van de Leur mass-land equation. Derive its form for (i) constant recharge and (ii) intermittent recharge cases.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A farmland receives a uniform rainfall of intensity 30 mm/day for 3 days. The initial water-table height is $H_0 = 0.5$ m. Given: $K = 1.0$ m/day, $\mu = 0.05$, $L = 30$ m, $D = 6$ m. Compute (i) the drainage factor, (ii) the peak water-table height, and (iii) the time (after rainfall ends) for the water table to drop to 0.6 m.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

The Kraijenhoff van de Leur equation is a **lumped-parameter (0-D) mass-balance model** of a tract of land bounded by two parallel drains. It tracks the mean water-table height $\bar{H}$ using a single ordinary differential equation:

$$\mu \frac{d\bar{H}}{dt} = R - \alpha\mu\bar{H}$$

**Case (i) — Constant Recharge $R$:** The ODE integrates to:

$$\bar{H}(t) = \frac{R}{\alpha\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\alpha t}\right) + H_0 e^{-\alpha t}$$

As $t \to \infty$, the steady-state is $\bar{H}_\infty = R/(\alpha\mu)$.

**Case (ii) — Intermittent Recharge:** In two phases:
- **Recharge on** ($0 \le t \le t_p$): use the constant-$R$ form, reaching peak $\bar{H}_{peak} = \frac{R}{\alpha\mu}(1 - e^{-\alpha t_p}) + H_0 e^{-\alpha t_p}$.
- **Recharge off** ($t > t_p$): pure decay, $\bar{H}(t) = \bar{H}_{peak} e^{-\alpha(t - t_p)}$.

**Model Answer for (b):**

**(i) Drainage factor:**

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi^{2} K D}{\mu L^{2}} = \frac{9.8696 \times 1.0 \times 6}{0.05 \times 900} = \frac{59.218}{45} = 1.316\ \text{day}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Peak water-table height:**

$$R = 30\ \text{mm/day} = 0.030\ \text{m/day}, \quad t_p = 3\ \text{days}$$

Compute $e^{-\alpha t_p} = e^{-1.316 \times 3} = e^{-3.948} = 0.01930$

$$\frac{R}{\alpha\mu} = \frac{0.030}{1.316 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.030}{0.0658} = 0.456\ \text{m}$$

$$\bar{H}_{peak} = 0.456(1 - 0.01930) + 0.5 \times 0.01930 = 0.456 \times 0.9807 + 0.00965 = 0.447 + 0.00965 = 0.457\ \text{m}$$

**(iii) Time after rainfall to drop to 0.6 m? — Sanity check:** $0.6\ \text{m} > \bar{H}_{peak} = 0.457\ \text{m}$, so the water table never rises to 0.6 m under these conditions. **The water table is already below 0.6 m throughout.** If the question intends 0.06 m (a likely typo or unit interpretation), then:

$$0.06 = 0.457 \, e^{-1.316 \Delta t} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{-1.316\Delta t} = 0.1313$$

$$\Delta t = \frac{-\ln(0.1313)}{1.316} = \frac{2.029}{1.316} = 1.54\ \text{days after rainfall ends}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit error on $R$:** Rainfall given in mm/day must be divided by 1000 to convert to m/day. Marks lost here are unrecoverable.
> 2. **Confusing $H_{peak}$ with $H_\infty$:** $H_{peak}$ is the head at end of recharge; $H_\infty$ is the long-term limit. For $t_p \ll 1/\alpha$, $H_{peak} \approx H_0 + R t_p/\mu$ (small).
> 3. **Using $H_0$ instead of $H_{peak}$ as the starting point of recession:** In Phase 2, the decay is from $H_{peak}$, not from $H_0$.
> 4. **Mixing the Glover–Dum and Kraijenhoff equations:** Glover–Dum has $R = 0$; adding $R$ to it gives the Kraijenhoff form.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert $P$ in mm to m** when using the instantaneous-rise formula $\bar{H}_{peak} = H_0 + P/\mu$.
> 6. **Interpreting $t$ in the recession equation as absolute time instead of $(t - t_p)$.** This is the single most common mistake in intermittent-recharge problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Glover–Dum** = falling water table, **NO recharge**, exponential decay $\bar{H}(t) = H_0 e^{-\alpha t}$.
- **Kraijenhoff v.d. Leur** = fluctuating water table, **WITH recharge**, two cases (constant and intermittent).
- **Drainage factor:** $\alpha = \pi^{2} K D / (\mu L^{2})$ — controls the speed of water-table response.
- **Time constant of the system:** $\tau = 1/\alpha$ days. After $5\tau$, the system is essentially at equilibrium.
- **Steady-state head with constant recharge:** $\bar{H}_\infty = R/(\alpha\mu)$ — design drain depth so this is below the root zone.
- **Peak head with intermittent recharge:** $\bar{H}_{peak} = \frac{R}{\alpha\mu}(1 - e^{-\alpha t_p}) + H_0 e^{-\alpha t_p}$.
- **Post-recharge decay:** $\bar{H}(t) = \bar{H}_{peak} e^{-\alpha(t-t_p)}$ for $t > t_p$.
- **Cumulative volume drained (Glover–Dum):** $V(t) = \mu H_0 (1 - e^{-\alpha t})$.
- **Instantaneous-recharge shortcut:** $H_{peak} \approx H_0 + P/\mu$ for quick design checks.
- **Unit discipline:** $K$ in m/day, $D, L$ in m, $\mu$ dimensionless, $R$ in m/day, $P$ in m, $\alpha$ in day⁻¹, $t$ in days.
- **Calibration tip:** Plot $\ln(\bar{H}/H_0)$ vs $t$ from field observations; the slope gives $-\alpha$ directly.
- **Engineering mantra:** *“Glover–Dum tells you how fast the water table falls; Kraijenhoff tells you how high it rises.”*
- **KTU favourite trap:** Mixing the two equations or applying Glover–Dum where recharge is non-zero.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout of Open Drainage Systems: Types - Field Drains, Design Considerations of Ditch Drains, Mole Drains - Design Considerations and Suitability
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET632 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Layout of Open Drainage Systems: Types - Field Drains, Design Considerations of Ditch Drains, Mole Drains - Design Considerations and Suitability

# 🌊 Module 4 — Open Drainage Systems: Layout, Field Drains, Ditch Drains & Mole Drains

---

## 📘 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Open Drainage System — Formal Definition

An **Open Drainage System** is a network of unlined or lined open channels (ditches) constructed at the ground surface or at shallow depths, designed to collect, convey, and dispose of excess surface water and/or shallow groundwater from agricultural fields, roads, and rural areas by gravity flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET632, Module 4):**
> *"Open drainage systems consist of excavated channels of varying cross-section (trapezoidal, parabolic, V-shaped) used to remove excess water. The layout comprises field drains, submains, and main drains arranged in patterns such as parallel, herringbone, or natural systems."*

### 1.2 Classification of Open Drains

| Class | Function | Typical Depth | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Field Drains** (Ditch Drains) | Collect water from individual fields | **0.3 – 0.8 m** | Local surface/sub-surface removal |
| **Sub-main Drains** | Convey water from field drains | **0.8 – 1.5 m** | Collector function |
| **Main Drains** (Outfall Drains) | Discharge to natural watercourse | **1.5 – 3.0 m** | Final disposal |

### 1.3 Layout Patterns of Open Drainage Systems

**(a) Parallel / Gridiron Pattern**
- Field drains run parallel to each other, perpendicular to the submain.
- Used in **regular, uniformly-sloping** lands.

**(b) Herringbone Pattern**
- Laterals join the main/sub-main at **oblique angles** (like a fish's bones).
- Used when the **main slope is not uniform**.

**(c) Natural Pattern**
- Drains follow **natural depressions** and watercourses.
- Economical; suited to **irregular topography**.

**(d) Random / Dendritic Pattern**
- Drains are placed **only in problem spots** (wet patches).
- Used for **patch-drainage** on undulating land.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy 🪈**
> Think of an open drainage system as the **veins of a leaf**. The small capillaries (field drains) collect water, the midrib (sub-main) gathers it, and the petiole (main drain) finally discharges the water away. Just as veins must be properly oriented (parallel/branching) to function, the layout pattern is critical for efficient drainage.

### 1.4 Field Drains — Definition

**Field Drains** are the **smallest, most numerous channels** in the open drainage hierarchy. They directly receive water from the agricultural field and feed it to higher-order drains.

- **Spacing:** Generally **20 m to 100 m** depending on soil and slope.
- **Cross-section:** V-shaped or shallow trapezoidal.
- **Grade:** Minimum **0.1% to 0.5%** for self-cleaning velocity.

> [!TIP]
> **Why study field drains?** They are the *first line of defense* against waterlogging. A poorly designed field drain (insufficient slope, wrong shape, weed-choked) makes the entire system fail, even if the main drains are perfect.

### 1.5 Ditch Drains (Open Ditches)

A **Ditch Drain** is a shallow, excavated open channel with side slopes, used to:
1. Lower the water table in the root zone.
2. Intercept and convey surface runoff.
3. Provide **outlet** for subsurface drains (tile drains, mole drains).

**Standard Geometry of Ditch Drains:**

| Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Depth ($d$) | **0.6 – 1.2 m** | Shallow field ditches |
| Side slope ($z : 1$, H : V) | **1.5 : 1 to 2 : 1** | Depends on soil stability |
| Bottom width ($b$) | **0.3 – 0.6 m** | For maintenance access |
| Longitudinal slope ($S$) | **0.0005 to 0.005** | Non-silting & non-scouring |
| Freeboard | **0.15 – 0.30 m** | Above design HWL |

### 1.6 Mole Drains — Definition

A **Mole Drain** is a cylindrical, unlined subsurface channel formed by drawing a **mole plough** (a torpedo-shaped shank with a cylindrical foot) through heavy clay soil at depth, creating a continuous tunnel that acts as a drainage conduit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Ditch Drain** = open channel, visible at surface.
> - **Mole Drain** = closed cylindrical channel, **invisible** (formed in clay subsoil without any pipe material).

### 1.7 Conceptual Analogy for Mole Drains 🐾

Imagine pushing a **hot iron rod** through a slab of soft clay. The rod leaves behind a smooth, stable tunnel. Water seeps through the surrounding clay into the tunnel and flows along it. This is precisely how a **mole drain** works — the **mole foot** compresses the clay walls, making them relatively impermeable, and the resulting tunnel acts as the drainage pipe.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Considerations for Ditch Drains

The design of ditch drains requires careful attention to:

### (A) Hydraulic Design (Manning's Equation)

The most fundamental equation governing open channel flow in ditch drains is **Manning's Equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = discharge (m³/s)
- $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient (**0.025 – 0.040** for earth ditches)
- $A$ = cross-sectional flow area (m²)
- $R$ = hydraulic radius = $A / P$ (m)
- $P$ = wetted perimeter (m)
- $S$ = longitudinal slope of the ditch (m/m)

### (B) Velocity Constraints — Non-Silting & Non-Scouring

To prevent sediment deposition (**silting**) and channel erosion (**scouring**), the velocity must lie between:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{min} \leq V \leq V_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Condition | Typical Value | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimum (non-silting) velocity** | **0.6 – 0.9 m/s** | Carries fine sediment |
| **Maximum (non-scouring) velocity** | **0.9 – 1.5 m/s** (sandy soil) | Higher for clay (up to **1.8 m/s**) |

### (C) Cross-Section Geometry

**Trapezoidal ditch (most common in KTU curriculum):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (b + z \cdot d) \cdot d \\
P &= b + 2d\sqrt{1 + z^2} \\
T &= b + 2zd \quad \text{(Top width)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $b$ = bottom width
- $d$ = depth of flow
- $z$ = side slope (horizontal per unit vertical)
- $T$ = top water surface width

### (D) Depth & Spacing

Ditch depth governs how far the water table is lowered. The **drainage equation** (steady-state, Hooghoudt's form for ditches) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{8 K \cdot d_e \cdot m + 4 K \cdot m^2}{L^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $q$ = drainage coefficient (m/day)
- $K$ = hydraulic conductivity (m/day)
- $d_e$ = equivalent depth (Hooghoudt's correction)
- $m$ = height of water table above drain level at midpoint
- $L$ = drain spacing (m)

### (E) Freeboard, Lining & Maintenance

- **Freeboard** ≥ 0.15 m above maximum design water level.
- **Lining options:** grass turf, stone pitching, brick, or concrete for stability.
- **Maintenance access:** minimum bottom width 0.3 m for manual cleaning.

---

## 2.2 Design Considerations for Mole Drains

### (A) Soil Suitability

The most critical design parameter. Mole drains are viable **only** when:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soil Criterion for Mole Drains:**
> **Clay content ≥ 30–35%** by weight, and **plasticity index (PI) ≥ 10**.
> Sandy or organic (peat) soils **collapse** — the mole tunnel closes within days.

### (B) Mole Plow Specifications

| Parameter | Typical Range | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Mole diameter** | **50 – 100 mm** | Larger = longer life but harder pull |
| **Depth of draw** | **0.4 – 0.7 m** | Below plough pan, in stable clay |
| **Leg/shank length** | Match depth | Vertical alignment |
| **Drawbar pull** | **30 – 80 kN** | Tractor/track power needed |

### (C) Spacing of Mole Drains

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= C \cdot \sqrt{\frac{K \cdot d}{q}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $L$ = mole drain spacing (m)
- $K$ = horizontal hydraulic conductivity (m/day)
- $d$ = depth of mole drain below ground surface (m)
- $q$ = design drainage rate (m/day)
- $C$ = empirical constant (**3 – 5** for heavy clay)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU Design Values:**
> - Mole spacing: **2 – 5 m**
> - Mole depth: **0.45 – 0.60 m**
> - Diameter: **75 mm** is most common

### (D) Longitudinal Grade

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{mole} &\geq 0.002 \text{ (0.2\% minimum)} \\
S_{mole, max} &\leq 0.05 \text{ (5\% to prevent erosion of wall)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (E) Life Span

Mole drains are **temporary** — the tunnel gradually closes due to swelling, biological activity, and consolidation.

| Soil Type | Expected Life |
|---|---|
| Heavy clay (PI > 25) | **5 – 10 years** |
| Medium clay (PI = 15–25) | **3 – 5 years** |
| Silty clay (PI < 15) | **< 2 years** (often not viable) |

### (F) Stability Check (Critical for KTU)

The **mole channel stability** depends on the soil's shear strength. A simplified check uses the **Terzaghi/Kezdi stability criterion**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_v}{c_u} &\geq 3 \quad \text{(stable mole channel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\sigma_v$ = overburden pressure at mole depth = $\gamma \cdot d$
- $c_u$ = undrained shear strength of the clay at mole depth (kPa)
- $\gamma$ = unit weight of soil (**18 – 20 kN/m³**)

---

## 2.3 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Application | Unit Convention |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \tfrac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Ditch flow capacity | m³/s, SI |
| 2 | $V = \tfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Ditch velocity check | m/s |
| 3 | $A = (b + zd)d$ | Trapezoidal area | m² |
| 4 | $P = b + 2d\sqrt{1+z^2}$ | Wetted perimeter | m |
| 5 | $R = A/P$ | Hydraulic radius | m |
| 6 | $q = \tfrac{8K d_e m + 4 K m^2}{L^2}$ | Hooghoudt drain spacing | m/day |
| 7 | $L = C \sqrt{Kd/q}$ | Mole drain spacing | m |
| 8 | $S_{min} = (V_{min} \cdot n / R^{2/3})^2$ | Min. slope (non-silting) | m/m |
| 9 | $\sigma_v / c_u \geq 3$ | Mole stability criterion | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_{life} = f(\text{PI, clay content, climate})$ | Mole drain longevity | years |

> [!TIP]
> **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Manning's equation** is the single most-tested formula in open drainage design — always carry it.
> 2. **Hooghoudt's equation** tests your understanding of steady-state drainage theory.
> 3. **Mole stability criterion** is a favourite short-answer question (3-markers).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Agricultural productivity** | Proper drainage can **double crop yield** in waterlogged areas (FAO data). |
| **Flood mitigation** | Open ditches in paddy fields reduce peak runoff by **30 – 50%**. |
| **Salinity control** | Ditch drains leach salts from the root zone — critical in **Kuttanad, Kerala**. |
| **Low-cost land reclamation** | Mole drains in heavy clay are **5 – 10× cheaper** than tile drains. |
| **Land drainage in coastal Kerala** | Open ditches are the predominant system in **Kole lands, Pokkali fields**. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Manning's Equation in the Context of Ditch Design

### Starting Point — Chezy's Formula

The Chezy formula (1775) states that mean velocity is proportional to the square root of the energy slope:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= C \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot S}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $C$ is Chezy's coefficient (m^0.5/s).

### Manning's Modification (1889)

Empirically, **Robert Manning** found that $C$ depends on $R$ and roughness $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{R^{1/6}}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Substituting into Chezy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{R^{1/6}}{n} \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot S} = \frac{R^{1/6}}{n} \cdot R^{1/2} \cdot S^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Combining the exponents:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot R^{\tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{2}} \cdot S^{1/2} = \frac{1}{n} \cdot R^{\tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{3}{6}} \cdot S^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final form (multiplying by area):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A \cdot V = \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the form universally used in KTU open-channel problems.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Design of a Trapezoidal Field Ditch

**Given (KTU-style problem):**
- Required discharge: $Q = 0.5$ m³/s
- Longitudinal slope: $S = 0.001$ (1 in 1000)
- Manning's $n = 0.030$ (earthen, unlined)
- Side slope: $z = 1.5 : 1$ (H : V)
- Bottom width: $b = 0.5$ m

**Step 1: Express $A$ and $P$ in terms of depth $d$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (b + z d) d = (0.5 + 1.5 d) d = 0.5d + 1.5d^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= b + 2d\sqrt{1 + z^2} = 0.5 + 2d\sqrt{1 + 2.25} = 0.5 + 2d\sqrt{3.25}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{3.25} &\approx 1.803 \\
P &\approx 0.5 + 3.606 \, d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into Manning's equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.5 &= \frac{1}{0.030} \cdot (0.5d + 1.5d^2) \cdot \left( \frac{0.5d + 1.5d^2}{0.5 + 3.606d} \right)^{2/3} \cdot (0.001)^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Simplify the constant term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.001)^{1/2} &= 0.03162 \\
\frac{1}{0.030} \cdot 0.03162 &= 1.054
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.5 &= 1.054 \cdot (0.5d + 1.5d^2) \cdot \left( \frac{0.5d + 1.5d^2}{0.5 + 3.606d} \right)^{2/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Solve by trial (try $d = 0.6$ m):**

- $A = 0.5(0.6) + 1.5(0.6)^2 = 0.30 + 0.54 = 0.84$ m²
- $P = 0.5 + 3.606(0.6) = 0.5 + 2.164 = 2.664$ m
- $R = 0.84 / 2.664 = 0.3154$ m
- $R^{2/3} = 0.3154^{0.6667} \approx 0.463$
- $A \cdot R^{2/3} = 0.84 \times 0.463 = 0.389$
- $Q = 1.054 \times 0.389 \times 1.0 = 0.410$ m³/s ❌ (too low)

**Step 5: Try $d = 0.7$ m:**

- $A = 0.5(0.7) + 1.5(0.7)^2 = 0.35 + 0.735 = 1.085$ m²
- $P = 0.5 + 3.606(0.7) = 0.5 + 2.524 = 3.024$ m
- $R = 1.085 / 3.024 = 0.3588$ m
- $R^{2/3} \approx 0.504$
- $A \cdot R^{2/3} = 1.085 \times 0.504 = 0.547$
- $Q = 1.054 \times 0.547 = 0.577$ m³/s ✅

**Step 6: Interpolate for $Q = 0.5$ m³/s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &\approx 0.6 + 0.1 \cdot \frac{0.5 - 0.41}{0.577 - 0.41} = 0.6 + 0.1 \cdot \frac{0.09}{0.167}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &\approx 0.6 + 0.1 \cdot 0.539 \approx 0.654 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Add freeboard (0.2 m):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{total} &= 0.654 + 0.20 = 0.854 \text{ m} \approx 0.85 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Final design:** Ditch depth = **0.85 m**, bottom width = 0.5 m, side slope 1.5:1.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Mole Drain Spacing

**Given:**
- Hydraulic conductivity $K = 0.10$ m/day
- Mole depth $d = 0.55$ m
- Design drainage rate $q = 0.005$ m/day (5 mm/day)
- Empirical constant $C = 4$

**Apply the spacing formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= C \cdot \sqrt{\frac{K \cdot d}{q}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K \cdot d &= 0.10 \times 0.55 = 0.055 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Kd}{q} &= \frac{0.055}{0.005} = 11.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Square root:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{11.0} &= 3.317
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by $C$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 4 \times 3.317 = 13.27 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Result:** Mole drain spacing ≈ **13 m** (i.e., mole channels every 13 m, perpendicular to the slope).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Mole Stability Check

**Given:**
- Mole depth $d = 0.55$ m
- Unit weight of clay $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³
- Undrained shear strength $c_u = 4.5$ kPa

**Step 1: Compute overburden pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_v &= \gamma \cdot d = 19 \times 0.55 = 10.45 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute stability ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_v}{c_u} &= \frac{10.45}{4.5} = 2.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with threshold (≥ 3):**

Since **2.32 < 3**, the mole channel is **likely to collapse** in this soil.

> ⚠️ **Engineering judgment:** Either increase depth (more $\sigma_v$) or apply only in stiffer clay zones.

---

## 3.5 📋 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ditch vs. Mole Drains

| Criterion | Ditch Drain | Mole Drain |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Open channel | Subsurface tunnel |
| **Soil suitability** | Most soils | Heavy clay (PI ≥ 10) |
| **Depth** | 0.6 – 1.5 m | 0.4 – 0.7 m |
| **Spacing** | 20 – 100 m | 2 – 5 m (sometimes up to 13 m per Kezdi) |
| **Construction cost** | Moderate (excavation) | Low (mole plough) |
| **Land loss** | 5 – 10% area lost to ditches | Negligible |
| **Maintenance** | Annual desilting | Re-draw every 3 – 10 yrs |
| **Life span** | 20+ years | 3 – 10 years |
| **Hydraulic capacity** | High (open section) | Low (~10–20 mm/day) |
| **Stability criterion** | Side slope vs. soil $c$ | $\sigma_v / c_u \geq 3$ |
| **KTU exam frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

---

# 🎯 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between ditch drains and mole drains. Mention two situations where mole drainage is preferred over open ditch drainage.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

| Aspect | Ditch Drain | Mole Drain |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Open channel, visible | Subsurface tunnel, invisible |
| Soil | Any soil | Heavy clay (PI > 10) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Life | 20+ years | 3 – 10 years |
| Land loss | Yes (5–10% area) | No |

**Mole drain preferred when:**
1. Land is **flat with heavy clay** subsoil, where deep excavation is uneconomical.
2. The farmer wants to **retain full land area** for cultivation (no open ditches).

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State Manning's formula for open channel flow. List the recommended range of Manning's 'n' for (i) clean earth ditch and (ii) stone-pitched ditch.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = discharge (m³/s)
- $A$ = flow area, $R$ = hydraulic radius, $S$ = bed slope
- $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient

**Manning's n values:**
- Clean earth ditch (straight, uniform, no weeds): **$n = 0.022 – 0.025$**
- Earth ditch with weeds & stones: **$n = 0.025 – 0.040$**
- Stone-pitched / lined ditch: **$n = 0.020 – 0.025$**

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Q.A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Design a trapezoidal field ditch to carry a discharge of **0.8 m³/s**. Take longitudinal slope $S = 0.0015$, side slope $z = 1 : 1$, bottom width $b = 0.6$ m, and Manning's $n = 0.025$. Check whether the velocity is within the non-scouring and non-silting limits. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **mole drainage system** with a neat sketch. List the **suitability criteria** and **design parameters** (depth, spacing, diameter) used in KTU/MoWR guidelines. **[7 marks]**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1: Express A and P:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (b + zd)d = (0.6 + d) d = 0.6d + d^2 \\
P &= b + 2d\sqrt{1+z^2} = 0.6 + 2d\sqrt{2} = 0.6 + 2.828d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute in Manning's equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.8 &= \frac{1}{0.025} \cdot (0.6d + d^2) \cdot \left( \frac{0.6d + d^2}{0.6 + 2.828d} \right)^{2/3} \cdot (0.0015)^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Simplify constants:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{0.025} \cdot (0.0015)^{1/2} &= 40 \cdot 0.03873 = 1.549
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Trial $d = 0.7$ m:**

- $A = 0.6(0.7) + (0.7)^2 = 0.42 + 0.49 = 0.91$ m²
- $P = 0.6 + 2.828(0.7) = 0.6 + 1.980 = 2.580$ m
- $R = 0.91 / 2.580 = 0.3527$ m
- $R^{2/3} = 0.3527^{0.6667} \approx 0.498$
- $Q = 1.549 \times 0.91 \times 0.498 = 0.702$ m³/s ❌ (too low)

**Step 5: Trial $d = 0.8$ m:**

- $A = 0.6(0.8) + (0.8)^2 = 0.48 + 0.64 = 1.12$ m²
- $P = 0.6 + 2.828(0.8) = 0.6 + 2.262 = 2.862$ m
- $R = 1.12 / 2.862 = 0.3914$ m
- $R^{2/3} \approx 0.535$
- $Q = 1.549 \times 1.12 \times 0.535 = 0.928$ m³/s ✅ (slightly high)

**Step 6: Interpolate for $Q = 0.8$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &\approx 0.7 + 0.1 \cdot \frac{0.8 - 0.702}{0.928 - 0.702} = 0.7 + 0.1 \cdot \frac{0.098}{0.226} = 0.7 + 0.0434 \approx 0.74 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= Q/A = 0.8 / (0.6(0.74) + 0.74^2) = 0.8 / (0.444 + 0.548) = 0.8 / 0.992 = 0.806 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Check limits:**
- $V = 0.81$ m/s — within **0.6 – 0.9 m/s** range. ✅
- $V_{max}$ for sandy soil = 0.9 m/s; for clay = 1.5 m/s. Within limits.

**Step 9: Add freeboard = 0.20 m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{design} = 0.74 + 0.20 = 0.94 \text{ m} \approx 0.95 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Final Design:** $d = 0.95$ m, $b = 0.6$ m, $z = 1:1$.

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Mole Drainage System — Explanation:**

A **mole drain** is a cylindrical subsurface channel (typically 50–100 mm diameter) formed in heavy clay soil by drawing a **mole plough** (tractor-mounted) at depths of **0.40 – 0.70 m** below the surface. The torpedo-shaped foot of the plough compresses the surrounding clay, creating a stable, near-impermeable wall.

**Sketch (block schematic — refer Section 4.3 above).**

**Suitability Criteria:**

| # | Criterion | Required Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clay content | **≥ 30–35%** by weight |
| 2 | Plasticity Index (PI) | **≥ 10** (preferably 15–25) |
| 3 | Soil structure | Homogeneous, stable clay |
| 4 | Topography | Flat to gently undulating |
| 5 | Organic/peat soils | **NOT suitable** (walls collapse) |

**Design Parameters (MoWR / KTU):**

| Parameter | Recommended Value |
|---|---|
| **Mole diameter** | **75 mm** (most common) |
| **Depth of mole** | **0.45 – 0.60 m** (below plough pan) |
| **Spacing** | **2 – 5 m** (heavy clay); up to **10 – 15 m** for Kezdi's formula |
| **Longitudinal slope** | **0.2% to 5%** |
| **Life** | **3 – 10 years** |
| **Outlet** | Connects to a lined **ditch drain** or manhole |

**Stability Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_v}{c_u} = \frac{\gamma \cdot d}{c_u} \geq 3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 📝 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

### **Q.B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Discuss the **layout patterns** of open drainage systems with neat sketches. Compare parallel (gridiron) and herringbone systems in terms of land suitability and cost. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A field of heavy clay soil is to be drained using mole drains. Given $K = 0.08$ m/day, mole depth $d = 0.50$ m, design drainage rate $q = 0.004$ m/day, and the Kezdi constant $C = 4.5$. Compute (i) the required mole spacing, and (ii) the undrained shear strength $c_u$ required to ensure stable mole channels at the given depth (assume $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³). **[7 marks]**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Four major layout patterns:**

**1. Parallel / Gridiron Pattern**
- Field drains run **parallel** to each other.
- Each lateral meets the submain at **90°**.
- Suitable for **uniform, rectangular fields** with **gentle, regular slope**.

**2. Herringbone Pattern**
- Laterals enter the submain at **oblique angles** (e.g., 45°).
- Used where the **main slope direction is irregular** and laterals must follow contour.

**3. Natural Pattern**
- Drains follow **natural depressions and streams**.
- Economical; suited to **undulating terrain**.

**4. Random / Dendritic Pattern**
- Drains placed only at **isolated wet spots**.
- Lowest cost for **patch drainage**; minimum disturbance.

**Comparison: Parallel vs. Herringbone**

| Feature | Parallel (Gridiron) | Herringbone |
|---|---|---|
| Land shape | Rectangular, regular | Triangular, irregular slopes |
| Layout angle to submain | 90° | 30° – 60° |
| Cost | Lower (uniform) | Slightly higher |
| Land utilization | Excellent | Slightly less efficient |
| Suitability | Uniform slope, broad fields | Variable slope direction |

> **KTU Tip:** If the field has a **uniform gentle slope**, **gridiron is preferred** for economy.

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Part (i) — Mole spacing using Kezdi's formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= C \cdot \sqrt{\frac{K \cdot d}{q}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Compute $K \cdot d$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K \cdot d &= 0.08 \times 0.50 = 0.040 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $K \cdot d / q$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Kd}{q} &= \frac{0.040}{0.004} = 10.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Square root:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{10.0} &= 3.162
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by $C$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 4.5 \times 3.162 = 14.23 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Mole spacing $L \approx 14.2$ m** (every 14 m perpendicular to slope).

**Part (ii) — Required $c_u$ for stable mole:**

Stability criterion: $\sigma_v / c_u \geq 3$, i.e.:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_u &\leq \frac{\sigma_v}{3} = \frac{\gamma \cdot d}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Compute $\sigma_v$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_v = 19 \times 0.50 = 9.5 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $c_u$ (upper limit to ensure stability):**

Wait — re-evaluating the criterion. The condition $\sigma_v / c_u \geq 3$ means:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_u \leq \frac{\sigma_v}{3} = \frac{9.5}{3} = 3.17 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hmm — this is the **upper limit** on $c_u$. If $c_u$ is too high, the soil is **too stiff** and the mole plough cannot form a stable channel without fracturing the clay. Soil that is too soft ($c_u$ very low) is also bad.

**The valid stability range is typically:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 \leq \frac{\sigma_v}{c_u} \leq 5
\end{aligned}
$$

For $d = 0.5$ m and $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_v &= 9.5 \text{ kPa} \\
\frac{\sigma_v}{3} = 3.17 \text{ kPa} \quad &;& \quad \frac{\sigma_v}{5} = 1.9 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **safe $c_u$ range** is approximately **1.9 – 3.2 kPa**.

> ✅ **Result:** Required undrained shear strength for stable mole formation = **$c_u \approx 1.9$ to $3.2$ kPa** at mole depth.

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Exam):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in Manning's equation** — $Q$ must be in m³/s, $A$ in m², $R$ in m, $S$ in m/m. Mixing units (e.g., cm with m) costs full marks.
> 2. **Not adding freeboard** in ditch design — examiner specifically deducts 1 mark for missing freeboard (0.15 – 0.30 m).
> 3. **Skipping the velocity check** — Always verify $V$ lies between $V_{min}$ and $V_{max}$. Skipping this = -1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing ditch spacing with mole spacing** — Ditch spacing is **20–100 m**; mole spacing is **2–15 m**. Mixing these is a classic error.
> 5. **Mole stability inverted** — Many students write $c_u / \sigma_v \geq 3$ instead of $\sigma_v / c_u \geq 3$. Examiner catches this!
> 6. **In Part B (14 marks), sub-parts MUST be answered separately** — failing to label (a) and (b) clearly leads to 0.5 mark deduction.
> 7. **For Hooghoudt's equation, students often forget the $4Km^2$ term** — use the complete form for steady-state, parallel drains.

---

## 5.5 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Open drainage** = gravity-driven, open-channel network; visible at surface.
- [x] **Layout patterns:** Parallel, Herringbone, Natural, Random — choose based on topography and slope.
- [x] **Field drains** are the smallest, shallowest (0.3–0.8 m) and most numerous channels.
- [x] **Ditch drains** = trapezoidal or V-shaped; designed using **Manning's equation**.
- [x] **Non-silting velocity** ≥ **0.6 m/s**; **Non-scouring velocity** ≤ **0.9–1.5 m/s** (depending on soil).
- [x] **Freeboard** of **0.15–0.30 m** must be added above the design water level.
- [x] **Mole drains** = cylindrical tunnels in **heavy clay** formed by a **mole plough** — no pipe material used.
- [x] **Soil criterion for mole:** clay content **≥ 30–35%**; plasticity index **≥ 10**.
- [x] **Mole design parameters:** diameter 50–100 mm, depth 0.4–0.7 m, spacing 2–15 m, slope 0.2–5%.
- [x] **Mole life:** 3–10 years (re-draw periodically).
- [x] **Stability check:** $\sigma_v / c_u \geq 3$ (overburden vs. undrained shear strength).
- [x] **Mole spacing (Kezdi):** $L = C \sqrt{Kd/q}$ where $C = 3$–$5$.
- [x] **Ditch spacing (Hooghoudt):** $q = (8Kd_e m + 4Km^2)/L^2$ (steady-state, parallel).
- [x] **Manning's n values:** clean earth = 0.022; weed-choked = 0.030–0.040; lined = 0.013–0.018.
- [x] **Kerala context:** Open ditches dominate in **Kole lands of Thrissur** and **Pokkali fields of Ernakulam/Alappuzha**.
- [x] **Common KTU exam trap:** confuse *drain spacing* with *drain depth* — both are 2-decision variables, but are not interchangeable.
- [x] **Mole drains need an outlet** — they discharge to a **lined ditch drain** at the field boundary.

---

topics.title: Sub-surface Drainage Systems: Pipe Drainage Systems Design for Uniform and Non-uniform Flow Conditions, Transport, and Dewatering Situations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET632 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sub-surface Drainage Systems: Pipe Drainage Systems Design for Uniform and Non-uniform Flow Conditions, Transport, and Dewatering Situations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sub-Surface Pipe Drainage System — Formal Definition

A **Sub-surface Pipe Drainage System** (commonly called a **Tile Drainage System** or **Underground Drainage System**) is an engineered network of permeable conduits installed below the ground surface at a designed depth and gradient to lower the water table, remove excess gravitational water from the crop root zone, and convey it safely to an outlet. The primary function is the **control of the depth of the water table** to prevent waterlogging and secondary salinization, while maintaining an **optimal aeration zone** for root respiration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET632 Module 4):**
> Sub-surface drainage comprises buried perforated or open-jointed pipes (laterals, submains, and mains) laid at a pre-determined grade that intercepts and removes surplus groundwater, satisfying both **agronomic (crop)** and **engineering (hydraulic)** criteria.

### Key Terminology for Board Exam Mastery

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Lateral** | Small-diameter pipe receiving inflow directly from the soil through perforations/joints |
| **Sub-main** | Collector pipe receiving flow from several laterals |
| **Main** | Outermost pipe conveying total discharge to the outlet |
| **Drainage Coefficient (q)** | Depth of water (mm/day) to be removed from the area in 24 hours |
| **Hydraulic Gradient (S)** | Slope of the water table midway between drains (also slope of pipe for self-cleaning) |
| **Spacing (L)** | Distance between two parallel laterals |
| **Depth (D)** | Vertical distance from ground surface to the centre line of the lateral at the midpoint |
| **Permeability (K)** | Hydraulic conductivity of the soil (m/day) |
| **Drainable Porosity (μ)** | Volume of water drained per unit volume of soil (dimensionless) |
| **Equivalent Depth (d_e)** | Hooghoudt's effective depth used to correct for finite drain depth below impervious boundary |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Basement Flooding" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bathroom Mat Principle**
> Imagine your bathroom floor is flooded. You place a porous mat (the soil) over a slotted channel (the perforated pipe). Water seeps through the mat into the channel and flows out. Now imagine *many* such channels running parallel under a giant wet field — that is exactly a **sub-surface pipe drainage system**. The spacing between channels is the **drain spacing (L)**, the depth of the channel below the surface is the **drain depth (D)**, and the slope of the channel is the **hydraulic gradient (S)**. The soil's ability to push water sideways toward the channel is the **hydraulic conductivity (K)**.

### 1.3 Classification of Pipe Drainage Design Conditions

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly distinguishes between **two hydraulic regimes** that govern the design of the lateral pipes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification by Hydraulic Flow Regime**
> 1. **Steady-State (Equilibrium) / Uniform Flow Conditions:** The recharge rate equals the drainage discharge indefinitely. The water table shape is time-invariant. → **Hooghoodt, Kirkham, Schilfgaarde, Donnan** equations.
> 2. **Unsteady-State (Transient) / Non-Uniform Flow Conditions:** Recharge occurs intermittently (rainfall events); the water table rises and falls. → **Glover–Dumm, USBR, Van Schilfgaarde, Boussinesq** equations.
>
> **Operational Use Cases (KTU Module 4 explicit headings):**
> - **Transport Drains:** Long pipelines conveying water from agricultural fields, road subgrades, or urban catchments to a disposal point. They are **non-perforated** and sized for **steady uniform open-channel/pipe flow**.
> - **Dewatering Drains:** Temporary systems used during construction (foundations, basements, tunnels) to lower the groundwater table. They are sized for **pumping-induced unsteady flow** with a target drawdown time.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Kerala Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units & Constants Used in KTU Numerical Problems**
> - **K** (hydraulic conductivity) → **m/day** (typical Kerala laterite: 0.5 – 5 m/day; alluvial: 1 – 10 m/day; clay: < 0.1 m/day).
> - **Drainage coefficient (q)** → **mm/day** (KTU typical range 5–25 mm/day for humid tropical Kerala).
> - **Spacing (L)** → **m** (typical 10 m heavy clay → 50 m sandy loam).
> - **Pipe diameter (D_p)** → **mm** (laterals 50–100 mm; mains 100–300 mm).
> - **Darcy-Weisbach friction factor (f)** for smooth plastic pipes: **f ≈ 0.02** (Manning's n ≈ 0.011 for PVC/PE).
> - **Standard design velocity (V_design)** for self-cleaning in transport lines: **0.6 m/s ≤ V ≤ 1.5 m/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydraulic Problem — "Why" Drain Spacing Equations Exist

When a horizontal perforated pipe (lateral) is laid below the water table, water flows radially into it from all sides, causing the water table to deform into a **drawdown curve** between two adjacent drains. The shape of this curve is governed by:

- **Darcy's Law** for 2D flow in the saturated zone.
- **Continuity** between recharge (q) entering the water table and outflow through drains.
- **Boundary conditions** at the drain (water table reaches drain centre) and at the impermeable layer (no flow).

The solution to this 2D potential-flow problem yields **steady-state equations** (when inflow = outflow at all times) and **unsteady-state equations** (when the water table is falling between recharge events).

---

## 2.2 A. Design for Uniform (Steady-State) Flow Conditions

### 2.2.1 Hooghoudt's Equation — The Workhorse of KTU Problems

**Assumption:** Water table shape is an ellipse; the impermeable layer is at a finite depth below the drain. Hooghoudt corrected earlier infinite-depth solutions by introducing the **equivalent depth d_e**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hooghoudt's Steady-State Drain Spacing Equation:**
> $$L^2 = \frac{4K\left(d^2 + 2dd_e\right)}{q}$$
> where:
> - $L$ = drain spacing (m)
> - $K$ = hydraulic conductivity (m/day)
> - $q$ = drainage coefficient (m/day) — *convert mm/day to m/day by ÷1000*
> - $d$ = height of water table above drain centre at midpoint (m); also called **drawdown**
> - $d_e$ = Hooghoudt's equivalent depth (m), a function of drain radius $r$ and actual depth of impervious layer $D_i$ below drain centre.

**Equivalent depth (d_e) — Hooghoudt's empirical table (most common values for KTU exams):**

| $D_i$ / r | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 12 | 14 | 16 | 20 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d_e / r$ | 0.39 | 0.79 | 1.10 | 1.36 | 1.59 | 1.80 | 1.99 | 2.16 | 2.46 |

For $D_i \gg L/2$, $d_e \approx L / (8 \cdot [\ln(L/r) - 1)]$ — but in KTU exams the table value is normally given.

**Intuition — Why elliptical shape?** Darcy's law in 2D gives streamlines that bend around the drain; mathematically these are confocal ellipses with the drain as the focus. Hooghoudt fitted this geometry.

---

### 2.2.2 Kirkham's Equation (Infinite Impervious Layer)

For drains placed far above an impervious boundary (i.e., the boundary does not influence flow within the spacing), Kirkham's exact solution applies:

$$q = \frac{4\pi K (d + r - r_0) }{L \left[ \ln \coth\left(\dfrac{\pi L}{8d}\right)\right] }$$

where $r_0$ = effective drain radius (with envelope) and $r$ = drain radius.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Heuristic:** Use **Hooghoudt** when the impervious layer is *close* ($D_i < L/4$). Use **Kirkham** when impervious layer is *deep* and the problem says "infinite" or "semi-infinite" aquifer.

---

### 2.2.3 Donnan's Equation (Simplified for Circular Drains)

$$L^2 = \frac{4K d (d + 2D_p)}{q}$$

A simplified form used in some KTU problems where $D_p$ is the depth from drain to impervious boundary.

---

## 2.3 B. Design for Non-Uniform (Unsteady-State) Flow Conditions

When recharge is **intermittent** (e.g., a single storm event or irrigation cycle), the water table *rises* during the recharge period and *falls* between events. The **target** is to lower the water table from an initial height $h_0$ to a final height $h_f$ within a time $t$. This is the **Glover–Dumm equation** — the most-tested unsteady-state formula in KTU.

### 2.3.1 Glover–Dumm Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glover–Dumm (Unsteady-State) Drain Spacing Equation:**
> $$L^2 = \frac{\pi^2 K (d_0 - d_f) t}{\mu \, \ln\left(\dfrac{1.16 \, h_0}{h_f}\right)}$$
> where:
> - $d_0$ = initial water table height above drain centre (m)
> - $d_f$ = final water table height above drain centre (m)
> - $h_0$ = initial water table height above drain centre (m) [same as $d_0$]
> - $h_f$ = final water table height above drain centre (m)
> - $t$ = drainage time required (days)
> - $\mu$ = drainable porosity (specific yield), dimensionless
> - $K$ = hydraulic conductivity (m/day)
> - $L$ = drain spacing (m)

**Intuition:** During the *fall* of the water table, water stored in the soil drains out. The volume to be removed is the **shaded area** between initial and final water tables times $\mu$. Time required depends on how fast soil can push water sideways (controlled by K and L).

### 2.3.2 USBR (Bureau of Reclamation) Modified Form

For deeper drains or layered soils, USBR uses:

$$L^2 = \frac{4 K D_p \, t \, (h_0 - h_f)}{F \cdot \mu \cdot \ln(h_0 / h_f)}$$

where $F$ is a geometry factor (function of $D_p/L$ from tables) and $D_p$ is drain depth below initial water table.

### 2.3.3 Boundary Conditions for Both Regimes

| Boundary | Steady-State | Unsteady-State |
|---|---|---|
| At drain (x = 0, L) | $h = 0$ (water table at drain centre) | $h = 0$ (draining) |
| At impervious layer (z = $D_i$) | $\partial h/\partial z = 0$ (no flow) | $\partial h/\partial z = 0$ |
| At midline (x = L/2) | Symmetry: $\partial h/\partial x = 0$ | Symmetry: $\partial h/\partial x = 0$ |
| Time | $h$ independent of time | $h(x, t)$; initial $h = h_0$ |

---

## 2.4 C. Transport Drains — Hydraulic Design

Transport drains are **solid-wall (non-perforated)** pipelines carrying collected drainage water to the disposal point. They are designed as **open-channel or pressure-pipe flow** with self-cleansing velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manning's Equation for Pipe Flow (commonly used for KTU):**
> $$V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$
> $$Q = A \cdot V = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$
> where $R$ = hydraulic radius = $D_p/4$ for full pipe, $S$ = bed slope, $n$ = Manning's roughness.
>
> **Darcy–Weisbach alternative** (more rigorous):
> $$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2g D_p}$$
> $$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g D_p S}{f}}$$

**Self-Cleansing Condition (KTU design criterion):**

> V_min = **0.6 m/s** (to prevent silt deposition)
> V_max = **1.5 m/s** (to prevent pipe erosion at joints)

---

## 2.5 D. Dewatering — Design Philosophy

> [!NOTE]
> **Dewatering** is the temporary removal of groundwater to allow construction in dry conditions. Unlike agricultural drainage (which removes *excess* water slowly), dewatering must:
> 1. Lower the water table **below the excavation depth** with a safety margin.
> 2. Maintain the drawdown **continuously** during construction.
> 3. Be **temporary** — system is decommissioned post-construction.

**Common Methods (KTU syllabus):**

| Method | Mechanism | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Sump pumping** | Sump + pump in trench | Shallow excavations < 6 m |
| **Wellpoint (jet eductor)** | Series of small wells connected to a header pipe with vacuum pump | Depth 5–6 m; can be staged |
| **Deep wells + submersible pumps** | Large-diameter bored wells | Deep excavations > 10 m |
| **Horizontal drains (French drains)** | Trenches with gravel + perforated pipe | Slope stabilization |

**Dewatering Design Formulae:**

- **Dupuit–Forchheimer (steady drawdown to a well in confined/unconfined aquifer):**
  $$Q = \dfrac{\pi K (H^2 - h_w^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$
  where $H$ = initial saturated thickness, $h_w$ = water level in well, $R$ = radius of influence, $r_w$ = well radius.

- **Sichart's equation (radius of influence, empirical):**
  $$R = 3000 (H - h_w) \sqrt{K}$$
  with $K$ in m/s and depths in m, $R$ in m.

- **Equivalent drain spacing (using Hooghoudt's equation)** is the standard approach for **linear dewatering trenches**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **EXAM GOLD — Master these 7 equations; they cover 80% of KTU Module 4 questions:**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hooghoudt** | $L^2 = \dfrac{4K(d^2+2dd_e)}{q}$ | $L, K, d, d_e, q$ | Steady spacing, finite impervious layer |
| 2 | **Donnan** | $L^2 = \dfrac{4Kd(d+2D_p)}{q}$ | $L, K, d, D_p, q$ | Quick steady estimate |
| 3 | **Kirkham** | $q = \dfrac{4\pi K(d+r-r_0)}{L[\ln\coth(\pi L/8d)]}$ | $q, K, d, r, r_0, L$ | Steady spacing, deep impervious |
| 4 | **Glover–Dumm** | $L^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2 K (h_0-h_f)t}{\mu\ln(1.16 h_0/h_f)}$ | $L, K, h_0, h_f, t, \mu$ | Unsteady, time-to-drain |
| 5 | **Manning (pipe)** | $Q = \dfrac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | $Q, n, A, R, S$ | Transport drain sizing |
| 6 | **Dupuit drawdown** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi K (H^2 - h_w^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$ | $Q, K, H, h_w, R, r_w$ | Dewatering well |
| 7 | **Sichart (R)** | $R = 3000(H-h_w)\sqrt{K}$ | $R, H, h_w, K$ | Dewatering radius |

**Common Conversion Traps (KTU):**
- $q$ in mm/day → **÷ 1000** to get m/day.
- $K$ in cm/s → **× 864** to get m/day.
- Drainable porosity $\mu$ for sandy loam ≈ **0.10–0.15**; clay loam ≈ **0.05–0.08**.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agricultural Waterlogging Control:** The Indo-Gangetic plains, Kerala's Kuttanad region, and coastal polders all depend on Hooghoudt/Glover-Dumm design.
- **Highway & Railway Subgrade:** Transport drains along road edges keep the subgrade dry, preventing pavement failure.
- **Urban Stormwater:** Sub-surface transport drains convey runoff from storm sewers to natural drains.
- **Metro & Building Foundations (Kerala's Cochin Metro, KSRTC depots):** Dewatering systems using wellpoints are critical for below-water-table construction.
